behavior: A peaceful, nervous schooling fish of the middle to upper water column that should be kept in groups of five or more; an avid plant-eater that will graze soft vegetation down to the stems.
reproduction: A group spawner that scatters eggs which drop to the bottom; the parents are not strong egg-eaters but are best removed after spawning.
dimorphism: The male's anal fin is more elongated and colourful than the female's.

description: A large, disc-shaped serrasalmid reaching 40 cm, identifiable by a distinctive black vertical bar on its flank. Despite its piranha family membership, it is omnivorous rather than predatory, though it will consume small fish and invertebrates. Only suitable for very large aquariums; keep in groups of five or more with similarly sized, robust tankmates.

A rounded, deep-bodied silver dollar from the Amazon and Orinoco basins. Peaceful and gregarious; keep in groups of five or more. Omnivorous with strong herbivorous tendencies; supplement with fresh vegetables, and do not expect soft-leaved aquarium plants to survive.

A disc-shaped silver dollar from the Paraguay, Paraná, and Uruguay river systems, somewhat more cold-tolerant than Amazon relatives. Peaceful and gregarious; keep in groups of five or more with robust tankmates. Not plant-safe; feeds primarily on fruits, seeds, and vegetable matter in nature.

A massive South American serrasalmid reaching 88 cm — one of the largest in the piranha family. Commonly sold as cheap juveniles but grows rapidly to a size requiring public-aquarium-scale facilities (2,500+ litres). Primarily herbivorous; will consume small fish when full-grown. Not suitable for home aquariums.
behavior: Largely peaceful with similarly large fish but will eat anything small enough to swallow; grows enormous, and its constant activity and occasional fin-nipping make it suitable only for very large tanks.
reproduction: A migratory broadcast spawner not bred in home aquaria, though farmed in numbers for food in South America and the Far East.
dimorphism: Mature females are a little rounder-bellied than males.

description: A unique scale-eating specialist (lepidophage) that attacks the flanks of other fish to strip and consume their scales — a feeding strategy unlike any other common aquarium fish. Adults are highly aggressive and must be kept alone. Best suited to experienced keepers who can appreciate its unusual biology.
behavior: A specialised scale-eater that darts at other fish to bite off scales; adults are highly aggressive and best kept singly, as mature females in particular attack tankmates relentlessly.
reproduction: Breeding is unreported in the hobby.
dimorphism: The male has a clearly convex anal fin, which the female lacks.

description: The Orinoco piranha, closely related to the red-bellied piranha but exclusive to Venezuela's Orinoco basin. Requires a massive dedicated aquarium — at least 1,300 litres — and must be kept either alone or in a shoal of five or more; intermediate group sizes increase aggression. Not compatible with community tanks.
behavior: Juveniles shoal together, while adults form loose dominance hierarchies and are best kept alone; even well-fed fish may treat the fins of similarly-sized tankmates as food.
reproduction: Breeding is unrecorded in home aquaria, but is presumed similar to the related red-bellied piranha.
dimorphism: Females reach a larger size and a more rounded body shape than males, especially when in spawning condition.

description: The largest Pygocentrus piranha, endemic to Brazil's isolated São Francisco River basin. Extremely aggressive; suitable only for very large dedicated specimen tanks or public aquariums. Rarely available in the hobby and considered one of the most demanding piranha species to maintain.

An unusually elongated piranha with a distinctly pike-like body plan, adapted for ambush predation on smaller fish. Highly aggressive and strictly solitary; cannot be housed with any tankmates. Relatively accessible among dedicated piranha keepers — one of the few Serrasalmus suitable for a 450-litre home setup.
behavior: A solitary, highly aggressive piranha that feeds on the fins and scales of other fish and cannot be trusted with any tankmate; conspecifics fight until only one remains, so it must be kept alone.

description: One of the largest piranha species, regularly exceeding 40 cm. Extremely aggressive; adults will fight to the death with conspecifics and must be kept in strict isolation. Only suited to very large dedicated aquaria of 1,200+ litres and exclusively for experienced piranha keepers.

A medium-sized piranha from Brazil's Tocantins basin with a notably wide temperature tolerance. Like all Serrasalmus, it must be kept strictly alone and is unsuitable for community aquariums. Suitable for the dedicated piranha enthusiast with a well-filtered 450-litre dedicated setup.

A rarely traded Serrasalmus piranha from the Rio Madeira in the Amazon basin. Like all congeners, it must be kept in strict isolation — no tankmates of any kind. Care requirements follow the genus: well-oxygenated, slightly acidic water with efficient filtration and a sandy substrate.

A widely distributed piranha with an unusually broad temperature range, found from the Amazon south to the Uruguay River. Aquarium specimens typically reach 18-21 cm; wild females are reported to grow considerably larger. Strictly solitary; must be kept alone with robust filtration.
reproduction: One of the easiest piranhas to breed in aquaria, having been spawned successfully though it demands ample space and experience.
dimorphism: There are no reliable external differences between the sexes, though females in spawning condition look plumper.

description: A small, brightly colored Peruvian piranha known for its ruby-red coloration. Primarily a fin and scale eater in the wild, it can be acclimated to frozen and dead foods in captivity. Despite its small size it is still strictly a specimen fish, kept alone in a dedicated 110-litre setup.
behavior: A solitary, unpredictable piranha that does not make a reliable tankmate for anything, including its own kind, and is safest kept alone.

description: A sleek, needle-nosed surface predator from northern Colombia and Venezuela with a gar-like body form. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow, but will ambush anything that fits in its mouth. Best kept in groups of four or more; requires a very long tank with a tight lid, as it is an exceptional jumper.
behavior: A near-exclusive surface hunter that is surprisingly peaceful toward anything too large to swallow; juveniles school strongly while adults grow more solitary, so it is best kept in a group of four or more.

description: A sleek, torpedo-shaped pike characin from the Amazon, Tocantins, and Orinoco basins. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow but will ambush smaller tankmates. Despite its relatively modest adult length, it demands a very spacious aquarium of nearly 2,000 litres and is best kept in groups of four or more with a secure, tight-fitting lid.
behavior: A surface-dwelling predator that is peaceful toward fish it cannot eat and shoals as a juvenile before becoming more solitary; it is skittish and a prodigious jumper, so a tightly covered tank is essential.

description: The larger of the two commonly kept Boulengerella species, reaching 42 cm. A surface-oriented ambush predator that requires enormous, public-aquarium-scale facilities. Best kept in groups of four or more; a tight-fitting, heavy lid is essential as this species is an exceptional jumper.

A powerful piscivore reaching 105 cm, distributed across the Congo, Nile, Niger, Zambezi, and other major African river systems. An apex predator that must be housed alone in public-aquarium-scale facilities. Juveniles can be weaned onto dead foods relatively easily, but adults still require enormous, well-filtered setups.
behavior: An extremely aggressive shoaling predator that hunts in packs in the wild and will tear apart anything smaller without warning; it is best kept alone or with similarly sized conspecifics in a very large tank.
reproduction: Has been bred in aquaria but little reliable information exists, as it is normally a migratory spawner.

description: An African pike characin from West and Central African river systems, superficially resembling the South American pike characins in body plan. Highly predatory; best housed alone or only with fish too large to be eaten. Requires large, well-filtered setups and is rarely encountered in the hobby.
behavior: A lightning-fast ambush predator that lies in wait and strikes suddenly; it can be kept in a group but bullies smaller individuals and may turn territorial enough to harass tankmates to death.

description: A massive predatory characin reaching 89 cm, infamous for its enormous canine teeth that can exceed 10 cm in large specimens — giving rise to the common name 'vampire fish'. An obligate piscivore that requires live fish initially, though some individuals learn to accept dead alternatives. Only suitable for very large public aquariums.
behavior: An aggressive, obligate fish-eater that can take prey up to half its own length and is especially hostile toward its own kind and similar-looking fish, so it needs a very large tank and careful tankmate choices.

description: A massive, torpedo-shaped predatory characin reaching 80 cm with a distinctly gar-like silhouette and prominent fang-like teeth. An obligate piscivore that in practice requires public-aquarium-scale facilities. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow; rarely kept by private hobbyists.
behavior: A slender, obligate piscivore and a prodigious jumper that can be skittish in close quarters but is otherwise peaceful toward fish too large to swallow; it needs a long, securely covered tank.

description: A slender, pike-like predatory characin with a bold black spot at the base of the caudal peduncle. Despite its modest adult size, this highly active open-water swimmer requires an enormous aquarium. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow; best kept in matched groups of four or more to reduce cannibalism.

The larger of the two commonly kept Acestrorhynchus species, distinguished by spotted body patterning. A highly active open-water predator requiring an enormous dedicated setup of at least 2,000 litres. Best maintained in groups of four or more similarly sized individuals.

A popular Sri Lankan barb known for the intense crimson-red and jet-black coloration males develop during breeding. Peaceful and hardy; suitable for community aquariums with other small, peaceful species. Keep in groups of six or more to dilute any mild fin-nipping tendency.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop vivid red-crimson body coloration and jet-black dorsal barring; females remain silver-olive and are less colorful.
behavior: A generally very peaceful, mid-water schooling barb best kept in a group of six or more; rival males colour up as they compete, and a proper shoal keeps the fish settled rather than skittish.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care; it spawns among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops in a controlled breeding setup.

description: A colorful South Indian barb with green, red, and black banded patterning, also known as the panda barb. Peaceful but lively; may unsettle very timid companions. Keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium to encourage natural schooling behavior.
behavior: A peaceful, gregarious mid-water barb that schools and shows lively male-to-male displays; males colour up most when competing for females or establishing a pecking order.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care that spawns regularly in mature aquaria.
dimorphism: Females are larger and fuller-bodied, while males are more vividly coloured, often with red and/or black in the dorsal fin.

description: A widely distributed Asian barb with a characteristic spot-and-stripe pattern, found from Pakistan through India to Southeast Asia. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates cooler water than most tropical barbs and can thrive in unheated aquariums in temperate climates. Peaceful; keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A peaceful, mid-water schooling barb that suits a community tank; a mixed-sex group of eight or more brings out natural shoaling and the males' competitive colour displays.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care; adults eat the eggs given the chance.
dimorphism: Adult males are noticeably slimmer and more colourful than females, especially in the unpaired and ventral fins.

description: A tiny, subtly beautiful barb from the Indian subcontinent, rarely exceeding 5 cm. Very peaceful and ideally suited to nano or species-specific setups; can be outcompeted by boisterous tankmates due to its small size. Keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium.

A small, peaceful barb from the Irrawaddy, Salween, and upper Mekong basins. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates cooler water down to 18°C, making it suitable for temperate or subtropical setups. A good choice for peaceful community aquariums or nano tanks with other small, similarly sized species.

A small, colorful Sri Lankan endemic with distinctive two-spot patterning and vibrant orange-yellow highlights. Peaceful and suitable for community aquariums with other small, non-aggressive species. Keep in groups of six or more in a well-planted setup with soft, slightly acidic water.
behavior: A generally very peaceful, mid-water schooling barb that suits a community tank and behaves most naturally in a group of six or more.

description: A striking medium-sized Indian barb named for the long filament extensions on the dorsal fin of adult males. Peaceful in community settings; males may establish hierarchies but aggression is generally mild. Prefers cooler water (20-25°C) than most tropical barbs and does best in groups of eight or more.
dimorphism: Males develop elongated filament extensions on the dorsal fin and display brighter coloration; females are plumper and less ornamented.
behavior: An active, mid-water schooling barb that needs a group of eight or more and plenty of swimming length; males jockey for rank within the shoal rather than harassing other fish.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care; adults eat the eggs and should be removed after spawning.

description: A medium-sized schooling barb from the Cauvery and Tungabhadra River systems of Karnataka, India. Males develop elongated dorsal fin rays, giving them a striking silhouette. Tolerates cooler water (19-25°C) than most tropical barbs; keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium.
dimorphism: Males develop extended dorsal fin rays and display more intense coloration; females are deeper-bodied.
behavior: An active, mid-water schooling barb that needs a group of eight or more and open swimming space; males form a hierarchy within the shoal while staying peaceful toward other fish.

description: A rare barb endemic to the hill streams of Kanyakumari District, Tamil Nadu, India — listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Closely related to the Arulius Barb and has similar care requirements; prefers cool, well-oxygenated water. Very uncommon in the trade; specimens are typically wild-caught or from specialist breeders.

A boldly patterned barb from the forested hill streams of Sarawak and West Kalimantan on Borneo. Peaceful and well-suited to a large community with similarly sized fish; wild individuals can reach 15 cm but aquarium specimens typically top out at 12 cm. Keep in groups of six or more in a spacious, well-planted setup.
behavior: A boisterous, mid-water schooling barb best kept in a group of six or more, where its energy and any nipping stay focused on the in-group hierarchy rather than on tankmates.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care; it spawns among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops.
dimorphism: Spawning males intensify in colour and develop head tubercles, while females grow slightly larger, heavier-bodied and less colourful.

description: A robust, wide-ranging barb from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, identified by its distinctive T-shaped or spanner-like markings. Adults can reach 15 cm and become boisterous; best kept with similarly sized, robust species. Provide a large tank of at least 150 cm length and keep in groups of six or more to distribute aggression.

A medium-sized barb from clear streams and acidic swamps of Peninsular Malaysia and formerly Singapore, now listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Wild populations have declined severely; most trade specimens are farm-raised. Care is similar to the Clown Barb; peaceful and best kept in groups of six or more in a well-planted aquarium.

A strikingly patterned barb from river shallows, streams, and ditches across the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Wild individuals reach 12 cm but aquarium specimens typically top out at 10 cm. Keep in groups of eight or more in a large, well-planted setup; prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water.

A small, patterned barb endemic to blackwater rivers and peat swamps of southern Kalimantan, Borneo. The intricate scale patterning gives it a snakeskin-like appearance. Requires soft, very acidic water; feed primarily on small live and frozen invertebrates. Keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A peaceful, mid-water schooling barb best kept in a group of eight or more; it is easily out-competed for food, so it should not share a tank with boisterous or very vigorous species.

description: A small, vividly banded barb from the blackwater streams and peat swamps of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Often confused in the trade with the five-band barb (*Desmopuntius pentazona*); best distinguished by the absence of a dorsal-fin spot. Needs soft, acidic water and live or frozen invertebrate foods. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A tiny, attractively marked African barb from slow-moving, forested waters of the Congo River basin in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Rarely exceeds 3.5 cm; best suited to a species-specific or West/Central African biotope nano setup. Requires stable, soft, slightly acidic water within a narrow temperature range; feed on small live and frozen invertebrates.

One of the smallest barbs in the hobby, from slow-moving, shaded rainforest streams and swamps of West and Central Africa (Cameroon to Congo). Feeds naturally on insect larvae and small crustaceans; accepts appropriately sized live and frozen foods. Demands very soft, acidic water and benefits from a biotope-style setup with leaf litter and subdued lighting.

A small, scaleless cyprinid endemic to Inle Lake in Myanmar — listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Males are iridescent silvery-blue with a vivid red snout and red tail lobes; females are subdued. The high-altitude origin means it needs cooler, alkaline water unlike most nano cyprinids. Males are aggressive with each other; keep a single male with multiple females.
dimorphism: Males are iridescent silvery-blue with red snout and red tail lobes; females are duller with a dark pigmentation spot near the anus.
behavior: Peaceful toward other species but with lively male-to-male sparring, so it is best kept in a shoal of six or more skewed toward females to spread out the dominance disputes.

description: One of the smallest and most widely available nano fish, native to blackwater swamps and streams of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Singapore. Reaches only 2.5 cm; requires soft, very acidic water. Best kept as a single-species group of eight or more in a well-planted nano tank; easily outcompeted by larger or boisterous tankmates.
behavior: A peaceful, rather timid mid-water schooler that suits small, densely planted tanks and should be kept in a group of eight or more to feel secure and show natural behaviour.
reproduction: An egg-scattering, continuous spawner with no parental care; small numbers of eggs are laid daily among plants in a mature aquarium.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured, while females are rounder-bellied.

description: A brilliantly colored nano rasbora endemic to the blackwater peat swamps of southern Borneo. The body is a vivid orange-red, making groups highly attractive in a well-planted setup. Extremely delicate; requires very soft, acidic water and small live or frozen foods. Easily overlooked or outcompeted in a community; best in a species tank or with other micro-fish.
behavior: A peaceful, timid mid-water schooler suited to small planted tanks; it should be kept in a group of eight or more alongside other small, gentle species rather than typical community fish.
reproduction: An egg-scattering, continuous spawner with no parental care; a few eggs are laid daily when conditions are favourable.
dimorphism: Females are rounder-bellied and often slightly larger, while males are more colourful, especially dominant individuals.

description: A tiny, reddish-pink nano rasbora from the lower Tapi River drainage of southern Thailand. Closely related to other Boraras species; distinguished by a black spot on the flank. Tolerates slightly less acidic water than some relatives (pH 5.0-7.0) but still needs soft conditions and small live or frozen invertebrate foods. Suitable only for nano or species-specific setups.

One of the smallest vertebrates kept in the aquarium hobby, rarely exceeding 1.5 cm. Endemic to northeastern Thailand in the Mekong basin. Very peaceful but easily intimidated; strictly a nano or species-only fish. Requires small live or frozen invertebrates and soft-to-neutral water. Impressive in groups of eight or more against a dark substrate.

A slender, active rasbora with a bold black horizontal stripe running from snout to tail. Native to slow-moving, heavily planted waters of the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins and northern Malay Peninsula. Hardy and peaceful; an ideal schooling fish for a planted community aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A peaceful, active mid-water schooling rasbora that should be kept in a group of eight or more to curb its nervousness and produce a natural, coordinated display.
reproduction: An egg-scattering, continuous spawner with no parental care that spawns in well-planted mature tanks without intervention.
dimorphism: Adult males are slimmer, smaller and more intensely coloured than females.

description: A larger, elegant rasbora from the streams, rivers, and peat swamps of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Singapore, and Borneo. Named for its iridescent, glittering flanks. Peaceful and suitable for a large, well-planted community; provide a long tank with ample swimming space and keep in groups of six or more.
behavior: A very peaceful, active mid-water rasbora that is gregarious by nature and should be kept in a group of six or more with room to swim.

description: A small, adaptable rasbora from the peat swamp forests of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. The vivid emerald-green eye is the key identifying feature. Peaceful and tolerant of a wide pH range; suitable for planted nano tanks and larger community setups alike. Keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A very peaceful, mid-water schooling rasbora that should be kept in a group of eight or more to settle its nerves and bring out natural shoaling behaviour.
reproduction: An egg-scattering, continuous spawner with no parental care that spawns frequently in well-planted tanks.
dimorphism: Females are rounder-bellied and often slightly larger than males.

description: One of the largest rasboras, reaching 15 cm, with a striking forked tail marked by black-tipped lobes. Native to forest streams and peat swamp rivers of Peninsular Malaysia and Borneo. Peaceful and ideal for a spacious community with robust species; active swimmer that needs a long tank with open water. Keep in groups of six or more.
behavior: A gregarious, fast-swimming rasbora that holds near the surface and is an accomplished jumper able to slip through the smallest gap, so it needs a group of six or more and a tightly covered tank.
reproduction: An egg-scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits no parental care.

description: A medium-sized rasbora with a distinctive yellow tinge to the tail, native to blackwater streams, rivers, and forest ditches of Sumatra, Peninsular Malaysia, and Borneo. Tolerates very acidic water down to pH 4.0. Peaceful and active; provide a long tank with ample swimming space and keep in groups of six or more.

A medium-large rasbora from forested streams and peat swamps of Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo, now extirpated from Singapore. Identified by a series of bold dark spots ('portholes') along the lateral line. Peaceful and suitable for a spacious community with robust, similarly sized fish. Keep in groups of six or more.

One of the largest rasboras in the hobby, reaching 15 cm in a spacious aquarium. Native to peat swamps, forest streams, and slow-moving rivers of Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, and Borneo. Despite its size it is very peaceful; can, however, intimidate smaller, shyer companions. Keep in groups of six or more in a large, well-planted setup.

A close relative of the popular Harlequin Rasbora, distinguished by a glowing orange mark along the flank rather than the harlequin's solid black wedge. Native to blackwater streams and peat swamps of Sumatra and Borneo. Peaceful and suitable for a planted nano or community aquarium; keep in groups of eight or more for best behavior and coloration.
behavior: A very peaceful, mid-water schooling rasbora best kept in a group of eight or more, where it settles and shows its natural shoaling behaviour.
reproduction: Attaches its eggs to the undersides of broad plant leaves or other surfaces rather than scattering them, and gives no parental care.
dimorphism: Mature females are deeper-bodied and slightly larger than the slimmer, more intensely-coloured males.

description: A jewel-like nano fish from the blackwater peat swamps of Borneo, Sumatra, and Bangka Island, barely exceeding 2 cm. Males shimmer with electric blue-green iridescence. Requires very soft, acidic water and should be kept in very large groups of 20 or more for natural schooling behavior. Strictly a nano or species-specific setup; easily outcompeted in a standard community.
dimorphism: Males develop intense iridescent blue-green coloration; females are more subdued and rounder-bodied.

description: A tiny, rosy-red nano fish endemic to Inle Lake in Myanmar — listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Unlike most nano fish that prefer acidic water, Inle Lake is alkaline, so this species requires neutral to hard water (pH 6.0-8.0) and cooler temperatures (20-24°C). Very shy; best kept in a species-specific setup or with other small Inle Lake endemics.

A brilliantly colored small danio from the Mogaung Chaung river system of northern Myanmar, displaying a vivid orange-red band along the flank. Tolerates a very wide temperature range (16-26°C), making it suitable for unheated aquariums in temperate climates. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted setup.
behavior: A generally very peaceful, mid-water schooling danio that should be kept in a group of eight or more; males spar to sort out a hierarchy but rarely bother other tankmates.
reproduction: An egg-scattering spawner with no parental care that spawns frequently in well-conditioned tanks.
dimorphism: Mature females are rounder-bellied, less colourful and a little larger than males, most obviously in spawning condition.

description: A small danio from fast-flowing, clear streams of northern Myanmar, distinguished by vivid orange fins. Closely related to and sometimes confused with the Glowlight Danio. Tolerates cool water down to 16°C; an excellent choice for subtropical or unheated setups. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more.

A tiny nano danio from the Mogaung Chaung drainage of northern Myanmar, barely reaching 3 cm. Often sold as 'Gold Ring Danio' in the trade due to a series of golden rings along the body. Peaceful and adaptable; suitable for nano aquariums. Keep in groups of eight or more alongside other small, peaceful species.

A small, attractively spotted danio endemic to central and southern Myanmar, found in the Ayeyarwady, Sittaung, and Bago river drainages. Peaceful and hardy; tolerates a wide range of temperatures and water chemistry. A good choice for a smaller planted community aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A very peaceful, active but somewhat timid mid-water danio that schools and should be kept in a group of eight or more, where males put on competitive displays among themselves.
reproduction: An egg-scattering spawner with no parental care; it spawns readily in well-planted tanks and fry may appear spontaneously.
dimorphism: Mature females are rounder-bellied, less colourful and slightly larger than males.

description: A jewel-like nano fish from Inle Lake in Myanmar, listed as Endangered in the wild but commonly captive-bred. The turquoise body and vivid orange-pink fins make it highly sought-after. Unlike typical nano fish, it needs alkaline water (pH 7.0-8.0) and cooler temperatures (20-24°C), matching its highland lake origin. Keep in very large groups of 20 or more to disperse male aggression.
behavior: A timid, gregarious shoaler of densely planted tanks that holds in the mid-to-lower water column; males spar among themselves but stay peaceful toward tankmates, and the fish grow bolder in large groups of twenty or more.

description: A softly colored danio with rosy-pink iridescence, native to rivers and streams of the Mekong basin in northern Thailand, Laos, and Myanmar. Hardy and peaceful; one of the more muted members of the Danio genus yet appealing in groups. Keep in groups of eight or more alongside other small, peaceful community fish.

A subtly beautiful danio from the peninsular Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, and adjacent islands, notable for its electric blue lateral stripe. Closely resembles the Blue Danio in appearance. Peaceful and hardy; tolerates a wide temperature range (18-26°C) and neutral to slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A large, fast-moving danio from the streams and rivers of the Indian peninsula and Sri Lanka, ranging from lowland floodplains to 1,300 m elevation. Active and boisterous; can disturb slow-moving or long-finned tankmates despite not being overtly aggressive. Best kept with robust, similarly sized species in a large, well-aerated aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.
behavior: A constantly active, mid-water schooling danio that needs a group of eight or more and plenty of swimming room; it is not aggressive, and rival males simply colour up in each other's presence.
reproduction: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care that spawns frequently in well-planted mature tanks.
dimorphism: Mature females are rounder-bellied, less colourful and slightly larger than males.

description: A small, rarely seen devario from mountain streams of the Ping River basin in northern Thailand, sometimes called Fire Bar Danio in the trade. Feeds primarily on terrestrial insects in the wild; readily accepts quality dried foods supplemented with small live or frozen invertebrates. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more.

A large, torpedo-shaped cyprinid with greatly elongated, whisker-like pectoral fins that give it its common name. Native across South and Southeast Asia from Pakistan to Myanmar. An active surface swimmer prone to fin-nipping, especially in small groups or with long-finned species; best kept in large schools with robust, similarly sized tankmates. Requires a tight-fitting lid as it can jump.
behavior: A gregarious, surface-dwelling shoaler that should be kept in a group of eight or more and is an accomplished jumper, so the tank needs a tight-fitting cover.

description: A slender, nearly translucent surface-dwelling cyprinid widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia. Feeds primarily on small invertebrates at the surface; keep a tight-fitting lid as it is an active jumper. Peaceful and adaptable; tolerates a wide pH range. Keep in groups of eight or more and avoid slow-moving or long-finned tankmates.
behavior: A surface-shoaling barb that swims in a group at or just below the waterline and is an accomplished jumper, so it needs a group of eight or more and a tightly covered tank.

description: A rare cousin of the White Cloud Mountain Minnow, endemic to the Ben Hai River system of north-central Vietnam. Slightly smaller and more cold-tolerant than the common White Cloud. Peaceful and hardy; suitable for nano tanks and temperate setups. Best kept in groups of ten or more alongside other small, peaceful species.
behavior: A very peaceful, mid-water schooling fish that prefers a group of ten or more and appreciates gentle water movement in the tank.

description: A large, silver-bodied shark-shaped cyprinid native to Borneo, Sumatra, and Peninsular Malaysia. Widely sold as juveniles at 5-8 cm but grows to 35 cm and requires a massive tank — commonly mis-stocked. Peaceful toward other fish despite its size but is an active, boisterous swimmer that will overwhelm small tankmates. Keep in groups of five or more in a tank of at least 240 cm length.
behavior: A skittish, powerful-jumping schooler that needs a securely covered tank and a group of its own kind; kept singly or in small numbers it develops a pecking order and can turn aggressive toward similar-looking fish.
reproduction: Not bred in home aquaria, but farmed in large numbers for the trade using hormones.
dimorphism: Sexually mature females are noticeably thicker-bodied than males, though young fish cannot be reliably sexed.

description: A massive, jet-black labeo from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins capable of reaching 90 cm in the wild; aquarium specimens typically reach 50-60 cm. Highly territorial and belligerent toward conspecifics and similarly-shaped species. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist private installations. Not recommended for home aquariums.
behavior: A large, belligerent and aggressively territorial bottom-dweller, especially toward its own kind and similarly shaped fish in tight spaces, so it is best kept singly in a very large tank.
reproduction: A seasonal spawner at the onset of the wet season in nature; breeding is not established in home aquaria.

description: A streamlined African labeo from rocky, fast-flowing rivers of the Zambezi, Limpopo, and Congo basins. Named for its vivid red eye. Grazes on diatoms and algae scraped from rocks; will also accept plant matter and quality dried foods. Can be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped bottom dwellers; usually kept as a single specimen.

A boldly patterned African labeo from the Congo and Ogowe river basins, recognized by its dark-spotted or marbled body. Territorial and aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped species; must be kept singly. Grazes on algae and aufwuchs; will also eat small invertebrates and quality sinking foods. Only wild-caught specimens are available.

A common SE Asian garra widely available in the aquarium trade, often confused with the true Siamese Algae Eater. Tolerant of a wide range of conditions; feeds on algae, biofilm, and meaty foods. Not particularly tolerant of conspecifics in small tanks — keep in groups of three or more to establish a hierarchy and reduce aggression. Sometimes reclassified as Ceratogarra cambodgiensis.
behavior: A bottom-dwelling algae and biofilm grazer that turns aggressive toward similarly shaped fish when kept singly but settles into a hierarchy in a group of three or more.

description: A striking small garra from the fast-flowing, well-oxygenated hill streams of the Arakan Mountains in western Myanmar. Named for its panda-like black-and-white patterning. Relatively peaceful toward non-garra species; develops a pecking order with conspecifics. Needs excellent water flow and oxygenation; keep in groups of three or four in a rocky biotope setup.
behavior: A bottom-dwelling biofilm grazer that forms a loose hierarchy in groups, settling disputes by charging, flaring its fins and paling in colour rather than causing real harm.
reproduction: Bred commercially in Myanmar since 2008, spawning seasonally from May to July with eggs hatching in roughly 24-30 hours.
dimorphism: Males develop tubercles on the head, lateral line and caudal peduncle and are slimmer-bodied than females.

description: A cold-water garra from the rivers and springs of Turkey, Syria, Jordan, and the Middle East — famous for use in 'fish spa' treatments where it nibbles dead skin. Requires cool, well-oxygenated water (14-20°C) that most tropical setups cannot provide. Tolerates a wide pH range but not tolerant of conspecifics; keep in small groups of three or four with good water flow.

A large, algae-eating cyprinid from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, valued for its appetite for tough algae types including black beard algae. Tolerates cool water down to 16°C. Strictly a herbivore — avoid high-protein foods. Peaceful when kept in groups of six or more; individual specimens can become territorial. Requires a long tank of at least 180 cm.

A massive SE Asian barb reaching 1 m in length, native to Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula. The common name comes from the intoxicating effect of its flesh when eaten after the fish has ingested fermenting plant matter. Peaceful toward similarly-sized tankmates but will consume smaller fish. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist installations.

A large, powerful predatory barb from the Mekong, Chao Phraya, Malay Peninsula, Borneo, Java, and Sumatra, capable of reaching 70 cm. Primarily a piscivore that feeds on smaller fish, insects, and crustaceans. Becomes increasingly dominant with age; juveniles are more tolerant. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist setups. Keep in groups of five.

A large, peaceful herbivore widely distributed across the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Java, and Borneo. Named for its characteristic thick, bony lips used for scraping algae and plant matter. Occasionally appears in the aquarium trade. Peaceful with most species; needs a large tank with ample herbivore-friendly foods.

A fast-moving, surface-dwelling cyprinid endemic to the fast-flowing hill streams of Kerala in the Western Ghats, India. Feeds primarily on terrestrial and aquatic insects; readily accepts quality dried foods with live or frozen supplements. Vigorous and boisterous; develops a pecking order and is unsuitable for slow-moving or delicate tankmates. Keep in groups of five or more.

A large, silver-bodied barb with vivid red fins from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins and peninsular Thailand. Closely related to the Tinfoil Barb. Active and boisterous; will consume smaller tankmates and disturb timid species. Keep in groups of six or more with robust, similarly-sized fish. Readily accepts most foods including plant matter, flakes, and pellets.
reproduction: An egg scatterer with no parental care; not bred in home aquaria, though raised in aquaculture in its native range.

description: A sleek, torpedo-shaped surface fish native to rivers of the Malay Peninsula, Indochina, and Indonesia. Reaches 30 cm in the wild but typically stays around 22 cm in aquaria. An accomplished jumper that demands a tightly-fitted lid and a large, open swimming space. Feeds primarily on surface insects, invertebrates, and small fish fry; keep only with robust tankmates too large to be eaten. Best kept in groups of three or more.

A tiny corydoras from the Madeira River basin that spends more time hovering in the mid-water column than most of its bottom-dwelling relatives. Keep in groups of 6 or more; a fine-sand substrate is appreciated even though it frequently schools away from the bottom. Accepts most small prepared and frozen foods. FishBase currently recognises this species as Gastrodermus pygmaeus.
behavior: A very peaceful, tightly shoaling dwarf cory that, unlike most of its relatives, spends much of its time swimming in midwater as well as foraging the substrate; it should be kept in a group of six or more.
reproduction: Deposits its eggs on the tank glass or among fine-leaved plants with no parental care, the adults eating the eggs if given the chance.
dimorphism: Females are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when full of eggs, and are often slightly larger.

description: One of the smallest corydoras species and one of the most pelagic, spending much of its time hovering and schooling in open water rather than scavenging the bottom. Native to the Amazon and Paraguay basins. Requires small live and frozen foods such as micro-worms, Daphnia, and Cyclops. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase currently recognises this species as Gastrodermus hastatus.
behavior: A peaceful, gregarious dwarf cory unusual for swimming in midwater rather than staying substrate-bound; it should be kept in a group of six or more to feel secure.
reproduction: Spawns after conditioning and a large, cool, soft-water change, depositing eggs on the glass, fine-leaved plants or spawning mops.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably broader and deeper-bodied than males.

description: A tiny, speckled corydoras from soft, acidic streams of the Upper Orinoco basin in Venezuela. Slightly more bottom-oriented than its close relatives C. pygmaeus and C. hastatus, though it also ventures into open water. Prefers fine sand, leaf litter, and dim lighting. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase currently recognises this species as Hoplisoma habrosum.
behavior: A peaceful, gregarious dwarf cory that forages close to the substrate and gathers near plants and structure; it is best kept in a group of six or more, ideally ten.
reproduction: Spawns when triggered by a cool water change and extra oxygenation, depositing eggs on the glass, among fine-leaved plants or in spawning mops.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably broader and deeper-bodied than males.

description: A mid-sized corydoras from the Purus River basin of Brazil, recognisable by its reticulated dark pattern on a pale body. A hardy, peaceful community fish that is well-suited to the beginner. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate; will graze on sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and Daphnia. FishBase currently recognises this species as Hoplisoma schwartzi.
behavior: A very peaceful bottom-dwelling cory that is far more confident and active in the company of its own kind, so it should be kept in a group of six or more.
reproduction: Spawns in the classic cory T-position, depositing eggs on the tank glass, though it is reputedly tricky to induce.
dimorphism: Females are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when full of eggs, and are often slightly larger.

description: A striking blackwater corydoras from the Rio Negro basin of Brazil, distinguished by its elongated dorsal-fin spine and distinctive dark patterning. Requires soft, acidic water typical of the Negro's blackwater environment. More demanding than common corydoras; keep in groups of 6 or more with pristine water quality. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma davidsandsi.

A soft-water corydoras from the upper Rio Negro and Meta River basins, closely resembling the bandit cory (C. metae) but identifiable by a narrower eye stripe that does not meet the dorsal blotch. Naturally inhabits acidic blackwater streams; prefers soft, acidic to neutral conditions in captivity. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma melini.

Named for the bold black mask across its eyes and a large dorsal blotch connected by a stripe, the bandit cory is native to the Meta River basin of Colombia. A hardy, peaceful community fish suitable for beginners once water parameters are stable. Often confused with the false bandit cory (C. melini); the bandit's mask stripe connects cleanly to the dorsal spot. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma metae.
reproduction: Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, with spawning triggered by water changes and a 2:1 male-to-female ratio.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when gravid.

description: A small, spotted corydoras from the Napo River drainage of eastern Ecuador and Loreto, Peru. A typical community corydoras with a calm temperament and modest space requirements. Accepts sinking pellets supplemented with small live or frozen foods. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Gastrodermus napoensis.

A small, striped corydoras from the upper Amazon basin, named after the Loreto region of Peru. A peaceful, undemanding community fish with slightly cooler temperature preferences than many Amazon corydoras. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand; accepts sinking pellets and small live or frozen foods. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma loretoense.
reproduction: Spawns on the tank glass, typically in areas of higher flow, following the usual cory pattern of males pursuing females.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when full of eggs.

description: A widely distributed corydoras found throughout the upper Amazon basin in Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Named for its spotted patterning, which superficially resembles the leopard or three-line corydoras. A hardy, adaptable species well suited to community aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma leucomelas.

A small corydoras from the Guaporé River basin of Brazil and Bolivia, easily identified by the distinct dark blotch on the caudal peduncle that gives it its common name. A calm, social fish suitable for community tanks with other peaceful species. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma caudimaculatum.
reproduction: Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, with spawning triggered by a large, cooler, well-oxygenated water change.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and higher-bodied than males.

description: A distinctive corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, named for the bold black-and-white striped tail fin that resembles a flag. One of the larger corydoras species. Inhabits blackwater environments and prefers soft, slightly acidic water in captivity. Keep in groups of 6 or more; avoid strongly alkaline conditions. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis robineae.

A reticulated corydoras from the Amazon River in Peru and Brazil, superficially resembling the network corydoras (C. reticulatus) but distinguished by a different pattern arrangement. A peaceful community fish that accepts most sinking foods. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis sodalis.

A handsome corydoras from the Purus River basin of Brazil with attractive dark reticulation on a pale body. Prefers slightly cooler temperatures than many Amazonian corydoras. A peaceful community fish; keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. Less commonly available than many other corydoras. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis pulcher.

A small corydoras from the Meta River basin of Colombia, named in honour of the renowned fish author and collector Dr. Herbert R. Axelrod. Prefers soft to moderately soft, slightly acidic water. A peaceful community fish that does best in groups of 6 or more on a fine-sand substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma axelrodi.
reproduction: Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, with no parental care.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males.

description: A tiny and seldom-seen corydoras from the upper Caquetá River basin of Colombia, rarely available in the trade. Reaches only about 4 cm and is one of the smaller corydoras species. Care is typical for small Colombian corydoras: soft, slightly acidic water, a fine-sand substrate, and groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma reynoldsi.

A subtropical corydoras from the coastal rivers of Santa Catarina and Paraná, and the Iguaçu River basin in southern Brazil. Unlike most tropical corydoras, it prefers distinctly cooler water temperatures (18–23°C) reflecting its temperate habitat. Unsuitable for warm tropical aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more; a well-oxygenated tank is beneficial. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma ehrhardti.

A larger blackwater corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, named for its mimicry of the external appearance of Corydoras adolfoi. Requires soft, acidic water typical of the Rio Negro. One of the larger and more striking corydoras, reaching 6.5 cm. Keep in groups of 6 or more; avoid neutral or alkaline conditions. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis imitator.
reproduction: Eggs are deposited on the glass, among fine-leaved plants or in spawning mops, the mops making removal easier.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably broader and deeper-bodied than males.

description: A handsome blackwater corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, with a distinctive dark stripe along the body. Named after Dr. Warren Burgess. Prefers soft, acidic water conditions reflecting its blackwater origins; avoid hard or alkaline water. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma burgessi.
reproduction: Spawning is triggered by a large, cooler water change; the eggs are laid on the glass or vegetation and incubate for 3-4 days.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when gravid.

description: A small corydoras from the upper Amazon basin with a distinctive dark mask across the face. Prefers slightly cooler and softer water than many tropical corydoras, reflecting its montane Amazonian origins in Ecuador and Peru. A peaceful community fish; keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma atropersonatum.
reproduction: Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, with spawning triggered by a large water change and extra oxygenation.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when gravid.

description: A plain, uniformly-coloured corydoras from the Orinoco River basin, distinguished by its lack of any strong pattern. Despite its seemingly drab appearance, it has a subtle sheen and is sought by corydoras collectors. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma concolor.

An attractive, spotted corydoras from the lower Tapajós River basin of Brazil, with a reticulated pattern that gives it a decorative appearance. A hardy, peaceful community fish well suited to the beginner. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate; accepts sinking pellets and small live or frozen foods. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis ornatus.

A small, attractively spotted corydoras from the coastal rivers of the Guianas. Distinguished by the dark spots and a blotch on the dorsal fin. A hardy, adaptable species suitable for community aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma melanistium.

A medium-large corydoras from the Suriname River basin and coastal rivers of French Guiana, identifiable by its scattered dark spots on a pale body. One of the original Corydoras species described by Bloch in 1794. A peaceful, hardy community fish suitable for most neutral to soft-water community setups. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma punctatum.

A small, seldom-imported corydoras from the Nanay River basin of Loreto, Peru. Rarely available in the hobby and lightly documented. Care is typical for Amazonian corydoras: moderate temperatures, slightly soft to neutral water, fine sand, and groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis sychri.

A small corydoras from the Guaporé River basin of Brazil and Bolivia, at the border between Amazon and Paraná drainages. Prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water. The elongated body shape distinguishes it from many of its relatives. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Gastrodermus guapore.
reproduction: Eggs are deposited on the glass, among fine-leaved plants or in spawning mops; the species is unusual for aggregating in midwater.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably broader and deeper-bodied than males.

description: A cool-water corydoras from the coastal rivers of southeastern Brazil (Espírito Santo to Paraná), with attractive blue-green tones and a spotted pattern. Requires cooler temperatures than most tropical corydoras, reflecting its subtropical habitat. Incompatible with warm tropical aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand in a well-oxygenated tank. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma nattereri.

A well-spotted corydoras from the upper Amazon basin, widely kept in the hobby but frequently mislabelled or confused with similar spotted species such as C. punctatus. A robust, adaptable community fish tolerant of a range of water conditions. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis ambiacus.

A moderately-sized corydoras from the Inírida and upper Orinoco River basin in Colombia, with a distinctive mask through the eye and a dark dorsal blotch. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water from its blackwater and clearwater habitats. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis delphax.

A tiny aspidoras catfish from the upper Araguaia River basin of Brazil, related to but distinct from the Corydoras genus. Slightly smaller than most corydoras and requires very clean, soft water. Behaves similarly to its relatives: groups of 6 or more, fine-sand substrate, and varied diet of small foods. FishBase has reclassified this species to Gastrodermus pauciradiatus.
reproduction: Spawns much like Corydoras, often induced by a large, much cooler water change, depositing its eggs on surfaces.
dimorphism: Females grow larger and are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied than males, especially when gravid.

description: A small aspidoras catfish endemic to the upper Paraná River basin of Brazil. Rarely available in the hobby. As a subtropical species from southern Brazil, it prefers cooler and neutral to slightly acidic water compared to most tropical corydoras. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand with good water quality.

A small scleromystax from the coastal rivers of southeastern Brazil (Espírito Santo to São Paulo). More robust and armoured than typical corydoras, the Scleromystax genus is sexually dimorphic with males developing odontode bristles. Requires a long tank with good flow. Keep in groups of 6 or more with a mix of males and females when possible.

One of the largest corydoras-type catfish, from the upper Paraguay River basin of Brazil. Despite its size (up to 10 cm), it retains the peaceful, gregarious nature of the family. Requires a spacious tank with a soft substrate and excellent filtration. Keep in groups of 6 or more. The elongated dorsal fin spine is a distinguishing feature.
dimorphism: Adult females are noticeably rounder and higher-bodied and grow slightly larger than males.

description: A large, hog-nosed brochis from the western Amazon basin with a distinctive elongated, downturned snout adapted for rooting through substrate. One of the largest members of the corydoras family. Peaceful and suitable for large community tanks. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a deep, soft-sand substrate that allows natural foraging behavior.

An elegant, elongated loricariid catfish from the Magdalena, San Jorge, and Cesar River basins of Colombia, with a characteristic long rostrum and trailing filaments on the tail and fins. A dedicated algae grazer that also rasps wood and accepts blanched vegetables. Prefers warm, well-oxygenated water. Can be kept singly or in pairs. FishBase recognises this species as Sturisomatichthys aureus.

A large loricariid from the Pacific and Caribbean drainages of Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador, reaching 30 cm. Unusually cool temperature requirements (20–22°C) reflect its coastal rivers influenced by the Humboldt Current. Incompatible with warm tropical aquaria. Requires a long tank, excellent water quality, and a diet rich in vegetable matter. FishBase recognises this species as Sturisomatichthys panamensis.

A slender, stick-like loricariid from the Paraguay River basin, one of the most widely kept whiptail catfish. Camouflages itself flat against wood and substrate. Peaceful and largely undemanding; accepts sinking pellets, blanched vegetables, and small live foods. Males guard eggs laid on flat surfaces. Can be kept in pairs or small groups.

A remarkable twig-mimic catfish from the Orinoco basin with an extremely elongated body and rostrum. A specialist herbivore — the bulk of its diet must be algae and blanched vegetables; prepared foods alone are insufficient. Sensitive to deteriorating water quality and requires consistent pristine conditions. Largely sedentary and will not compete for food in busy tanks. Best kept singly or in species tanks.
reproduction: Spawns at night, the female attaching eggs to a vertical surface (usually the tank glass), which the male then guards and fans until they hatch in about 6-10 days.
dimorphism: Mature males develop a broader snout bearing rows of odontodes, which are absent in females.

description: An unusual, extremely flattened loricariid from the Paraná, Paraguay, and Uruguay basins that resembles a flounder in profile. Spends the day buried in soft substrate and emerges to feed at night. Requires a very large tank with a deep, fine-sand substrate and cool subtropical temperatures (18–24°C). A specialist species demanding excellent water quality and live or frozen invertebrate foods.

A large, boldly striped wood-eating pleco from the Orinoco and Amazon systems. Requires a constant supply of driftwood to rasp — this is a dietary necessity, not just decoration. Peaceful with most fish but very aggressive toward other plecos and catfish; only one specimen per tank is recommended. Produces copious waste and demands excellent filtration.
reproduction: A difficult cavity spawner triggered by simulating the rainy season with cooler, softer water; a bonded pair is needed and the female lays her eggs in a sheltered spot.
dimorphism: Hard to sex reliably, though mature males are reported to have a more pointed genital papilla.

description: A sought-after wood-eating pleco from the Cauca and Magdalena River basins of Colombia, recognised by its striking vivid-blue irises. Like other Panaque, it requires driftwood as a primary dietary component and strong filtration to handle the resulting cellulose waste. Highly aggressive toward other plecos; keep only one per tank. Rarely encountered in the trade.

One of the largest plecos known, reaching at least 60 cm and reported from main-channel habitats of the upper Amazon (Solimões) in Peru and Brazil. Strictly a public aquarium fish — no home aquarium can realistically house an adult. A wood-eating specialist like its Panaque relatives. Rarely available; juveniles occasionally appear in the trade but quickly outgrow any domestic setup.

A small, attractive pleco (L204) from the San Alejandro River in the Ucayali basin of Peru, with a striking pattern of white or cream vermiculations on a dark body. Like other Panaqolus, it requires driftwood to rasp. Territorial toward other plecos but manageable in size. Provide good water flow and hiding places. Rarely imported; commands a premium price.

A heavily spined, leopard-spotted pleco from the Rupununi River basin of Guyana and Brazil. Unlike the wood-eating Panaque group, Pseudacanthicus species are carnivorous and require high-protein foods such as meaty sinking pellets, prawns, and frozen invertebrates. Highly aggressive toward other catfish; one per tank only. The dense armoured spines make handling hazardous.

A heavily spined, dark-bodied Pseudacanthicus from the Amazon basin in Brazil and Ecuador. Unusually cool temperature requirements (20–24°C) distinguish it from most Amazon species. Like all cactus plecos, it is carnivorous, requiring meaty foods, and highly aggressive toward other catfish — keep singly. Dense armour and spines make handling dangerous.

A striking L264 pleco from the Tapajós River basin of Brazil, identifiable by the white spots on a dark body and a distinctive upturned snout. Unlike the vegetarian Panaque group, Leporacanthicus are strongly carnivorous — live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, mussel, and prawn are essential. Males are territorial with conspecifics; provide a cave per fish. Water movement should be moderate to strong.

A distinctive, flat-bodied pleco from the lower Rio Negro basin with an attractive marbled or banded pattern and broad, fan-like pectoral fins. A primarily herbivorous species requiring a vegetable-rich diet supplemented with algae wafers and blanched greens. Softer, acidic water reflecting its blackwater origins is preferred. Peaceful and suitable for community tanks with compatible species.
reproduction: A cave spawner: the male selects the cave and tends the eggs, fanning them with his fins and defending them until they hatch.
dimorphism: Mature males develop longer odontodes along the snout and pectoral spines than females.

description: A massive, heavily spined pleco from the lower Tocantins River that reaches 100 cm and is strictly a public aquarium fish — no domestic tank can house an adult. Juveniles are sold in the hobby but quickly outgrow any private setup. Adults become extremely territorial and must be kept singly. A generalist detritivore accepting a wide range of sinking and fresh foods.

One of the largest Amazonian plecos, reaching 100 cm in the wild (captive specimens typically 50–60 cm). A public aquarium fish entirely unsuitable for the home hobbyist as an adult. Juveniles are occasionally sold; buyers must plan for a species that becomes massive, aggressive, and heavily armoured. Requires extremely strong filtration and large quantities of varied food.

A large, distinctive loricariid from the upper Amazon basin, recognisable by its rough, raised-ridge armour and the fleshy protuberances around the snout that give it its common name. A dedicated algae and biofilm grazer requiring a large tank with substantial algae growth or supplemental vegetable feeding. Keep singly or in species setups with adequate territory.

A mid-sized, striped Peckoltia widely distributed across the Amazon basin, known as the banded peckoltia pleco. An omnivorous loricariid that rasps algae and biofilm but also benefits from small meaty foods. Males are territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped plecos; provide caves and line-of-sight breaks. Accepts a range of sinking and fresh vegetable foods.

A larger Peckoltia from the rivers of Guyana and Venezuela, including the Essequibo, Rupununi, and Orinoco systems. Uncommonly imported and infrequently available. An omnivorous loricariid that feeds on algae, biofilm, and small invertebrates. Males are territorial; provide plenty of caves and hiding spots. Prefers warm water with moderate flow.

The L183 starlight bristlenose pleco from the upper and middle Amazon, recognisable by white-spotted fins and the bristle tentacles on the snout of males. Larger and warmer-preferring than the common bristlenose (A. cirrhosus), requiring soft, slightly acidic water and stronger flow. Territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped plecos. Feeds on algae, sinking foods, vegetables, and occasional invertebrates.
reproduction: A cave spawner with the male responsible for brood care, guarding and fanning the eggs inside the cave.
dimorphism: Adult males develop fleshy head tentacles plus odontodes on the pectoral fins and gill covers, all lacking in females.

description: A strikingly tentacled Ancistrus from the Xingu and Tocantins River basins, named for the elaborate fleshy growths on the snout of mature males. A specialist of fast-flowing, rocky river habitats that requires strong current and high oxygen levels. Needs high-protein foods including frozen bloodworm, mussel, and prawn. Territorial; provide individual caves. Not suitable for still-water community setups.

A large, spotted sailfin pleco from the Amazon basin, one of the most commonly sold but widely mis-sized plecos in the hobby — often sold as a 'tank cleaner' for small aquaria but capable of reaching 58 cm. A prolific algae grazer and biofilm feeder. Requires a very large tank with excellent filtration and produces substantial waste. Territorial with other plecos; keep only one per tank.

One of the largest doradid catfish, reaching 90 cm, with a row of sharp lateral scutes that give it its common name. Despite its size, a true gentle giant — peaceful with nearly all tankmates it cannot swallow. A nocturnal detritivore that sifts the substrate for food. Requires a very large tank with substantial horizontal space and excellent biological filtration.
reproduction: Not achieved in home aquaria; this large doradid is not known to have been bred in captivity.

description: A massive doradid catfish named for its ability to crush and consume entire snails and other hard-shelled invertebrates with powerful pharyngeal teeth. From the Amazon, Tocantins, and Essequibo basins. Highly gregarious — must be kept in groups of 4 or more and will pine and refuse food when kept singly. Peaceful with large tankmates. A public or specialist aquarium species.

A small, armoured doradid catfish from the Amazon basin and coastal Guiana rivers. Nocturnal and secretive; spends the day hiding in caves or dense vegetation. Produces audible sounds by vibrating the swim bladder. Gregarious — best kept in groups of 4 or more of its own kind or with similar species. Will consume very small fish and invertebrates; not suitable with nano species.

A heavily spined doradid catfish from the Amazon and Essequibo River basins, very similar to A. cataphractus but slightly larger and more densely spined. Nocturnal, secretive, and highly gregarious — keep in groups of 4 or more. Produces audible croaking sounds. Will eat very small fish and invertebrates. The dense spines lock when extended, making the fish difficult to dislodge from crevices and dangerous to net.
reproduction: Little is documented, though related talking catfish form pair bonds and excavate breeding pits; success in home aquaria is unclear.
dimorphism: Mature females tend to be noticeably fuller-bodied than males.

description: A medium-sized armored doradid catfish from the Guianas and Amazon Basin, closely related to the larger striped Raphael. Nocturnal and secretive, spending days hidden in caves and driftwood hollows. Peaceful with fish too large to swallow but may consume very small tankmates at night. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and shrimp.

A strikingly patterned auchenipterid catfish from the blackwater Branco and Trombetas rivers of Brazil, with bold dark spots on a pale body recalling jaguar markings. Shy and nocturnal; requires caves and driftwood for daytime shelter. Small fish may be consumed at night — avoid very small tankmates. Gregarious and reportedly less shy in groups of 3+. FishBase records typical adults at 17 cm; hobby sources report up to 25 cm.

A small, nocturnal driftwood catfish from the upper Amazon of Peru, also known as the midnight catfish. Males develop a modified anal fin for internal fertilization — a trait unique to auchenipterids. Spends daylight hours wedged tightly into wood crevices and is rarely visible until lights go off. Peaceful with similarly sized or larger tankmates but may consume very small fish and shrimp after dark.

A large, active pimelodid from the Amazon, Orinoco, Paraná, and Guianan rivers, with a pale silver body boldly spotted in black and a deeply forked tail. Typical aquarium adults reach 28 cm; wild specimens exceed 38 cm SL (~44 cm TL). Nocturnal predator — will consume any fish small enough to fit in its wide mouth. Solitary individuals hide all day; groups of 3+ are more visible and active.

A fast-moving pimelodid from the Paraná and São Francisco basins, commonly sold as a juvenile at 18 cm but reaching 51 cm in the wild; long-term captive adults typically settle around 18–30 cm. Shoaling by nature — groups of 3+ are considerably more active and less skittish. Predatory toward smaller fish; keep only with robust tankmates of similar or larger size.
reproduction: No reports of successful breeding in the hobby, as this migratory species needs environmental cues that are hard to replicate in an aquarium.

description: A shovelnose catfish with a distinctive elongated, duck-bill snout that it uses to probe wood and roots for prey; adults reach 40–54 cm and require a 15,000-litre system. Exclusively suitable for public aquaria — juveniles are widely sold but almost always surrendered as they grow. Any fish that fits in its broad mouth will be eaten. Accepts large frozen and fresh seafood in captivity.
reproduction: Little is documented about captive breeding; in nature it spawns at the onset of the rainy season.

description: A spectacularly striped pimelodid, also sold as the gold zebra catfish, with bold black-and-white banding that fades in adults; reaches 60 cm. Suitable only for public aquaria or very large private systems — almost entirely piscivorous, and any tankmate small enough to swallow will be eaten. Adapts to large frozen prawns, squid, whitebait, and fish strips in captivity. Solitary and highly predatory.

One of the largest freshwater fish in South America, reaching nearly 2 metres and famous for completing the longest freshwater migration known — some individuals travel 5,500 km from the Amazon estuary to Andean spawning grounds and back. An exclusively public-aquarium species; no private enclosure can meet the requirements of a 192 cm piscivore. Adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity but must be fed sparingly — adults require feeding only once weekly.

The largest catfish in South America, reportedly reaching 3.6 m and over 200 kg in the wild, though aquarium specimens rarely exceed 2–2.5 m. Found across the Amazon, Orinoco, and Guianan river systems. A truly enormous obligate piscivore that is entirely unsuitable for any private aquarium — only the largest public aquaria can house adults. Adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity but requires feeding no more than once weekly.
reproduction: Unrecorded in captivity, owing to a natural migration cycle that spans thousands of kilometres between spawning and nursery grounds.

description: A bizarre, highly flattened ambush predator from the São Francisco River of Brazil, named for its enormous gaping mouth relative to its body. Sedentary by nature — it buries itself in sandy substrate and waits for prey. Cannot be kept with any fish less than half its own length. Males provide parental care to adhesive eggs. A specialist fish for experienced aquarists with very large systems and knowledge of South American endemics.
behavior: Sedentary ambush predator; spends most of its time motionless, buried in sand or lying on the bottom. Analogous in strategy to the Asian Chaca catfish despite distant relation.
reproduction: A paternal nest-guarder that has occasionally spawned in aquaria, the male taking over and defending the nest of eggs and fry.

description: A huge, nearly scaleless pimelodid from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, reaching 140 cm and commonly encountered at 130 cm / 50 kg in the wild. Also called the manguruyu or black manguruyu. A nocturnal migratory piscivore that hunts shoaling characins; entirely a public aquarium species. Prefers cooler water than typical Amazonian fish, reflecting its large river habitat.

A large shovelnose catfish with bold black-and-white tiger striping from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, reaching 105 cm in aquariums and up to 130 cm in the wild. Debatably an aquarium fish at all given its eventual size; only very few dedicated private aquarists can accommodate it. Exclusively piscivorous; adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity. Territorial with conspecifics — must be kept alone. Adults require feeding once weekly maximum.

A large, bottom-dwelling pimelodid from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, also known as the sailfin pim or achara catfish, reaching 100 cm. Attractively marbled in brown and cream with elongated dorsal and pectoral fin rays. A large-scale predator that belongs in very large specialist or public aquarium systems only. Piscivorous by nature; adapts to large frozen and fresh seafood in captivity.

One of the most sought-after Synodontis, with a dark body covered in bright white spots that fade with age. Typical aquarium adults reach 25 cm; wild individuals can exceed 55 cm. Nocturnal and increasingly territorial — best kept as a solitary specimen or pair, not with other Synodontis. Omnivorous; accepts most sinking foods and live or frozen invertebrates. Not recommended for general community tanks due to adult aggression.

A medium-large Congo catfish with bold black-and-white barring and a distinctively elongated dorsal fin ray — the 'clown' patterning makes this one of the most recognizable Synodontis. Reaches 32 cm. Nocturnal and generally peaceful toward unrelated species but may become territorial with age. A sturdy community fish for Congo-themed tanks with robust tankmates. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and vegetable matter.

A medium-sized Congo synodontis also sold as the pyjama syno or orange-striped squeaker, with bold orange or yellow longitudinal stripes on a dark body. Reaches ~19.5 cm and adapts well to captivity. Nocturnal and generally peaceful but may show mild territorial tendencies as an adult. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, algae wafers, and vegetable matter.

A small, peaceful synodontis endemic to the fast-flowing rapids of the lower Congo River — one of the world's most biodiverse aquatic habitats. Feeds on insect larvae, algae, gastropods, crustaceans, sponges, and opportunistically on fish eggs. Prefers high-flow tanks with strong oxygenation. Suitable for Congo-biotope setups; peaceful with similarly sized tankmates but avoid aggressive or territorial species.

A small synodontis endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika in Zambia, often sold in the hobby as the 'false petricola' or 'dwarf petricola.' Reaches about 10 cm TL. Omnivorous, feeding naturally on insect larvae, algae, crustaceans, sponges, and bivalves. Requires hard, alkaline Tanganyikan water; does not tolerate soft or acidic conditions. Peaceful toward similarly sized tankmates.

The only synodontis native to Lake Malawi; reaches ~19 cm and is well-suited to Malawi cichlid community tanks. Hardy and adaptable, it will not fight with conspecifics but is aggressive toward other Synodontis species. May eat very small fish. Omnivorous and accepts most foods including cichlid pellets, frozen invertebrates, and blanched vegetables. Prefers the hard, alkaline water of its home lake.
reproduction: Breeding is not documented in aquaria; it is suspected to be a nocturnal egg scatterer.
dimorphism: Mature females are plumper, while males have a higher, more pointed dorsal fin.

description: A medium-large synodontis endemic to Lake Tanganyika, reaching 27 cm with a heavily granulated body texture that gives it its common name. Feeds naturally on insect larvae, algae, crustaceans, sponges, bivalves, and fish eggs at depths of 20–40 m. Requires hard, highly alkaline Tanganyikan water — unlike Congo synodontis, it will not tolerate soft or acidic conditions. Suitable for Tanganyika community tanks with robust cichlids.

A handsome Congo catfish instantly recognized by the single large dark spot on its pale flanks, reaching ~26.5 cm. Widespread across the Congo Basin including Pool Malebo, the Kasai, Ubangi, and Luapula drainages. Nocturnal and generally peaceful toward unrelated species but may show territorial tendencies with age. Accepts a varied diet including sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and vegetable matter.

A small, peaceful synodontis from Lake Tanganyika, reaching ~15 cm. One of the smaller Tanganyika synodontis and well-suited to Tanganyikan community tanks with smaller cichlids and other hard-water species. Omnivorous and accepts most foods. Unlike Congolese synodontis, it requires the hard, alkaline conditions of its home lake.

A slender, semi-transparent catfish from the Congo River system and adjacent Gabon drainages, reaching ~15 cm. Strongly diurnal and shoaling — keep in groups of 10 or more; individuals become withdrawn and stop feeding. Prefers moderate water movement and dimmed lighting. Despite its glass-catfish appearance, it is robust and active, spending the day in constant motion near the midwater.

A small, three-striped African catfish from the Niger and Cross River systems of Nigeria and Benin, sometimes sold as a glass catfish due to its semi-transparent body. Peaceful and schooling; a group of six or more is essential — single specimens withdraw and may refuse food. Typical aquarium size is around 8 cm; wild maximum reaches ~11.5 cm. Accepts most small sinking foods and frozen invertebrates.

A large, long-snouted catfish with giraffe-like brown blotching, found across the Nile, Chad, Congo, Niger, and Tanganyika systems — one of the most widespread African catfish. Reaches 60–80 cm and requires a very large aquarium. Despite its size it is a gentle omnivore, scavenging insects and plant material from muddy substrates. Males guard egg nests. Not a predator of tankmates but must be housed only with robust fish of substantial size.

An obligate air-breather capable of 'walking' across land using pectoral spines, surviving 18+ hours out of water. Typical aquarium adults reach ~50 cm; wild specimens in invasive populations (established in Florida) can exceed 100 cm. Highly predatory and voracious — will consume fish, molluscs, invertebrates, and plant matter. Invasive in many regions; regulations may prohibit keeping or releasing this species. A heavy lid is essential.
behavior: Obligate air-breather that surfaces frequently; can survive out of water for extended periods. Uses pectoral spines to walk on land. Highly invasive — check local regulations before acquiring.
reproduction: Digs a pit in the substrate to spawn, the male guarding the nest after the eggs are laid and the female helping once the fry hatch.
dimorphism: Wild males show spotting in the dorsal fin; otherwise the genital papilla is elongate and pointed in males and shorter and blunt in females.

description: A slender, striped bagrid catfish from the Indian subcontinent found in lowland lakes, swamps, and slow-moving rivers with muddy bottoms. Reaches ~21 cm SL (~24 cm TL). Nocturnal and somewhat predatory — will consume small fish and invertebrates at night. Does better in small groups of 3 or more.
