Tolerates a range of conditions but prefers neutral, slightly soft water. Occurs occasionally in mildly brackish lowland habitats.

A large, slender silurid catfish from the Indian subcontinent, valued as a food fish and kept in the aquarium trade as juveniles. Reaches 45 cm and requires a very large aquarium. Fairly peaceful and shy for its size, but will consume any fish small enough to fit in its mouth. Readily accepts most live and frozen foods; larger individuals take earthworms, prawn pieces, and pellets.

A tiny torrent catfish from fast-flowing mountain streams of India and Bangladesh, reaching only 3–4 cm. Flattened body with adhesive texture lets it cling to rocks in strong current. Peaceful and best kept in a dedicated species or nano biotope setup with slow-to-medium flow and no large or boisterous tankmates. Feed live or frozen bloodworm and daphnia after lights out.
behavior: Clings to smooth rocks and wood using its flattened undersurface. Prefers moderate to strong flow and well-oxygenated water reflecting its torrent-stream habitat. Nocturnal feeder.

description: A hardy callichthyid catfish from the Amazon, Orinoco, and Guianan drainages, closely related to Corydoras. Reaches ~15 cm and adapts to a wide range of conditions including oxygen-poor water, using a modified intestine for aerial respiration. Males build elaborate bubble nests for breeding. A peaceful community fish compatible with most tankmates, though males become territorial around active nests.
reproduction: Males build floating bubble nests; females deposit eggs inside. Males aggressively defend the nest until eggs hatch.
dimorphism: In males the leading pectoral-fin ray is thickened and orange to yellow in colour.

description: A large callichthyid catfish known by many local names — tamatá, atipa, hassa, kwi kwi, cascadura — across its range from Venezuela to Argentina. Reaches ~24–26 cm and is highly adaptable to poor-oxygen conditions using air-intestine breathing. Nocturnal omnivore feeding on detritus, insects, and microcrustaceans. Males build floating bubble nests triggered by seasonal rains. Hardy and easy to keep; introduced invasively in Florida.

A slender callichthyid catfish from the Amazon Basin named for the row of dark spots along its lateral line. Reaches ~10 cm and is completely peaceful — suitable for most community tanks with similarly sized fish. Groups of 3–6 are more active and exhibit interesting social behavior. Males build bubble nests for spawning. Accepts sinking pellets, bloodworm, brine shrimp, and chopped earthworms.
reproduction: Reportedly a bubble-nester, though some accounts describe eggs laid in pits excavated from the substrate.
dimorphism: The leading pectoral-fin rays are slightly thickened in males.

description: Similar to the porthole catfish but distinguishable by bold black-and-white striping on the caudal fin — the 'flagtail.' Reaches up to 12.5 cm and is completely peaceful. Groups of 3–6 show the most natural behavior. Males build bubble nests. Prefers slightly warmer water than the porthole catfish. Accepts the same varied diet of sinking foods and frozen invertebrates.

A venomous South Asian catfish whose pectoral spines carry a toxic secretion capable of causing severe, long-lasting pain — handle with extreme caution. Reaches 30–41 cm and uses a modified air sac to breathe atmospheric air in stagnant conditions. Gregarious; best kept in small groups. Peaceful toward similarly sized fish but will consume anything smaller. Feed meaty foods: earthworms, bloodworm, chopped prawn.
behavior: CAUTION: pectoral spines are venomous and cause severe pain. Use a wet towel or polythene bag when handling — never use a net. Obligate air-breather; must access the surface.

description: A bizarre ambush predator from Borneo, Malaysia, and Indonesia that mimics a dead leaf — mottled brown texture, flattened body, fringed margins. Reaches ~20 cm and is almost entirely immobile, waiting for prey to approach. Primarily requires live small fish and earthworms; weaning onto frozen foods is possible but difficult. Must be kept alone — will consume any fish or shrimp it can fit in its enormous mouth.
behavior: Motionless ambush predator. Wiggles maxillary barbels as a lure to attract prey. Known to produce a clicking sound.

description: One of the largest freshwater fish of Asia, the wallago or 'helicopter catfish' reaches 240 cm and is a supreme apex predator. Clearly unsuitable for any private aquarium. Found from Pakistan to Vietnam and Indonesia, lurking in deep pools and large rivers where it ambushes large fish. Even public aquaria rarely house adults. Adapts to whole fish, trout, and large prawns in captivity; large specimens feed once or twice weekly.

A large, robust loach from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, also sold as the redtail botia; adults develop a blue-grey body with an orange-red tail. Reaches 20–28 cm and must be kept in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens become highly aggressive and destructive. Even in groups it may nip the fins of slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. A heavy, secure lid is essential as it is an adept jumper.
reproduction: Not bred by private aquarists; it is a seasonal migratory spawner in nature and commercial stock is produced using hormones.
dimorphism: Sexually mature females are fuller-bodied and grow a little larger than males.

description: A medium-sized Mekong/Chao Phraya loach with an olive body and reddish fins, also called the silver loach. Reaches 12–17 cm and requires a group of at least 5; individuals kept in small groups direct aggression outward toward tankmates. Active, gregarious, and social within a proper group. Avoid very small or long-finned tankmates. Provide good flow, smooth pebbles, and hiding spots.
reproduction: No reports of breeding in private aquaria, and unlike some relatives it is not commercially hormone-farmed.
dimorphism: Sexually mature females are fuller-bodied and grow a little larger than males.

description: A small, peaceful loach from the Mekong basin with bold black-and-white striping, reaching 7–9 cm. Mild-mannered compared to most botia-type loaches; can be kept in community setups with active fish of similar temperament. Avoid slow-moving or long-finned species. Groups of 5 or more are essential — this is a social species that shows stress when kept in small numbers. Cooler water tolerance (down to 20°C) suits subtropical setups.

A large, impressively patterned loach from Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula, with greenish-gold body crossed by bold dark tiger stripes. Reaches up to 24 cm. Belligerent toward smaller fish and must be kept in groups of 5+ to diffuse intraspecific aggression; solitary specimens terrorize tankmates. Prefers warm, soft, slightly acidic water reflecting its blackwater/peat-swamp origins.

A large, striking loach from India, Myanmar, and central Thailand with bold tiger-stripe patterning and a vivid red tail. Reaches 17–25 cm and is one of the larger Syncrossus species. Belligerent; must be kept in groups of 5+ to reduce aggression toward tankmates. Suitable only for large tanks with robust, similarly sized inhabitants. Avid fin-nipper — avoid slow or long-finned species.

A vividly patterned loach from northern India and Bangladesh with alternating dark and pale vertical bands on an orange-gold body. Reaches 12–15 cm and must be kept in groups of 5 or more; solitary specimens become withdrawn or redirect aggression toward similarly-shaped fish. An active fin-nipper — avoid slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Prefers clean, well-oxygenated water with moderate flow.
reproduction: Not bred by hobbyists, as with most botiid loaches, though it is produced commercially using hormones.
dimorphism: Sexually mature females are normally fuller-bodied than males.

description: A striking loach from Myanmar and northeastern India with golden-yellow and dark wavy-stripe patterning that varies between river populations. Reaches 11–13 cm and requires groups of at least 5; individually kept fish become withdrawn or aggressive toward similar-shaped species. Not especially aggressive in a proper group but may nip fins of slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Appreciates clean, well-oxygenated water with smooth pebbles.
reproduction: Not known to have been bred by hobbyists; commercial stock is produced using hormones, and hybrids have appeared on the market.
dimorphism: Females are fuller-bodied with a rounded snout, while males have an elongated snout with noticeably fleshier lips.

description: A loach from the Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages with distinctive ladder-like dark saddle markings on a pale body. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~13 cm but wild fish can approach 25 cm; plan tank size accordingly. Requires groups of 5 or more — solitary fish become withdrawn or aggressive toward similar species. A cool-water tolerance down to 19°C makes it suitable for subtropical setups.

A uniformly dark, eel-like loach from the blackwater swamps and forest streams of Java, Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula, reaching 7–9 cm. Fully peaceful and suitable for community tanks with non-nippy fish. Groups of at least 5 are essential — solitary specimens spend virtually all their time hidden. Soft, acidic water with fine sandy substrate and leaf-litter cover best replicates its natural environment.
reproduction: Rarely bred in aquaria, with at least one uncontrolled account of fry simply turning up among the substrate.
dimorphism: Females are typically heavier-bodied and a little larger, while in mature males the first pectoral-fin ray is branched and thickened.

description: The largest of the kuhli loaches, this eel-like species from the Mekong basin reaches 9–12 cm. Fully peaceful and thrives in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens remain hidden indefinitely. Inhabits slow-moving blackwater forest streams and swamps; soft, acidic water with fine substrate and ample cover replicates its natural environment. Can be kept alongside other peaceful community fish.
reproduction: Breeding is essentially unrecorded in the aquarium hobby.
dimorphism: Females are heavier-bodied and a little larger, while in mature males the first pectoral-fin ray is branched and thickened.

description: An eel-like loach from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with dark banding and pale undersides, reaching 7–9 cm. Fully peaceful and thrives in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens hide indefinitely. Prefers soft, acidic blackwater conditions with a fine sandy substrate, leaf litter, and gentle filtration.

An eel-like loach from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with alternating dark and pale half-bands along the upper body, reaching up to 12 cm. Fully peaceful and gregarious; groups of 5 or more are essential as solitary fish hide continually. Prefers soft, acidic blackwater conditions with fine sandy substrate, leaf litter, and dim lighting.

A slender, eel-like loach from the Ganges and Brahmaputra river systems of northern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh that sifts sandy substrates for invertebrates. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~9 cm; wild fish can approach 15 cm. Peaceful with all tankmates. Groups of 4 or more reduce shyness. Tolerates slightly cooler water (to 20°C), consistent with its subtropical distribution.

A beautifully marked loach from the river systems of South and Southeast Asia, with intricate dark-spotted patterning on a pale body; typical aquarium specimens reach ~10 cm but wild fish can approach 15 cm. Peaceful and non-aggressive; mixes well with other substrate-dwellers. Groups of 6 or more reduce timidity. Prefers clean, well-oxygenated water with rocky or sandy substrate and moderate current.

A small, stout loach from the Tenasserim river system of Myanmar and western Thailand with bold dark banding on a pale body, reaching 6–8 cm. Less aggressive than many Schistura species but still territorial; groups need ample rocky hiding spots and broken sight lines to reduce skirmishes. Not suited to general community tanks — best kept with robust, similarly sized bottom-dwellers.

A stunning hillstream loach from fast-flowing streams in Vietnam and Laos with an intricate reticulated pattern of pale lines on a dark body. Reaches 5–7 cm. Territorial toward its own kind and similarly-shaped species; a group of 6 or more helps establish a hierarchy and display natural behavior. Requires high flow, excellent oxygenation, and smooth boulders to graze; standard aquarium conditions will not suffice.
reproduction: Among the easiest loaches to breed in aquaria; the eggs develop tucked away in crevices between rounded river gravel.
dimorphism: Males have a squarer snout and rows of soft raised tubercles on the first few pectoral-fin rays, while females are broader-bodied with the snout running almost continuous with the pectoral fins.

description: A small hillstream loach from the fast-flowing rocky streams of Borneo with a pale body covered in dark spots. Reaches 6–8 cm. Peaceful in groups of 5 or more and safe with most non-territorial community fish. Requires very high current, strong oxygenation, smooth rocks for grazing, and regular access to biofilm; unsuitable for standard still-water setups.

A flattened, butterfly-shaped hillstream loach from fast-flowing rivers in southern China, reaching ~6 cm. Peaceful and suitable for species-specific hillstream setups or community tanks with similarly-sized, current-loving fish. Prefers slightly harder, neutral to mildly alkaline water reflecting its Chinese river origins. Requires very strong flow, excellent oxygenation, and biofilm-covered rocks to graze.

A striking hillstream loach endemic to headwater streams in Guangxi, China, with bold black-and-white panda-like patterning. Reaches 5–7 cm. Relatively peaceful but somewhat territorial within its own kind; groups of 4 or more show more natural behavior. Juveniles display vivid coloration that may fade with age. Requires very strong flow, cool water (20–24°C), excellent oxygenation, and biofilm-covered surfaces.

A large hillstream loach endemic to fast-flowing streams in Borneo with bold saddle-like dark markings on a pale body. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~10 cm; wild fish can approach 14 cm. Peaceful; bolder in groups of 6 or more. Requires very strong current, high oxygen levels, biofilm-covered rocks, and supplemental live or frozen invertebrates — a demanding species that rewards dedicated hillstream setups.

A colourful Australian gudgeon with a pale body peppered with purple-blue and orange spots. Typical specimens reach ~7 cm, but wild fish can grow to 14 cm; plan for the larger adult. Will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow — restrict companions to robust, similarly-sized fish such as rainbowfish. Can be kept singly or as a pair. Currently considered endangered in the wild, most trade specimens are captive-bred.
reproduction: Eggs are laid on a flat surface and tended almost constantly by the male, who fans and defends them until they hatch in about 5-7 days.
dimorphism: Males have a clearly pointed, often brightly coloured genital papilla and may develop a nuchal hump and brighter spawning colours, while females have a shorter, wider ovipositor.

description: A spectacularly coloured Australian gudgeon from northern and eastern river systems with electric-blue, orange, and red markings on males during breeding. Reaches 10–13 cm. Highly territorial between males; best kept singly or as a pair in a well-decorated tank with broken sight lines. Adapts to an unusually wide range of water conditions (pH 5–9, 20–30°C). Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved as larvae require a marine developmental stage.

A spectacular, jewel-coloured goby endemic to hill streams in Sumatra, where males display brilliant neon iridescence. Reaches 6–8 cm. Males are loosely territorial but several can coexist with adequate space. A dedicated biofilm grazer; the tank must be well-established with abundant algae on smooth rocks and require strong flow. Not suited to standard community setups or newly established aquariums.

A small, brilliantly coloured goby from clear hill streams across Indonesia, the Philippines, and the Solomon Islands. Males display vivid red-orange and blue iridescence; females are drab. Reaches 4–6 cm. Requires a mature tank with abundant biofilm on rocks and strong current. Males are territorial toward each other; keep as one male with two or more females. Breeding in captivity requires marine conditions for larval development.

A tiny, stunning goby from clear hill streams in Japan, Taiwan, the Philippines, and southern China, with males displaying intense blue-purple iridescence. Reaches 4–6 cm. A specialist biofilm grazer requiring a mature, well-established tank with abundant algae on smooth rocks and strong flow. Keep as one male with two or more females. Multiple males can coexist in larger, well-structured setups.

A small, stocky goby from cool, fast-flowing streams in southern China and Vietnam with mottled brown-and-white patterning and distinctive pale cheeks. Reaches ~7 cm. Males are territorial toward each other but peaceful toward dissimilar-shaped tankmates. Prefers cool, well-oxygenated, neutral to slightly hard water; suitable for unheated setups alongside temperate fish like danios and white cloud minnows.

A minute, brilliantly coloured goby endemic to a handful of streams near Lianhua Mountain in Guangdong Province, China; males display striking scarlet-orange markings. Reaches only ~4 cm. IUCN Endangered in the wild — ensure any specimens are captive-bred. Males are territorial but tolerate each other with adequate cover; keep at least two males plus females. Requires cool, clear, well-oxygenated water with a varied live/frozen diet.

A beautiful non-annual killifish from coastal forest streams of Equatorial Guinea and Gabon with a yellow-green body bearing rows of vivid red dots and a lyre-shaped tail. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and relatively undemanding; a pair or trio (one male, two females) can be kept in a small, heavily planted tank. An accomplished jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water.
reproduction: A non-annual species that deposits its eggs in small daily batches among fine plants or the substrate over roughly two weeks.
dimorphism: Adult males are larger and more intensely coloured with longer caudal, dorsal and anal fins than females.

description: A compact, vividly coloured killifish from coastal forest streams in Togo, Benin, and southern Nigeria with two bold dark lateral stripes and iridescent blue-green flanks on males. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful and easy to breed; a pair or small harem does well in a dedicated species tank with floating plants and leaf litter. Sensitive to water quality — keep nitrates low with frequent small water changes. A tight lid is essential.

A striking non-annual killifish from the middle and lower Congo basin with a greenish body densely spotted with vivid red dots and an elaborately patterned tail. Reaches 4–5 cm. Peaceful and beginner-friendly for a killifish; a pair or harem thrives in a well-planted 40 L tank. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and a diet of small live or frozen invertebrates. A tight-fitting lid is essential to prevent escapes.

A compact, colourful non-annual killifish from swamps and forest streams in Benin, Nigeria, and Cameroon. Males are brilliantly marked with blue-green flanks, red spotting, and a banner-like dorsal fin. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful; keep as a pair or trio in a species tank with floating plants and leaf litter. Prefers slightly acidic, soft water. A tight lid is mandatory — this species is an exceptional jumper.

A spectacularly patterned non-annual killifish from forest streams in southeastern Nigeria, Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, and northwestern Gabon. Males display an astonishing mosaic of red, blue, and green markings. Reaches 5–6 cm. Shy and easily intimidated; best kept in a species tank. Pair spawning on fine-leaved plants is reliable. A tight-fitting lid is essential as the species jumps readily.

A vivid non-annual killifish from coastal swamps and forest brooks of Gabon with males displaying rich blue-green iridescence and red spotting. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful and suitable for a small species tank with floating plants, peat substrate, and leaf litter. Prefers slightly acidic, soft water with moderate temperatures. A tight-fitting lid is essential — killifish of this genus are accomplished jumpers.

A cool-water non-annual killifish from inland plateau streams of Gabon and the Republic of Congo with males bearing bold broken-line red patterning on blue-green flanks. Reaches 4–5 cm. Notably cooler temperature requirements (18–22°C) than most Aphyosemion. Peaceful and best maintained as a pair or trio in a species tank with acidic, very soft water and peat filtration. A secure lid is essential.

A peaceful, shy killifish from forest streams of the lower Cross River system in southeastern Nigeria and Cameroon. Males are brilliantly coloured with blue, red, and yellow markings and an elaborate lyre-shaped tail. Reaches ~6 cm. Best kept in a species or species-heavy tank away from competitive feeders. Accepts high-quality flake but thrives on live or frozen invertebrates. Secure the tank — this species jumps.
dimorphism: Males are considerably more brightly coloured than females.

description: One of the larger Fundulopanchax killifish, endemic to the lower Sanaga basin in western Cameroon. Males are spectacularly marked with vivid blue, red, and orange iridescence on an olive-brown body. Reaches ~7 cm. Can be kept as a pair or trio; males may quarrel so provide ample hiding spots. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water. A non-annual species that is considered vulnerable in the wild; most available specimens are captive-bred.

A delicate, feather-finned killifish from coastal swamps and brooks in Togo, Benin, and southwestern Nigeria. Males carry elongated, filamentous fin extensions that give the species its common name. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and easy to breed; a pair or trio thrives in a species tank with floating plants, leaf litter, and peat filtration. Keep temperatures cool (21–23°C) and a tight lid fitted at all times.

A colourful but aggressive killifish from forest streams in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Males display intricate blue, red, and yellow markings. Reaches 6–7 cm. Despite its modest size, it is predatory toward any fish that fits in its mouth and quite aggressive for a killifish; best kept in a species-only setup as a pair or trio. Prefers cooler water (20–23°C) — excess heat reduces lifespan significantly. A secure lid is essential.

A tiny annual killifish endemic to the seasonal pools of Unguja Island (Zanzibar), Tanzania. Males are brilliantly coloured with vivid red, orange, and blue markings. Reaches 3–4 cm. An annual species with a lifespan of less than a year in the wild; in captivity specimens can live 1–2 years with stable conditions. Males quarrel — keep one male with two or more females. Eggs must be air-dried in peat for ~3 months to hatch.
reproduction: An annual killifish: pairs push into the soft substrate to lay single eggs that survive the dry season in diapause and hatch when the rains return.

description: An annual killifish from the seasonal floodplains of the lower Rufiji River delta in Tanzania. Males are richly coloured in red and blue. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and manageable in captivity; a pair can be kept in a 40 L tank with peat substrate for egg collection. As an annual species its lifespan is naturally short (1–2 years); eggs require dry storage for several months before hatching. Prefers hard, alkaline water reflecting its East African origins.

An annual killifish endemic to the Mafia Islands off the coast of Tanzania. Males display vivid yellow-orange and red patterning with intricate spotting. Reaches ~5 cm. An annual species with a natural lifespan under one year; eggs require dry peat storage for 2–4 months before hatching. Males compete — keep as one male with multiple females. Notably prefers unusually acidic water (pH 5.8–6.4) compared to most East African killifish.

A vivid annual killifish from coastal seasonal pools in Kenya, Somalia, and Ethiopia. Males display brilliant blue and red coloration. Reaches ~5 cm. An annual species requiring egg diapause in dry peat for 2–3 months; eggs should be checked and incubated at high humidity. Males fight — one male to several females is the recommended setup. Prefers slightly acidic to neutral water with peat filtration.

An annual killifish from the temporary pools and swamps of central Mozambique. Males are brilliantly coloured in red, orange, and blue. Reaches 4–5 cm. Inhabits pools that can reach extreme temperatures in the dry season — in captivity, maintain at 24–28°C to replicate warm tropical conditions. An annual species requiring egg diapause; eggs are stored in moist peat for several months before hatching. Males compete aggressively.

An annual killifish from the river drainages of eastern Tanzania (Ruvu, Mpiji, and lower Rufuji). Males reach ~6 cm with vivid coloration; females are smaller (~4 cm) and cryptically coloured. IUCN Vulnerable in the wild. A relatively straightforward Nothobranchius to keep; a pair does well at 20–24°C with peat substrate. Eggs incubate dry for 2–4 months. Males compete — keep one male with two or more females.

An annual killifish from the coastal plains of Kenya and Tanzania with males bearing red-tipped scales on a blue-green body. Reaches ~5 cm. One of the cooler-water Nothobranchius (18–22°C); suitable for an unheated room-temperature setup in temperate climates. An annual species with a very short natural lifespan; eggs require dry peat storage for several months before re-hydration. Males are aggressive toward each other.

An annual killifish from the Zanzibar Archipelago and adjacent coastal mainland rivers of Tanzania and Kenya. Males display black-spotted iridescent patterning. Reaches ~6 cm. An annual species; eggs require dry peat storage for 2–3 months before hatching. Males compete — keep one male with two or more females. Slightly wider temperature tolerance (22–28°C) than some Nothobranchius. A secure lid is essential.

A popular surface-dwelling killifish from coastal streams in Liberia, Côte d'Ivoire, and Ghana. Males have a vivid red chin; bodies are olive with fine horizontal dotted striping. Reaches ~6 cm. Will eat any fish small enough to swallow — avoid small nano fish as tankmates. Otherwise peaceful and easy to keep. Prefers slightly cool water (21–23°C) and a well-planted tank with floating cover. A tight lid is mandatory.

One of the larger Epiplatys panchax, with a greenish-gold body bearing six bold dark vertical bars. Wild fish approach 12 cm; typical aquarium specimens are 8 cm. Groups of 6 or more show more natural behavior. Will predate very small fish and invertebrates but otherwise peaceful in a community with similar-sized inhabitants. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and a well-planted setup with floating plants. A secure lid is essential.
reproduction: Relatively easy to breed; the eggs are deposited among fine plants or mosses and incubate for about 12-14 days before the fry appear near the surface.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful, longer-finned and larger than females.

description: A widespread surface-hunting killifish found from Pakistan and India through Southeast Asia and into Sri Lanka. The typical aquarium form is ~5 cm but wild fish can reach 9 cm. A voracious surface predator that will eat any fish, shrimp, or fry that fits in its mouth — mix only with fish too large to be preyed upon. Hardy and adaptable across a wide pH range. A tight lid is essential; this species jumps readily.

A large, brilliantly coloured surface-hunting killifish endemic to Sri Lanka. Males display striking iridescent green-blue scales; females are more cryptically coloured. Reaches up to 10 cm. IUCN Endangered in the wild — ensure specimens are captive-bred. Predatory; will eat any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth. Best kept with fish of comparable size. Requires a heavily planted tank and a secure, tight-fitting lid.

A small, attractively patterned catfish from the blackwater rivers and peat swamps of Sumatra, recognizable by a dark spot on the shoulder and a second on the caudal peduncle. Peaceful with fish of all sizes. Requires warm, very soft, and acidic water that mirrors its natural peat-swamp habitat.
behavior: Primarily nocturnal and prefers dense cover or driftwood. Peaceful toward all other species and unlikely to bother even very small tankmates.

description: A popular West African killifish prized for the vivid iridescent blue-green gleam around its eyes that gave it the 'lampeye' name. Hardy and peaceful, it suits planted nano tanks and thrives in groups of six or more. Readily accepts Artemia, Daphnia, and small frozen or flake foods at the surface.
behavior: Active shoaling species that spends most of its time near the surface. Best kept in groups of at least 6 to feel secure and display its best colors.

description: A spectacular lampeye killifish endemic to just two river systems in southwestern Cameroon. Males reach 6 cm and display striking iridescent eyes and vivid fins; females are considerably smaller at around 4 cm. Shy and easily outcompeted, it requires pristine, cool, soft-to-neutral water and a diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Rarely encountered in the trade.
dimorphism: Males grow to 6 cm and are more colorful with iridescent finnage; females are much smaller at around 4 cm.

description: A stunning annual killifish from seasonal coastal pools in southeastern Brazil, critically endangered in the wild. Males display rows of vivid pearlescent spots and striking red-blue fins. In captivity, eggs are collected and stored through a dry diapause period before re-wetting triggers hatching. Best kept as a pair or small trio and not mixed with aggressive species.
behavior: Annual species that completes its lifecycle in seasonal pools that dry periodically. Males are aggressive toward each other and should not be housed together.
reproduction: Egg-burying annual killifish; eggs require a dry diapause of several months before re-wetting triggers hatching.
dimorphism: Males are brightly colored with pearlescent spots and vivid fins; females are more cryptically patterned and slightly smaller.

description: A robust killifish endemic to the granitic Seychelles islands and the only freshwater killifish native to those islands. Males display golden-yellow scales with red-trimmed fins. A surface-hunting predator that will consume fish small enough to swallow — keep only with species of similar or greater size. Hardy and tolerant of a wide pH range, but a secure lid is essential as it jumps readily.
behavior: Active surface swimmer. Males are territorial with each other; best kept as a single male with females or one specimen per tank.
reproduction: Egg scatterer that deposits eggs among floating plants. Eggs hatch in around two weeks.
dimorphism: Males are more colorful with vivid golden-yellow flanks and red fin margins; females are smaller and more cryptically colored.

description: A vibrant rainbowfish endemic to a single highland lake in Papua New Guinea and listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Peaceful and best kept in schools of 6 or more. Wild specimens can reach 15 cm but aquarium-grown fish typically stay closer to 9 cm. Requires stable, alkaline water and ample open swimming space alongside dense plantings.
behavior: Active mid-water schooler. Males display vivid colors and spar harmlessly during courtship, but no injury results.

description: A small, active rainbowfish from northeastern Australia and Papua New Guinea, rarely exceeding 7 cm. Males develop striking iridescent coloration when mature and in prime condition. Best kept in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted aquarium with gentle flow. Water chemistry varies by population, so match parameters to the collection locality.
reproduction: A continuous spawner that lays eggs over several weeks, each attached to fine plants by a short thread.
dimorphism: Mature males are more colourful with longer dorsal and anal fins, while females are deeper-bodied.

description: A hardy, adaptable rainbowfish native to the Murray-Darling basin of southeastern Australia. Males display silvery-blue and yellow coloration and can reach 11 cm; females are smaller and duller. Unusually tolerant of cooler temperatures. Active and fast-moving — avoid housing with very small or slow-moving tankmates. Keep in shoals of 6 or more.
reproduction: The pair spawns over several weeks, laying batches of eggs each day that are attached to plants and surfaces by a fine thread.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful and develop the genus's characteristic high back with maturity.

description: A large, active rainbowfish from New Guinea rivers including the Markham, Ramu, and Sepik systems. Multiple geographic variants with different coloration are available in the hobby. Peaceful but boisterous — best suited to spacious tanks with other robust fish. Requires good filtration and open swimming space alongside planted areas.
reproduction: The pair spawns over several weeks, laying a few eggs each day that hatch in 7-12 days depending on temperature.
dimorphism: Mature males are larger, brighter and deeper-bodied with elongated dorsal and anal fins.

description: A large, seldom-seen rainbowfish from swamps, backwaters, and rivers of southern New Guinea. Males develop impressive coloration with age. Requires warm, neutral to slightly alkaline water and a long tank with open swimming space. Peaceful with similarly sized fish; keep in groups of 6 or more.

A medium-sized rainbowfish from tropical northern Australia, found in rainforest streams, lily lagoons, and slow-flowing coastal waterways. Males show dark banding along the body and vivid fin coloration. Prefers softer, slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted tank; popular in Australian aquarium circles.

A robust rainbowfish from the Kimberley and Pilbara regions of northwestern Australia, tolerating a wide range of habitats from rivers and creeks to swamps and reservoirs. Males develop vivid coloration that takes over 12 months to fully mature. Active and boisterous; keep in groups of 6 or more alongside similarly sized fish. Requires a spacious, well-filtered tank.

A critically endangered rainbowfish endemic to Lake Wanam in Papua New Guinea, now virtually extinct in the wild due to invasive tilapia. Captive populations are maintained by enthusiasts and are the only source for the hobby. Males develop stunning emerald-green iridescence. Requires warm, alkaline water; will not thrive in soft or acidic conditions. A conservation priority species.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to Lake Holmes in West Papua, Indonesia. Males are impressively large and colorful, developing vivid fin extensions with age. Requires neutral to alkaline, moderately hard water. A specialist species seldom seen outside dedicated rainbowfish collections; keep in groups of 6 or more in a large, well-maintained aquarium.

A critically endangered micro blue-eye endemic to a few rainforest streams near Kiunga in Papua New Guinea's upper Fly River system. Males display ornate elongated fin rays and vivid markings. Extremely rare in the hobby; best kept as a species-only tank with live micro-foods. Requires stable, cool, neutral water and densely planted conditions.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to the Mananjary River basin of eastern Madagascar, often confused in the trade with the related Bedotia madagascariensis. Males develop a characteristic red tail. Prefers cool, clean, alkaline water with moderate hardness; sensitive to pollution. Best kept in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted aquarium with good filtration.

An elegant rainbowfish from a small area near Maros, Sulawesi, Indonesia, distinguished by elongated dorsal and anal fin rays in males. Vulnerable in the wild. Requires neutral to alkaline, moderately hard water — will not thrive in acidic conditions. Very sensitive to water quality swings. Best kept as a shoal of 6+ with a 2-3:1 female-to-male ratio.
reproduction: The pair spawns over several weeks, laying small batches of eggs each day among fine plants.
dimorphism: Males are the more colourful sex and develop beautiful extensions to the second dorsal and anal fins.

description: A delicate rainbowfish from coastal wallum wetlands of southeastern Queensland and northern New South Wales, Australia. Males are notably larger and more colorful than females. Inhabits tannin-stained, acidic, slow-moving water with abundant vegetation — replicating these conditions in captivity is essential. Rarely seen outside Australian native fish enthusiast circles.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to a small number of fast-flowing rainforest streams in the Wet Tropics of northeastern Queensland, Australia. Monotypic — the sole species in its genus. Requires cool, well-oxygenated, neutral to slightly alkaline water with strong flow. Rarely available in the hobby; best suited to experienced keepers with appropriate setups.

A large, popular livebearer from coastal marshes and estuaries of the southeastern US and Mexico. Males develop an impressive dorsal fin used in courtship displays. Requires hard, alkaline water with vegetable matter in the diet; will decline in soft or acidic conditions. Males pursue females relentlessly — keep more females than males. Tolerates brackish water.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 10–140 fry every 3–6 weeks. Males relentlessly pursue females — maintain at least 2 females per male.
dimorphism: Males have a gonopodium and a large, long, sail-like dorsal fin, while females are larger and lack these features.

description: The largest of the mollies, native to the Yucatan Peninsula. Males develop an enormous sail-like dorsal fin and reach 15 cm. More demanding than the common sailfin molly — requires warm, hard, alkaline water and a large tank. Provide plenty of algae and vegetable matter. Does not require salt but tolerates brackish conditions.
reproduction: Livebearer; females store sperm and can produce multiple broods from a single mating.

description: A hardy, widespread livebearer from rivers, springs, and estuaries of Mexico and Central America — the wild ancestor of many hybrid molly varieties sold in the hobby. Feeds primarily on detritus, algae, and plant matter. Peaceful and adaptable; tolerates a range of water conditions including brackish. Unique cave-adapted populations also exist.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 30–80 fry approximately 28 days after fertilization.

description: A biologically unique all-female livebearer from southern Texas and northeastern Mexico that reproduces through gynogenesis — females require sperm from a related male molly species to trigger reproduction, but incorporate none of the male's DNA. Rarely sold commercially; mostly of interest to researchers and specialists. Peaceful and undemanding to keep.
reproduction: All-female species; reproduces gynogenetically using sperm from males of related Poecilia species as a developmental trigger without male genetic contribution.

description: A tiny, jewel-like blue-eye from Australia, southern New Guinea, and the Aru Islands, rarely exceeding 4 cm. Males have elaborate elongated fins tipped with vivid markings and display constantly to one another. Too easily outcompeted for most community tanks; best in a species-only or carefully chosen micro-tank setup. Requires live or very small foods and densely planted conditions.
reproduction: A continuous spawner: the female lays a few eggs daily over several days, each attached to fine plants or spawning mops by adhesive filaments.
dimorphism: Males are more highly patterned and colourful, with the unpaired fins becoming noticeably extended as they mature.

description: A variable blue-eye endemic to eastern Australia, ranging from small southern forms of around 3 cm to large northern males reaching 7 cm or more. Adapts readily to captivity and breeds freely in planted aquaria. Males can be intolerant of each other when spawning. Prefers live or frozen foods but accepts quality dried foods. Best kept in groups of 8 or more.
reproduction: A continuous spawner: the female deposits a few eggs daily over several days, attached to vegetation, spawning throughout daylight in warm conditions.
dimorphism: Males are more highly patterned, more colourful and larger, with noticeably extended unpaired fins at maturity.

description: An endangered blue-eye from coastal wallum heathland habitats of southern Queensland, Australia, found in tannin-stained, acidic slow-moving waters. Males develop a warm golden-amber tone with decorated fins. Requires soft, acidic water; will struggle in hard or alkaline tanks. A conservation priority species best suited to a carefully managed nano or species tank.

A small, peaceful blue-eye from sunlit swamps, billabongs, and slow streams of northern Australia and New Guinea. Males have elegantly elongated fin rays and display vivid coloration. Prefers warm water with dense surface vegetation. Best kept as a species tank or with other small, non-competitive fish. Feed live or frozen micro-foods for best results.

A pike-shaped, highly predatory livebearer from weedy canals, lagoons, and marshes from Mexico to Nicaragua. Females are much larger than males and will eat fish approaching their own size. Must be kept with tankmates too large to swallow. Tolerates a wide range of temperatures and even brackish water. Not for community tanks with smaller fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to small broods of 10–30 large fry.

description: A hardy, colorful platy from warm springs and slow-moving waters of northeastern Mexico, noted for highly variable color patterns that have driven extensive selective breeding. Tolerates cooler temperatures than most other livebearers. Peaceful and undemanding; thrives in hard, alkaline water with a planted setup. An excellent beginner fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 10–50 fry every 4–6 weeks.

description: A distinctive wild swordtail from northeastern Mexico, notable for its horizontally-extended sword (unlike the downward-angled sword of most swordtails) and metallic green coloration. Primarily kept by livebearer enthusiasts rather than the mainstream hobby. Requires hard, alkaline water. Males can harass females — maintain more females than males.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to small broods of live fry.

description: The smallest swordtail species, endemic to the Rio Axtla in northeastern Mexico. Lacks the typical sword of larger swordtails. Requires cool, hard water with excellent filtration and frequent water changes — more delicate than common swordtails. Feeds on algae and plant material among dense vegetation. Primarily available through specialist livebearer breeders.
reproduction: Livebearer; females produce small broods of rarely more than 10 fry.

description: A tiny, rarely seen livebearer endemic to the Soto La Marina River system in Tamaulipas, Mexico, inhabiting springs, streams, and slow-moving ponds. Named for the spike-like extension of the tail fin in males. Peaceful and suitable for a small, carefully managed tank. Prefers cool, hard water and dense aquatic vegetation. Mainly found with specialist breeders.
reproduction: Livebearer; gestation is 24–35 days with 20–50 fry per brood.

description: A small, hardy livebearer endemic to fast-flowing, hard-water streams in Jamaica. Males display attractive dark barring and are notably less cannibalistic toward fry than many livebearers. Peaceful and easy to keep; does best in hard, alkaline water with dense planting. An excellent choice for a small livebearer species tank or Caribbean biotope.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 20–80 fry approximately every 4 weeks.
dimorphism: Males are smaller than females and possess a gonopodium.

description: A relatively large limia endemic to southeastern Hispaniola, tolerant of an unusually wide range of salinities from clear springs to hypersaline coastal lagoons. Males develop vivid blue and yellow coloration and establish social hierarchies with competitive displays. Better suited to experienced livebearer keepers than absolute beginners; requires hard or brackish water and a well-structured tank.
reproduction: Livebearer; produces numerous fry with no parental care.

description: The largest of the commonly kept limias, endemic to Cuba where it inhabits a wide variety of habitats including streams, lakes, estuaries, and mangrove swamps. Females are substantially larger than males. Tolerates cooler temperatures than most tropical livebearers; thrives in hard, alkaline or slightly brackish water. Peaceful and compatible with other robust hard-water fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to live fry in regular broods.

description: A small, attractive Cuban livebearer with a distinctive black stripe running along the male's underside. Females are notably larger than the slender males. Prefers medium-hard, slightly alkaline water and appreciates a well-planted tank with floating cover. Omnivorous; requires vegetable matter in the diet. Best kept as a species tank or with other hard-water tolerant small fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; gestation is approximately 24 days producing 15–30 fry.
dimorphism: Males are smaller, with a gonopodium and a black stripe running from the mouth along the underside into the gonopodium, while females are larger.

description: One of the world's smallest fish and the smallest North American livebearer, endemic to heavily vegetated, slow-moving waters of the southeastern United States. Uses superfoetation — continuously birthing single fry rather than discrete broods. Requires very gentle filtration, dense planting, and tankmates of similar size. Easy to keep but not suitable for typical community tanks due to its tiny size.
reproduction: Livebearer with superfoetation; births 1–5 fry continuously over 10–14 days per cycle.
dimorphism: Males are much smaller than females and possess a proportionately enormous gonopodium.

description: A hardy, peaceful livebearer from Atlantic drainages of Guatemala through Panama, inhabiting swampy, vegetation-rich creeks and slow streams. Females are notably larger than the small, slender males. Feeds primarily on detritus, diatoms, and fine organic matter. Easy to keep and breed; a good alternative to the common guppy for soft-substrate, planted setups.
reproduction: Livebearer; females bear 10–80 fry after approximately 4 weeks gestation.

description: An insectivorous livebearer from Pacific-slope streams of Costa Rica and Panama, known for attractive coloration and feisty behavior. Adults are highly cannibalistic toward their own fry, making breeding challenging without separation. Best kept as a species tank or with robust, similarly sized fish. Prefers live or frozen invertebrate foods alongside dry diets.
reproduction: Livebearer; adults must be separated from fry immediately due to cannibalism.

description: A striking goodeid livebearer endemic to the Ameca River drainage in Jalisco, Mexico, now critically endangered in the wild. Males display a vivid yellow-tipped tail. Voracious omnivore that will consume plant material, so use robust vegetation. Active and competitive; may nip fins of slower tankmates. An important species for conservation-minded aquarists, with captive populations preserving its survival.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished via trophotaenia and born fully formed in broods of 10–30.
dimorphism: Males are smaller and more colourful with a black-and-yellow band on the tail and a notched anal fin (andropodium), while females are larger and plainer.

description: A boldly colored goodeid livebearer from the Grande de Santiago River basin in western Mexico, listed as Endangered in the wild. Males develop a vivid orange-red caudal peduncle. Tolerates a wide temperature range and is relatively undemanding, but may occasionally nip fins — this is reduced when kept in groups. Requires vegetable matter in the diet; an important species for goodeid conservation programs.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally and born fully developed.
dimorphism: Males are far more colourful with a higher back and a notched anal fin (andropodium), while females are larger and plainer.

description: The most widespread goodeid, distributed across nine Mexican states from highland lakes and rivers. Larger and more robust than most goodeids, with males displaying dark fin margins. Prefers cool, hard, alkaline water — unsuitable for typical tropical setups. Feeds on algae, plant matter, and invertebrates. A hardy, long-lived goodeid with Least Concern conservation status, unlike many of its relatives.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished via trophotaenia and born in moderate-sized broods.

description: A tiny goodeid livebearer from the Río Teuchitlán in Jalisco, Mexico, now Extinct in the Wild since 1996 due to competition from introduced platies. Survival depends entirely on captive breeding programs by dedicated goodeid enthusiasts. Grazes on benthic diatoms and algae; prefers a planted tank. Keeping this species is a genuine conservation act.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally via trophotaenia.

description: A critically endangered goodeid from shallow springs and marshes in Durango and Coahuila, Mexico. Males display attractive multi-colored flanks. Primarily herbivorous; feeds on algae and aquatic vegetation in heavily planted tanks. Threatened in the wild by invasive species and habitat loss — captive populations kept by specialist breeders are vital to the species' survival.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally and born fully formed.

description: A surface-dwelling livebearer from the fast-flowing mountain streams of South Sulawesi, featuring the characteristic elongated lower jaw of halfbeaks. Feeds primarily on flying insects at the surface; accepts floating dried foods but thrives on live or frozen invertebrates. Males may be aggressive toward each other; keep as a trio or more females than males. Requires a tightly covered tank as it jumps readily.
reproduction: Livebearer; females produce small broods of 5–20 large, well-developed fry.

description: A slender, surface-cruising halfbeak from lowland forest streams, rivers, and swamps of southern Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. Feeds primarily on invertebrates at or above the water surface. Prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water with abundant floating cover. Males may spar; keep with ample space. A tight-fitting lid is essential as this species jumps.
reproduction: Livebearer; produces small broods of large fry after extended gestation.

description: A remarkably tough, adaptable livebearer native to the central and eastern United States, widely and controversially introduced worldwide for mosquito control. Highly aggressive toward other fish — notorious for fin-nipping and harassing tankmates far larger than itself. Not recommended for community tanks. Hardy across an exceptional temperature range. Considered an invasive pest in many countries outside its native range.
reproduction: Livebearer; females bear 50–200 fry multiple times per season after a single mating.

description: A delicate, slow-moving beauty from blackwater peat swamps of Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. Requires very soft, highly acidic water — arguably the most demanding of the commonly sold gouramis. Feeds primarily on small live invertebrates; initially reluctant to accept prepared foods. Unusual among labyrinth fish as a maternal mouthbrooder. Keep in a species tank or with other blackwater specialists; avoid boisterous tankmates.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 30–40 eggs for 2 weeks.
dimorphism: Males have a straight lower jaw and a more pointed head, while females have a slightly rounded jaw with expandable skin used during mouthbrooding.

description: A small chocolate gourami from the blackwater peat swamps of South Kalimantan, Borneo. Distinguished from Sphaerichthys osphromenoides by bold, regularly spaced crossbands. Like its relatives, demands very soft, acidic blackwater conditions and live or frozen invertebrate foods. A paternal mouthbrooder. Best maintained as a species tank or with other blackwater specialists.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood.

description: A cryptic chocolate gourami from small, shaded forest creeks with abundant leaf litter and wood debris in Kalimantan, Borneo. Mimics a dead leaf as camouflage. Like all Sphaerichthys, it demands highly acidic blackwater with negligible hardness and feeds exclusively on small live invertebrates in the wild. Mouthbrooding species; rarely encountered in the hobby.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; incubation by one parent for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A tiny, jewel-like gourami endemic to the blackwater peat swamps of Bangka Island, now Endangered due to habitat loss. Males display spectacular nuptial colors — iridescent blue and red fin margins — during courtship. Requires extremely soft, acidic water with near-zero hardness and very gentle flow. Feeds only on tiny live invertebrates. A specialist species for the dedicated blackwater aquarist.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male guards the bubble nest and mouthbroods the fry.
dimorphism: Courting males show intense fin colour with a whitish-to-bluish margin and a black subdistal band, while females are far plainer and lack the blue and red banding.

description: One of the smallest and most rarely kept licorice gouramis, from blackwater streams of West Kalimantan, Borneo. Tolerates slightly cooler temperatures than most Parosphromenus species. Like all licorice gouramis, demands extremely soft, acidic water and lives only on tiny live foods such as Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, and micro worms. A conservation-priority blackwater specialist.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male mouthbroods or guards the fry in a bubble nest.

description: A tiny licorice gourami from blackwater peat swamp streams in Kalimantan, Borneo. Males display three colour forms — silver, brownish, or red-spotted — and perform elaborate courtship displays. Considered slightly more forgiving than some congeners but still demands very soft, acidic water and a diet of small live invertebrates. Best kept as a species tank or with tiny, non-competitive blackwater cyprinids.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male guards or mouthbroods the small brood.

description: One of the most gentle and easy-going gouramis, endemic to Myanmar. Distinguished by its characteristically thick lips used to pick invertebrates from surface vegetation. Shy and slow-moving; does best in a heavily planted tank with calm tankmates and minimal water flow. Accepts most prepared and frozen foods. A great community gourami for beginners who want something other than the common blue or dwarf gourami.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards nest and fry.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with slightly pointed dorsal and anal fins, while females are plainer and rounder-bodied when gravid.

description: A colorful, moderately sized gourami found across the Indian subcontinent and Myanmar. Males display vivid blue and orange banding and are more assertive than the closely related thick-lipped gourami, with potential inter-male aggression in smaller tanks. Unfussy eater; thrives in planted tanks with floating vegetation and gentle filtration. A rewarding and underappreciated species in the hobby.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards fry.
dimorphism: Males are much more colourful and develop pointed dorsal and anal fins with maturity, while females remain plainer.

description: A large, hardy gourami widely distributed across Southeast Asia and commonly farmed as a food fish. Bears attractive snakeskin-patterned scaling. Primarily herbivorous, grazing heavily on algae, plant material, and phytoplankton. Generally peaceful but its size means it will consume very small tankmates. Tolerant of a wide range of water conditions; one of the most adaptable labyrinth fish.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs a large floating nest.
dimorphism: Males develop pointed dorsal and anal fins with orange or red ventral fins, while females are plumper.

description: A rarely kept, elegant gourami from slow, heavily vegetated waters of India and Bangladesh. Notable for tolerating unusually cool temperatures. Often arrives in poor condition from import; susceptible to ectoparasites. Despite a peaceful disposition overall, adults can be openly hostile toward conspecifics — best kept singly or in a single pair in a well-planted, species-appropriate tank. Feeds on small live invertebrates.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; male broods the eggs and fry.

description: A remarkable ambush predator from blackwater peat swamps with one of the most protrusible jaws of any fish, capable of extending its mouth a third of its body length in a lightning lunge. Primarily eats live fish; very difficult to wean onto dead prey. Requires highly acidic, very soft blackwater with minimal flow. For specialist aquarists only — not compatible with any fish it can swallow.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder in some populations; incubation details vary by locality.

description: A large, aggressive combtail endemic to the rivers and streams of Sri Lanka, recognizable by its spiky, comb-like caudal fin extensions. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish — not suitable for community tanks. Can coexist with robust, schooling cyprinids in a spacious setup. Feeds on invertebrates and molluscs; accepts earthworms and larger frozen foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest aggressively.

description: A large, territorial anabantoid from peat swamps and slow-moving waters of the Malay Peninsula and Greater Sunda Islands. The largest combtail species; requires substantial space and robust tankmates that can defend themselves. Predatory and aggressive — will eat smaller fish and fight with conspecifics. Warmer water than the Sri Lankan combtail; tolerates a wider pH range.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards eggs and fry.

description: A small, strikingly banded African anabantoid from slow forest streams of the Congo Basin, including the Chiloango River. Very shy and easily intimidated; benefits from small dither fish and dense planting to encourage activity. Feeds on worms and aquatic invertebrates; requires live or frozen foods. Spawns nocturnally using a floating bubble nest like its relatives.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; spawns nocturnally among floating plants.
dimorphism: Males are the larger, much more colourful sex and have white edges to the anal and dorsal fins, while females are plainer.

description: A large, predatory African climbing perch from coastal rivers spanning Senegal to the Congo, inhabiting both slow and fast-moving waters. Will consume any fish or invertebrate that fits in its mouth. Patterned body provides excellent camouflage. Relatively hardy and tolerant of varied water parameters for an anabantoid. Best kept as a centrepiece predator with large, robust tankmates.
reproduction: Egg scatterer; pelagic eggs hatch in 24–36 hours with no parental care.

description: A cold-tolerant paradisefish from rice paddies, ponds, and slow streams of China and Korea. Exceptionally hardy — can survive near-freezing temperatures and even remain active when shallow water bodies freeze over. Lacks the elongated tail extensions of the common paradise fish; has a rounded caudal fin. Males are territorial with each other but manageable in a spacious planted tank with robust tankmates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are larger and have extended soft rays in the dorsal, anal and caudal fins.

description: A dark, attractively marked paradisefish endemic to Vietnam, closely related to the common paradise fish but with deeper, more uniform dark pigmentation. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates a wide temperature range. Males are territorial toward each other but similar in behavior to Macropodus opercularis. Does best in a planted tank with plenty of cover and similarly-sized tankmates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A rarely encountered paradisefish endemic to hill streams of the Quang Tri River basin in central Vietnam, inhabiting areas with submerged roots and overhanging vegetation. Distinguished by its striking red dorsal and caudal coloration. Care requirements are similar to other Macropodus species — tolerates a range of temperatures, prefers a planted tank, and males are territorial with one another.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A small croaking gourami from rice paddies, marshes, and swamps of the Mekong basin, closely related to but larger than the dwarf croaking gourami. Like its relatives, produces audible croaking sounds during courtship and territorial encounters using its pectoral fins and swimbladder. Peaceful and undemanding; suitable for smaller planted community tanks. Feeds on small invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fan the eggs.

description: A massive, endangered giant gourami endemic to the Kinabatangan and Segama river basins of Sabah, Borneo. Juveniles display vivid red fin coloration that develops fully with age. Like the common giant gourami, it requires a truly enormous tank and generates heavy bioload. Rarely caught even in the wild. An impressive, long-lived species for public aquaria or dedicated specialists with the space to house it properly.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; large nest constructed from plant material.

description: A peaceful, subtly attractive gourami with an elongated spike-like central tail ray, found across India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and the Malay Peninsula. Shy and best kept in a quiet, dimly lit, planted tank; prefers live or frozen micro-foods over dried preparations. Can be kept as a pair or small group with calm, small cyprinids. A cave or coconut shell is essential for spawning.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder in a cave or crevice; male guards the nest and fry.
dimorphism: Males have extended dorsal, anal and caudal fins with more intense colour and pale bodies, while females darken in colour.

description: A close relative of the spiketail paradisefish, endemic to ditches, swamps, and slow streams in Kerala, southern India. Slightly larger and prefers slightly warmer water than P. cupanus. Peaceful and shy; requires a heavily planted setup with gentle filtration and a cave for breeding. Both parents tend the bubble nest. Infrequently available in the hobby.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; both parents guard the nest and fry.

description: A tiny, exquisitely patterned anabantoid restricted to a small area of southwest Sri Lanka — one of the most geographically restricted labyrinth fish. Requires unusually cool temperatures for a tropical gourami. Only suitable for a dedicated species tank given its rarity and conservation value. Extremely shy; feeds exclusively on small live invertebrates. Captive breeding is a conservation priority.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder in a cave or crevice; pair spawns in a small clutch.

description: A wild betta from stagnant pools, ditches, and paddy fields of Thailand and the Malay Peninsula. Despite the species epithet ('peaceful'), males do fight — though less intensively than the common betta. Males display iridescent blue scaling and a distinctive red crescent on the tail fin. Best kept in a single-species tank or with very small, non-confrontational fish. Prefers live or frozen invertebrate foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards the fry.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful and develop extended fins as they mature, while females are plainer.

description: A bubble-nesting wild betta from sluggish ditches, rice paddies, and swamps of eastern Thailand and central Laos. Males display brilliant iridescent green scaling on the opercles and a barred caudal fin. Similar care to B. imbellis; males are territorial toward each other but generally manageable. Thrives in a heavily planted, low-flow tank. Prefers live or frozen small invertebrates alongside dried foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful and develop extended fins as they mature, while females are plainer.

description: A distinctively colored wild betta endemic to Samut Sakhon Province, Thailand, inhabiting brackish nipa palm swamps where it uses water-filled leaf axils as breeding sites. Uniquely among bubble-nesting bettas, it tolerates and benefits from slightly hard, alkaline to slightly brackish water. Males display iridescent blue-green scaling. Best kept in a species tank with dense cover.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest in floating vegetation or plant structures.

description: A small wild betta from shallow marshes, grass fields, and paddy fields in eastern Thailand and Cambodia. Very closely related to B. smaragdina and B. imbellis; often confused with the latter in the hobby. Males display iridescent emerald-green coloration. Peaceful relative to most bettas; males can sometimes coexist with sufficient cover. Prefers live or frozen micro-foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny paternal mouthbrooding betta from shallow, leaf-littered forest streams in East Kalimantan. Named for the distinctive white margins on the fins of both sexes. One of the more peaceful and community-tolerant wild bettas; can be kept in small groups if the tank is well-planted. Requires acidic, soft water and small live or frozen invertebrate foods.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 10–21 days.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful with a broader head shape than females.

description: A small, striking paternal mouthbrooding betta from blackwater forest streams in the Mahakam River basin of East Kalimantan. Named for its somewhat snakehead-like head profile. Males display brilliant red-orange coloration. One of the easiest wild mouthbrooding bettas to keep; tolerates groups. Requires highly acidic, very soft water and small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia and Artemia.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.
dimorphism: Adult males are more colourful and have a broader head shape than females.

description: One of the largest and most spectacular wild bettas, endemic to Brunei and Sarawak on Borneo and Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Males display intense red coloration with iridescent red opercular patches. Requires cool, highly acidic, soft blackwater and a diet of high-quality live or frozen invertebrate foods. Males fight constantly — keep only as a bonded pair. A prestigious, demanding species for the specialist.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for up to 30 days.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and more colourful, with a broader head and more extended fins than females.

description: One of the largest betta species, reaching over 12 cm, from forest streams in the Kayan and Howong drainages of East Kalimantan. A paternal mouthbrooder like other members of the unimaculata complex. More peaceful than bubble-nesting bettas toward other species but males spar with each other. Prefers cooler, clear water and a diet of meaty live or frozen foods. Needs a tightly covered tank as it is a strong jumper.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.

description: A large paternal mouthbrooding betta restricted to southern East Kalimantan, Borneo. Part of the unimaculata group of large mouthbrooders. Requires slightly acidic, soft water and a carnivore diet of live or frozen invertebrates and small fish. Males are territorial with conspecifics; best kept as a single pair or individually. A secure lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.

description: A medium-sized paternal mouthbrooding betta from slow ravines and isolated pools surrounded by leaf litter in northeastern Borneo. Feeds primarily on terrestrial insects in the wild; accepts live or frozen invertebrates in captivity. A gifted jumper — cover the tank securely. Males compete but overall less aggressive than bubble-nesting bettas toward other species.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for 2–3 weeks.

description: A widespread paternal mouthbrooding betta from forest streams and hill rivers of the Malay Peninsula, Penang, and Sumatra. Inhabits areas with overhanging vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter rather than dense aquatic vegetation. Less aggressive than most bettas; pairs can be kept together without constant fighting. Feeds on insects and invertebrates. Requires a tightly fitted lid.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with a broader head, more iridescent head scaling and more extended fins than females.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta endemic to acidic peat swamps of Johor, Malaysia, among the most extreme blackwater specialists of its genus. Requires exceptionally acidic, very soft water with negligible hardness. Males display a vivid blue-green iridescence. Feeds on small live invertebrates; reluctant to accept frozen or dried foods. For the dedicated blackwater specialist only.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from streams and standing waters of Java, Indonesia. One of the less aggressive wild bettas; pairs and small groups can coexist with adequate cover. Males display spotted patterning that gives the species its name. Requires soft, acidic to neutral water and small live or frozen invertebrates for best condition. A tightly fitted lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.
dimorphism: Mature males have a broader head and more iridescent scaling on the head, body and unpaired fins, and are more colourful than females.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from fast-flowing hill streams of Borneo, found amid submerged leaf litter, tree roots, and overhanging bank vegetation. Subtly attractive with fine horizontal banding. Pairs and small groups can be maintained together if sufficient cover is provided. Prefers small live or frozen invertebrates. Requires a tight-fitting lid and leaf-litter décor for natural behavior.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A diminutive, deep wine-red betta from blackwater peat swamps of Sumatra and Malaysia. Males display vivid crimson bodies with iridescent green fin margins. Bubble-nesting species that requires very soft, acidic blackwater with minimal hardness. Aggressive toward conspecifics — best kept as a solitary individual or as a single pair with dense cover. Prone to obesity; feed sparingly with live or frozen micro-foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured with longer fins, while females are duller.

description: One of the smallest members of the coccina complex, from highly acidic peat swamp forests in Sarawak and adjacent Malaysia. Brown base coloration with a striking iridescent blue-green spot on the flank. Demands extreme blackwater conditions with very low hardness and negligible dissolved minerals. Bubble-nesting species; best kept as a solitary individual or single pair. Feed tiny live or frozen invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny, Endangered bubble-nesting betta endemic to a very small area of peat swamp forest in Selangor, Malaysia. Among the most specialized blackwater bettas — requires extremely acidic water with near-zero hardness in a well-shaded, densely planted tank. Closely related to B. coccina and B. brownorum. Captive breeding populations are vital for conservation. Not a fish for the average aquarist.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny coccina-group bubble-nesting betta from extremely acidic peat swamps south of Pekan, Pahang, Malaysia. Among the more temperature-sensitive wild bettas, preferring cooler conditions than most tropical species. Demands near-zero hardness blackwater with very low pH. Males display reddish coloration typical of the coccina group. Best kept alone or as a single pair in a dedicated blackwater setup.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A diminutive coccina-group betta from blackwater peat swamp forests with very little sunlight in West Kalimantan. Males display vivid red finnage against a darker body. Requires extreme blackwater conditions with negligible hardness and highly acidic pH. Keeps well only in a dedicated species tank or blackwater setup. Feeds on tiny live or frozen invertebrates such as Daphnia and Artemia nauplii.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny Endangered betta from the blackwater peat swamps of Johor, Malaysia, threatened by oil palm plantation expansion. Males are largely blue-black with a striking dark, jewel-like appearance. Like all coccina-group bettas, demands highly acidic, extremely soft blackwater with minimal flow. A bubble-nesting species. Captive breeding programs are helping preserve this rare and exquisite fish.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A Critically Endangered coccina-group betta described in 2013, from shallow peat swamps of the Sebangau drainage in Central Kalimantan. Inhabits water just 5–50 cm deep among dense aquatic vegetation. Requires highly acidic blackwater with near-zero hardness. Only available through specialist breeders. Like all coccina-group bettas, best kept in a single-species blackwater setup.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: One of the smallest coccina-group bettas, endemic to peat swamp and secondary forest habitats on Bangka Island, Indonesia. Named for its wine-burgundy coloration. Requires extremely acidic, soft blackwater with virtually no dissolved minerals. Rarely available except through specialist breeders. Best kept in a tiny, dedicated blackwater species tank with dim lighting and dense floating vegetation.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A Vulnerable paternal mouthbrooding betta from swamp forests and forest streams in West Kalimantan, Borneo. One of the smaller mouthbrooding bettas. Requires soft, acidic water and live or frozen invertebrate foods. Can be kept as a pair or small group with adequate shelter and a heavily planted blackwater setup. Rarely seen in the hobby outside specialist circles.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–3 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from forest swamp streams with dense vegetation in Central and West Kalimantan, Indonesian Borneo. Males display orange-red opercular bars and extended finnage. Requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and a diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Best kept as a single pair or alone; males can be aggressive with each other. A firm favourite among Betta enthusiasts.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from the peat swamp forests of South Kalimantan, Borneo, closely related to B. foerschi. Males display attractive reddish-brown coloration with elongated finnage. Requires acidic, soft blackwater and a carnivore diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Best kept as a single pair in a dedicated blackwater species tank. Infrequently available in the hobby.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: An Endangered paternal mouthbrooding betta endemic to peat swamps of northern Sumatra near Lake Toba. Despite its common name, not closely related to B. splendens — males do not build bubble nests. Requires highly acidic, soft blackwater typical of Sumatran peat swamps. Males display vivid coloration. A sought-after species among wild Betta enthusiasts; only available through specialist breeders.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful and develop more-extended unpaired fins than females.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from limestone hill streams and sluggish marshes of Krabi Province, southern Thailand. Unusual among wild bettas for its preference for neutral to slightly alkaline water — reflecting its limestone karst habitat. Less aggressive than most bettas and can be kept as a pair or small group. Feeds on small aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–3 weeks.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with a broader head, more iridescent head scaling and more extended fins than females.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from swamps in South Kalimantan, Indonesian Borneo. One of the larger mouthbrooding bettas; requires a longer tank footprint than smaller species. Subdued but attractive coloration. Feeds on small live or frozen invertebrates; reluctant with dried food alone. Best kept alone or as a single pair with plenty of cover. A tightly fitted lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with a broader head, more iridescent head scaling and more extended fins than females.

description: A large, slender bubble-nesting betta from peat swamp streams of Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula, primarily hunting insects — especially damselfly larvae — at the water surface. One of the less commonly kept large bettas; requires a spacious, covered tank with acidic, soft water. Aggressive with conspecifics; best kept individually or as a single pair. Accepts dried food but does best on live or frozen invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: One of the most gentle cichlids available, from small, clear forest creeks of the Orinoco delta and coastal rivers of Guyana. Named for the keyhole-shaped marking on its flank. Shy and easily stressed; does best in a planted tank with leaf litter, driftwood, and small, non-aggressive tankmates. Susceptible to poor water quality — requires mature, well-filtered water. Excellent choice for soft-water community tanks.
reproduction: Open spawner on flat surfaces; both parents guard eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Adult males grow considerably larger than females and develop extended dorsal and anal fins.

description: A colorful, hardy Central American cichlid from muddy lake and swamp habitats with dense vegetation. Males develop dazzling yellow and orange breeding colors.
