reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Females carry eggs for approximately three weeks. Not easy to breed in captivity.
dimorphism: Males develop vivid metallic blue coloration with gold and black patterning; females and non-dominant males are drab silver-gray.

description: A striking Lake Malawi haplochromine from the southern rocky shorelines. Breeding males are spectacular — the body and head turn a deep sulphur-yellow to orange, contrasting with neon blue patterning on the face and a vivid yellow-orange dorsal fin. Peaceful enough to mix with other non-aggressive Malawi haps and Aulonocara, but males pursue females vigorously; keep one male with multiple females. A rock-dwelling species rarely seen in open water.
behavior: A rock-hugging species that rarely ventures into open water. Males are territorial during breeding but otherwise tolerant. Best kept as one male with a harem of females.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Females carry eggs for approximately three weeks.
dimorphism: Males are larger and develop brilliant sulphur-yellow to orange coloration with neon blue facial markings when breeding; females are smaller and drab silver-brown.

description: A tiny shell-dwelling cichlid from the southern shores of Lake Tanganyika that packs a ferocious territorial temperament into a very small body. Males claim a shell and excavate a sand pit around it, while females are confined inside the shell itself. Despite their size they will defend their territory vigorously against fish many times larger. A fascinating species for a species-only biotope setup with a fine sand substrate and multiple Neothauma shells.
behavior: Males maintain and defend a territory around one or more shells. Females are confined to the interior of their shell. The species can form small harems if space allows.
reproduction: Substrate spawner inside the shell. Eggs are attached to the inner shell surface; both parents guard the territory. Fry appear after about a week.
dimorphism: Males are noticeably larger than females and have more extended fins; females are small and slim, spending most time inside the shell.

description: One of the classic Tanganyika shell-dwellers, the brevis lives almost exclusively inside empty Neothauma snail shells. Despite its tiny size, it vigorously defends its shell and the sand around it. Males are noticeably larger than females. Easy to breed in a well-set-up species tank with fine sand and plenty of shells. Feed primarily on live and frozen foods such as brine shrimp and micro-worms; water must be hard and alkaline.
behavior: Lives in and fiercely defends its shell. Males patrol a small sand territory and will confront fish much larger than themselves. Spends the majority of time near the substrate.
reproduction: Spawns inside the shell. The female lays eggs on the inner shell surface and guards them; the male defends the outer territory. Fry emerge as free-swimming juveniles.
dimorphism: Males are significantly larger than females with more elaborate finnage; females are slim and spend much of their time inside the shell.

description: One of the larger Lamprologus-group cichlids from the southern half of Lake Tanganyika, named for the bold vertical barring across its body. A specialized mollusk-eater whose pharyngeal teeth are adapted to crush snail shells. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics and other cave-dwelling Tanganyika species; best kept as a single pair. A powerful, territorial cichlid that requires a hard, alkaline tank with plenty of rock structures.
behavior: Extremely aggressive toward conspecifics; territories are fiercely defended. Caves and crevices in the rockwork serve as spawning and refuge sites.
reproduction: Cave spawner. The pair spawn in a rock crevice; both parents guard eggs and fry actively.
dimorphism: Males are larger and often display more intense coloration; both sexes share the six-bar pattern.

description: A moderately-sized Tanganyikan cichlid from the southern lake, patterned with pale horizontal stripes and spotted fins. Extremely aggressive toward its own kind and unsuitable for community cichlid setups; best kept as a single pair or alone. Rarely accepts dried foods and requires regular offerings of live and frozen fare such as brine shrimp, bloodworm, and chopped prawn. Water must be hard and alkaline.
behavior: Strongly territorial. Males are highly aggressive toward other males and should not be kept together. Best managed as a single male-female pair in a rocky setup.
reproduction: Cave spawner. Lays eggs in a rock crevice or cave; both parents guard the territory. Fry are small and difficult to raise without live food.
dimorphism: Males are somewhat larger with more elaborate finnage; females are slimmer. Both sexes show similar striped patterning.

description: A small but territorial Tanganyikan cichlid similar in shape and temperament to the well-known Brichardi group. Often sold as 'Striped Brichardi,' it displays a pale body with thin horizontal stripes and elongated fin extensions in mature fish. Best kept as a mated pair in a species tank with rocky structure. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics; a matched pair will form a strong bond and cooperate to defend their territory.
behavior: Monogamous and pairs bond strongly. Both male and female defend the spawning territory aggressively. The species is best kept alone or with unrelated non-competing species.
reproduction: Cave spawner. Both parents guard eggs and fry with determination; older fry sometimes assist in guarding subsequent broods.
dimorphism: Males tend to be slightly larger with longer fin extensions; females are slimmer and smaller. Coloration is similar in both sexes.

description: A remarkable Lake Tanganyika shell-dwelling cichlid with one of the most extreme sexual size differences in the fish world: males reach 15 cm while females top out at just 6 cm. Males collect piles of empty Neothauma snail shells to attract females; females enter the shells to spawn and brood within. In the wild, dominant males maintain large shell piles with harems of females. Keep as a single male with several females in a sandy biotope with plenty of shells.
behavior: Males are highly territorial and aggressive toward rivals. The male collects and guards a pile of shells; females occupy shells within this territory. Sneaker males are observed in the wild.
reproduction: Females spawn inside the shell; the male fertilizes the eggs through the shell opening. The female guards the eggs and fry from inside; the male guards the outer territory.
dimorphism: Extreme sexual dimorphism: males are 2–3× larger than females. Males develop more elaborate fins; females remain small and slim.

description: The most brilliantly colored of the Julidochromis complex, with a bold yellow and black patterned body. Found along rocky shorelines in the extreme north and south of Lake Tanganyika. Forms monogamous or polygamous pair bonds and can display interesting cooperative breeding behavior with helper fish from previous broods. Territorial toward conspecifics, especially during breeding, but can be kept in community setups with other Tanganyika species of similar temperament.
behavior: Rocky substrate specialist; swims near the rock face and picks invertebrates from the surface. Pairs bond and establish a rock cave as a breeding site; previous fry may assist in guarding later broods.
reproduction: Cave spawner. The pair lay eggs on a rocky surface within a crevice; both parents guard the eggs and fry. Fry may stay with parents for an extended period.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar in coloration; males may develop slightly more pointed fins. The female is sometimes slightly larger.

description: A slender, attractively patterned Julie cichlid endemic to the southwestern shoreline of Lake Tanganyika. Displays a cream-yellow body with dark horizontal striping and spotted dorsal and caudal fins. More subdued in color than the golden julie but elegant in its own right. Territorial toward conspecifics but can be housed with other Tanganyikan species that occupy different niches. Best kept as a mated pair in a rocky biotope; the pair bond is strong and both parents guard fry.
behavior: Hugs the rock face and maintains a small territory around a crevice. The mated pair cooperates in territory defense; previous fry may serve as helpers in subsequent broods.
reproduction: Cave spawner. Eggs are laid on a rock surface within a crevice; both parents guard the clutch and fry actively.
dimorphism: Males and females are similar in appearance. Females may be slightly larger; males sometimes develop more elongated fin extensions.

description: A small but feisty Telmatochromis from Lake Tanganyika, patterned with bold dark horizontal stripes on a pale body. Often described as territorial yet shy, it spends much of its time near rocky substrate and crevices. Best kept as a pair in a species or Tanganyika community tank; males are aggressive toward rival males. Feed primarily on live and frozen foods supplemented with vegetable matter. Requires hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Shy but territorial. Prefers the shelter of rocky crevices and defends the area around its cave against intruders. Compatible with other Tanganyikan species that use different territories.
reproduction: Cave spawner. The pair choose a rocky crevice; females lay eggs on the substrate inside. Both parents guard the eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are somewhat larger with more pointed fins; females are smaller. Coloration is similar in both sexes.

description: A brilliantly colored open-water schooling cichlid from the northern portion of Lake Tanganyika. Males display vivid blue and yellow coloration depending on morph; females and juveniles are more subdued. A specialized zooplankton feeder that thrives in groups of 8 or more, making it one of the few truly schooling Tanganyikan cichlids. Peaceful and ideal for community Tanganyika setups; requires small, frequent feedings of live or frozen invertebrates.
behavior: A gregarious schooling species that must be kept in groups. Inhabits the open water column above rocky substrates. Males display constantly within the group, cycling in and out of bright breeding coloration.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder. Males display in mid-water; females collect eggs immediately after spawning and incubate them in the mouth. Fry are released after about three weeks.
dimorphism: Males develop brilliant blue, yellow, or bicolor patterns depending on locality morph; females are drab silver-brown. Males are also slightly larger.

description: A slender, peaceful schooling cichlid from the northern Lake Tanganyika, closely related to the Cyprichromis genus. Males display iridescent blue coloration with black fin edges; females are more subdued. Very shy and retiring — must be kept in groups of at least 8 and housed with equally peaceful tankmates. A micro-predator that feeds on zooplankton; requires live or small frozen foods offered in frequent small portions.
behavior: Inhabits the mid-water column in rocky Tanganyikan habitats. Very shy and easily stressed if kept in small numbers or with boisterous fish. Groups of 8+ provide confidence and natural behavior.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder. Females incubate eggs in the mouth; fry are released after approximately three weeks.
dimorphism: Males develop vivid blue iridescence with distinctive black fin edges; females are drab and pale. Males are slightly larger.

description: One of the most visually striking Tropheus variants, juveniles are jet black with vivid white spots that fade as they mature into adult coloration. Found only in the northern part of Lake Tanganyika near rocky shorelines. Like all Tropheus, must be kept in groups of 15–20+ to disperse intraspecific aggression; smaller groups suffer severe fighting and deaths. Feed almost exclusively on vegetable matter — spirulina, nori, and blanched greens. Animal protein causes bloat and death.
behavior: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics; a group of 15-20+ is the minimum to prevent lethal bullying. Peaceful toward other species but will chase anything in its territory.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Females carry eggs for about four weeks; broods are small (5-15 fry).
dimorphism: Males are slightly larger; both sexes show similar coloration. Juveniles have white spots that fade with age.

description: One of several Tropheus species endemic to the central rocky shorelines of Lake Tanganyika, displaying locality-specific color morphs. Like all Tropheus, highly aggressive toward conspecifics and requires large groups (15–20 minimum) to prevent lethal fighting. A strict herbivore — feed primarily on spirulina, blanched vegetables, and nori; high-protein foods cause fatal bloat. A challenging but rewarding species for experienced Tanganyika specialists.
behavior: Extremely aggressive intraspecifically. Large groups disperse aggression across multiple targets, reducing the risk of a single fish being targeted to death.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Small broods of 5-15 fry are incubated for approximately four weeks.
dimorphism: Males are slightly larger; coloration varies greatly by locality morph and is similar between sexes.

description: One of the most spectacular Tanganyikan cichlids, named for the male's magnificent elongated, feathery pelvic fins used in elaborate spawning displays. Males are strikingly colored in blue, green, and black with the extraordinary finnage; females are much plainer. A large, open-water species that requires a spacious tank with plenty of swimming room. Feeds primarily on vegetable matter and plankton; avoid high-protein foods. Males are boisterous and territorial toward rivals.
behavior: Males perform elaborate spiral spawning dances to court females, using their elongated pelvic fins in display. Requires dominant status in the tank; becomes withdrawn if intimidated by larger fish.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. The male builds a spawning crater in the sand; the female spawns and immediately incubates the eggs. Broods are small.
dimorphism: Extreme sexual dimorphism: males develop brilliant metallic coloration and massively elongated, feather-like pelvic fins; females are drab silver-gray.

description: A graceful Tanganyikan cichlid from the southern lake, closely related to the featherfin cichlid and known for the male's elongated pelvic fins tipped with egg-like orange spots used to attract females during spawning dances. Available in several locality color morphs. Primarily vegetarian; feed blanched greens and spirulina flakes as the bulk of the diet. Relatively peaceful but males become territorial during breeding; provide plenty of space.
behavior: Males perform elaborate spawning dances with their pelvic-fin egg dummies to court females. Peaceful outside breeding season but males are territorial toward rivals when spawning.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. The male uses egg-dummy spots on his pelvic fins to induce females to follow him to the spawning site and release eggs.
dimorphism: Males develop brilliant locality-specific coloration and elongated pelvic fins with orange egg-dummy spots; females are plain silver-brown.

description: A vivid haplochromine cichlid endemic to Lake Victoria, where it inhabits the rocky shallows around Makobe Island. Males display striking red-orange flanks over a dark body; females are drab brown. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics, especially rival males; best kept as a single male with several females in a species tank. Water quality and stable alkalinity are essential.
dimorphism: Males are brilliantly colored with red-orange flanks and dark barring; females are plain brown with faint barring.
reproduction: A maternal mouthbrooder: the female carries the eggs and fry in her mouth for about three weeks before releasing the free-swimming young.

description: The most intensely red of the jewel cichlids, the blood-red jewel turns a uniform deep crimson when in breeding condition. Native to the Congo River basin, it is normally placid outside of spawning but becomes ferociously territorial when paired and breeding. Best kept as a single pair in a dedicated tank; will kill other fish when guarding eggs or fry.
behavior: Relatively peaceful outside breeding season but fiercely territorial when spawning. The pair bonds strongly and cooperates in rearing fry.
dimorphism: Males and females both show vivid red coloration; females tend to be slightly smaller and develop an intense violet sheen on the belly when gravid.
reproduction: A biparental substrate spawner: the pair cleans a flat rock or pot, the female lays eggs for the male to fertilise, and both parents guard the brood until the fry are free-swimming.

description: A compact, bottom-dwelling cichlid from the fast-flowing lower Congo River, immediately recognizable by the prominent nuchal hump on mature males. Rheophilic by nature, it benefits from a powerhead to simulate current and appreciates caves and rocky hiding spots. Pairs bond closely and share parental duties; generally docile outside breeding but territorial toward other cichlids when spawning.
behavior: Bottom-dweller that hops along the substrate rather than swimming continuously, an adaptation to life in swift-water rapids. Pairs co-parent, guarding the cave spawn together.
reproduction: Cave spawner; the pair selects a sheltered rocky cavity and both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.
dimorphism: Males develop a large, rounded nuchal hump and grow significantly larger (to 13 cm) than females (to 10 cm).

description: A tiny freshwater pufferfish from blackwater streams of Sumatra and Borneo, distinguished from similar species by the male's vivid red tail fin. Less aggressive than many puffers but males are territorial; best kept singly or with peaceful, fast-moving cyprinids. Feed primarily snails and hard-shelled invertebrates to wear down the constantly growing beak.
behavior: Inquisitive and intelligent; will investigate anything new in the tank. Males are territorial and may bite fins of slow-moving tankmates.
dimorphism: Males are larger with a distinctive red tail fin; females are smaller and mottled brown without the red tail.

description: A uniquely transparent fish from South and Southeast Asia through which the entire skeleton and internal organs are visible. Hardy and peaceful in a suitable group, it is best kept with similarly sized, non-aggressive tankmates and provided a well-planted tank with gentle filtration. Avoid dyed or 'painted' specimens, which are artificially injected with fluorescent dyes and suffer serious long-term health problems.
behavior: Shy and best in a group of 6 or more; more active and less stressed in numbers. Avoids confrontation and may be outcompeted for food by boisterous species.
dimorphism: Males develop a dark edge on the dorsal and anal fins during breeding condition; females are plainer and typically rounder when gravid.
reproduction: An egg scatterer with no parental care; a pair may attach up to 200 eggs to plant leaves and stems, after which the adults are removed.

description: A tiny jewel of a fish from Myanmar streams, the red melon badis displays brilliant scarlet and orange coloration in males that rivals any nano fish. Shy and retiring, it needs a densely planted nano tank and small live or frozen foods — it will typically ignore dry foods. Males are aggressive toward each other, so keep only one per tank or ensure a large, heavily planted space.
behavior: Shy and easily dominated; will not compete at feeding time with active species. Males are fiercely territorial toward each other but generally ignore other species.
dimorphism: Males display vivid scarlet and orange colors with a bold black spot on the dorsal fin; females are drab brown at about 1.5 cm.
reproduction: The male holds a territory and courts females; eggs are laid on a solid surface, the female is then driven off and the male alone guards them until the fry hatch.

description: One of the world's oldest and most popular ornamental fish, goldfish come in hundreds of varieties ranging from sleek 30 cm comets to round-bodied 15 cm fancy forms. A coldwater species that does not require a heater; common and comet varieties are hardy but fancy varieties with extreme body shapes are more delicate. Heavy waste producers requiring robust filtration; never house in a bowl or unfiltered tank.
behavior: Active, social fish that recognize their owners and respond to feeding times. Common and comet varieties need substantial swimming space; fancy varieties are slower and prefer calmer water.
dimorphism: Males develop white tubercles (spawning spots) on the gill covers and pectoral fins during breeding season; females appear rounder when gravid.

description: Often sold as cute 6 cm juveniles with dramatic triangular dorsal fins, this endangered Yangtze River fish grows to over 1 metre and is only suitable for public aquariums or very large pond installations. Juveniles are peaceful scavengers but adults require vastly more space than any home aquarium. Purchasing should only be considered by those with facilities to house it long-term; most fish sold into the hobby cannot be rehomed appropriately.
behavior: Docile and non-territorial even as adults. In public aquariums they are often kept in large groups. Juveniles school together; adults become more solitary.
dimorphism: Females grow larger than males; males have more vivid banding as juveniles.

description: An ancient and striking cichlid endemic to Madagascar, also known as the starry-night cichlid for the male's jet-black body spangled with iridescent gold-to-blue spots. Vulnerable in the wild due to habitat loss and the aquarium trade. Piscivorous and aggressive toward conspecifics; best kept as a solitary specimen or a bonded pair in a large tank. A rewarding species for the experienced cichlid keeper.
behavior: Intelligent and territorial; pairs bond strongly and cooperate in brood care. Aggressive to any other fish perceived as a rival or threat. Males spar with nuchal hump displays.
reproduction: Pairs spawn on flat surfaces or in shallow depressions; both parents guard eggs and fry with great dedication.
dimorphism: Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump and grow significantly larger (to 28 cm), with more vivid iridescent spotting; females are smaller (to ~14 cm) and plainer.

description: A robust and personable West African cichlid best known for its unusual biparental mouthbrooding behavior where both the male and female take turns holding the fry in their mouths. Relatively peaceful with non-cichlid species but territorial toward conspecifics when spawning; best kept as a pair in a community with dither fish. Females are uniquely more colorful than males, displaying a pink belly and iridescent dorsal stripe.
behavior: Peaceful with other species when not breeding; pairs bond strongly and cooperate, trading mouthbrooding duties between both parents.
reproduction: Biparental mouthbrooder; uniquely, both the male and female carry eggs and fry in their mouths alternately, providing extended parental care.
dimorphism: Unusually, females are more colorful than males: females display a bright pink belly and a metallic white dorsal stripe; males are less patterned and larger.

description: A tiny and exquisitely marked dwarf cichlid endemic to the highly acidic, softwater Lake Mai Ndombe in the Congo basin, listed as Endangered due to overfishing for the aquarium trade. Notable for reversed sexual dimorphism where females are the more colorful sex. Requires very soft, acidic water to thrive; will not do well in hard alkaline conditions. Best kept as a pair in a species tank with fine sand.
behavior: Relatively peaceful with other species but aggressive toward conspecifics; best kept as a solitary pair.
reproduction: Cave spawner; the pair guards eggs and fry in a small cavity.
dimorphism: Females are more colorful with bright orange-red markings on the belly and fins; males are grayer with more subtle patterning.

description: A superb ambush predator from West African forest streams, the African leaf fish drifts motionlessly with mouth agape, appearing dead until prey is within striking distance. Requires very warm, highly acidic, soft water and a species tank; any fish small enough to fit in its mouth will be eaten. Usually accepts only live food though some individuals can be trained onto frozen bloodworm or dead prey with movement.
behavior: Solitary ambush predator; drifts motionlessly in mid-water mimicking a dead leaf or debris. Tolerates small groups of its own species in a large tank.
reproduction: Builds a bubble nest among floating plants; male guards the nest and fry.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar in appearance; females become noticeably rounder when gravid.

description: A large and rare bichir endemic to the upper Cross River in Cameroon, notable for its striking black dorsal markings, black pectoral fins, and orange ventral surface. A living fossil with functional lungs; must have access to the water surface to breathe air. Predatory and will consume tankmates it can swallow, but otherwise peaceful. Rare in the hobby with established captive breeding.
behavior: Slow-moving, nocturnal predator that relies on smell rather than sight. Spends most time on or near the substrate. Has external gills as a juvenile.
reproduction: Seasonal spawner; pairs scatter adhesive eggs among plants; no parental care.
dimorphism: Males have a broader anal fin used during spawning; females are somewhat rounder when gravid.

description: A strikingly colored predatory fish from South American blackwater and clearwater systems, the rainbow wolf fish displays remarkable juvenile mimicry of female killifish to lure prey. Strictly predatory and cannot be kept with smaller fish; fiercely intolerant of conspecifics. Adults accept meaty frozen and fresh foods; captive-bred individuals can often be weaned from live food. A good choice for a dedicated single-specimen predator tank.
behavior: Solitary ambush predator; highly intolerant of conspecifics, which often fight to the death. Active but stealthy; patrols the middle column.
dimorphism: No clear external dimorphism; females may be somewhat plumper when gravid.

description: A robust predatory fish from South American floodplains and swamps, the gold wolf fish has a modified swim bladder that enables it to breathe atmospheric air and survive in poorly oxygenated or even drying habitats. Solitary and extremely aggressive toward conspecifics — keep alone or only with large fish too big to be eaten. Adapts to frozen and fresh meaty foods in captivity.
behavior: Solitary and highly territorial; consumes any fish it can fit in its mouth and will fight conspecifics to the death. A facultative air-breather; makes regular trips to the surface.
dimorphism: No clear external dimorphism; females are somewhat plumper when gravid.

description: A remarkable weakly electric fish from Central and West African rivers, instantly recognizable by its dramatically elongated, downward-curved snout used to probe substrate crevices for invertebrates. Generates and detects weak electric fields for navigation and intraspecific communication. Best kept alone or in a large tank with peaceful, non-cichlid species; will not tolerate other mormyrids.
behavior: Weakly electric; communicates and navigates by generating electric fields. Nocturnal and secretive, spending the day in hiding. Intolerant of other mormyrids.
dimorphism: Sexes are externally similar; males may have slightly longer caudal peduncle.

description: The largest weakly electric fish in the world and the sole member of its family, the aba aba can reach 1.65 metres and is suited only to public aquariums or extremely large private installations. Uses a constant wave electric organ discharge for navigation; swims using undulations of its long dorsal fin with no tail fin and can move equally well forwards and backwards. Dangerous to handle; adults can deliver powerful bites and electric shocks.
behavior: Highly territorial and aggressive; must be kept alone. Navigates by electric field in the absence of visual cues; active at night.
reproduction: Builds a floating plant nest during the rainy season; the male guards the eggs and fry.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism in juveniles; adult females may be slightly larger.

description: A nocturnal knifefish from South and Southeast Asian rivers, the bronze featherback is the sole living member of its genus and a popular food fish in its native range. Like all notopterids it swims with a long, undulating anal fin. A voracious predator that requires a very large tank — suitable only for public installations or the most dedicated large-tank specialists.
behavior: Nocturnal and territorial; hides during the day and becomes active at night to hunt. Tolerates large peaceful tankmates but territorial with other knifefish.
dimorphism: Sexes are externally similar; females become rounder when gravid.
reproduction: Spawning is unrecorded in aquaria; wild fish lay their eggs among submerged vegetation in flooded forest during the wet season.

description: A large and ancient knifefish from the Ganges and Brahmaputra systems of India and Bangladesh, the Indian featherback is Near Threatened in the wild. Often confused with its more common relative the clown featherback (Chitala ornata). Reaches 1.2 metres; suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist private aquaria. Swims using its long, continuous anal fin. Of cultural significance in Hinduism as an avatar of Vishnu (Matsya).
behavior: Nocturnal predator that hunts by scent and electrosensory detection in low-light conditions. Territorial with conspecifics; peaceful with large tankmates it cannot swallow.
dimorphism: Sexes are externally similar; females are slightly larger and more rounded when gravid.
reproduction: The female deposits eggs on a solid surface such as a submerged stump and the male guards them; spawning is unreported in home aquaria.

description: A Critically Endangered Victorian cichlid from Lake Kyoga and Lake Nawampasa in Uganda, with striking horizontal black bars on a yellow-green body. Highly aggressive and best kept as a male-female pair or harem (one male, several females) in a species tank. Wild populations have collapsed due to Nile perch introduction; captive stocks are valuable for conservation.
behavior: Territorial and aggressive; males display intensely and will harass conspecifics. Harem breeding with maternal mouthbrooding.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder; females hold eggs and fry in the mouth for several weeks.
dimorphism: Males develop bright yellow-green flanks with bold black bars; females are smaller and drabber.

description: The smallest snakehead, reaching only around 10 cm, endemic to cool, slow-moving highland streams of Sri Lanka. Unlike most snakeheads it tolerates slightly cooler water. An obligate air breather; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Mildly aggressive toward conspecifics but can be kept with robust, similarly-sized tankmates.
behavior: Semi-aggressive; territorial with conspecifics but generally ignores fish it cannot swallow. Regularly surfaces to breathe atmospheric air.
dimorphism: Males and females are similar in coloration; females may be slightly fuller-bodied when gravid.
reproduction: A paternal mouthbrooder that forms lasting pair bonds, the male holding the eggs in his mouth for about 3-5 days.

description: A large and highly aggressive piscivore from rivers across West and Central Africa, from Guinea to the Congo basin. The largest of the jewel cichlids, it will eat any fish that fits in its mouth. Best kept as a single specimen or bonded pair in a species tank. Bright red coloration intensifies during breeding.
behavior: Highly aggressive and piscivorous; will attack and eat smaller tankmates. Pairs bond strongly and are highly protective of spawn.
reproduction: Open substrate spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.
dimorphism: Males are larger; both sexes develop vivid red flanks when in breeding condition.

description: A small, spectacularly coloured snakehead from the Brahmaputra basin in West Bengal, India, with iridescent blue-green spots on a dark body. Benefits from a seasonal temperature drop to 15–19°C in winter to mimic its native highland climate. An obligate air breather; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Pairs are generally peaceful toward each other but intolerant of conspecifics.
behavior: Pairs bond and can be kept together; males are hostile toward rival males. Tolerates most fish it cannot eat.
reproduction: Pair spawns at the surface; male guards the eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured with brighter blue spots; females are fuller-bodied.

description: A sleek, rheophilic cichlid from the fast-flowing lower Congo River and Malebo Pool, slimmer than its relative the buffalo head cichlid. Males develop a modest frontal hump with age. Adapted to strong currents; provide high flow and smooth rocks in the aquarium. Territorial with conspecifics but can cohabit with non-cichlid species.
behavior: Pair-bonding and territorial; characteristic hopping locomotion along the substrate. Requires strong water current.
reproduction: Pair spawns in caves; both parents guard eggs and wriggling fry.
dimorphism: Males are larger with a more pronounced frontal hump; females remain smaller and lack the hump.

description: A beautiful dwarf cichlid from coastal rivers in Gabon and the lower Congo, closely related to the common kribensis but more rarely seen in the hobby. Females display a vivid purple-pink belly patch, especially during spawning. Generally peaceful in a community setting, but pairs become territorial when breeding. Benefits from soft to neutral water and plenty of caves.
behavior: Shy and retiring outside breeding; caves and dense planting reduce stress. Pairs defend spawning territory vigorously.
reproduction: Cave spawner; female guards eggs and newly-hatched fry while male defends the wider territory.
dimorphism: Females are smaller with a vivid purple-pink belly; males are larger and less colourful.

origin: East Africa (rift valley rivers, Lake Tanganyika basin)
description: A widely distributed rift valley cichlid found in Lake Tanganyika and associated rivers, famous as a model organism in behavioural and neuroscience research. Dominant males flash vivid blue and yellow colours while subordinate males remain drab grey-green — a rapid change controlled by social status. Highly aggressive among males; best kept as one male with multiple females in hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Males are intensely territorial and compete for dominance through colour displays and fighting. Dominant males display bright coloration; subordinate males mimic female colouration.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs and fry in the mouth for 18–22 days.
dimorphism: Dominant males are brilliantly coloured (blue-yellow with black facial markings); females and subordinate males are plain grey-green.

description: The most commonly traded jewel cichlid, found in coastal rivers from Sierra Leone to Cameroon. Often sold simply as 'jewel cichlid' in the trade. Brilliantly coloured in red with iridescent blue spots, especially when breeding. Highly territorial and will attack tankmates aggressively; best kept as a bonded pair in a species tank.
behavior: Highly aggressive and territorial, especially during breeding. Pairs bond strongly and attack any perceived threat near the spawning site.
reproduction: Open substrate spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry fiercely. Fry are moved to pits in the substrate.
dimorphism: Both sexes turn vivid red with blue iridescent spots during breeding; males are slightly larger.

description: A larger relative of the common kribensis, endemic to the Niger and Cross River systems in Nigeria. Males reach around 10 cm and display colourful flanks; females show a prominent purple-pink belly patch during breeding. Generally peaceful in a community setting but pairs become territorial when spawning. Prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water with caves for refuge.
behavior: Peaceful outside breeding; caves and planted cover reduce stress. Pairs are monogamous and defend spawning territory.
reproduction: Cave spawner; female guards eggs and fry, male defends the surrounding territory.
dimorphism: Females are smaller with a vivid purple-pink belly; males are larger and more elongate.

description: A small, elegant kribensis from small rivers and streams in coastal Guinea, Sierra Leone, and Liberia, where water is very soft and slightly acidic. One of the more peaceful Pelvicachromis species, suitable for community tanks with similarly sized fish. Males are colourful with yellow-red flanks; females display a vivid purple-pink belly during spawning.
behavior: Peaceful and shy; pairs form monogamous bonds and spawn in caves. Requires very soft water to thrive and breed.
reproduction: Cave spawner; female guards clutch, male defends territory. Fry are free-swimming in about a week.
dimorphism: Females smaller with rounded fins and a vivid pink-purple belly; males more slender with pointed fins and brighter flanks.

description: A small, slender cichlid from the lower Congo River rapids, adapted to fast-flowing, clear, slightly acidic water. Males develop a beautiful blue-green iridescence; females display a bright pink-purple belly when ready to spawn. Best kept as a pair with ample cover; relatively peaceful with heterospecifics but aggressive with conspecifics.
behavior: Pair-bonding; aggressive toward conspecifics but largely peaceful with other species. Prefers calm to moderate flow.
reproduction: Cave spawner; female guards eggs while male defends broader territory.
dimorphism: Females have a conspicuous pink-purple belly when gravid; males are larger and iridescent blue-green on the flanks.

description: A large, boldly patterned West African cichlid with dramatic black-and-white vertical stripes that fade on older fish. Endemic to coastal rivers from Guinea-Bissau to Liberia. Highly aggressive toward other fish; best kept as a single specimen or as a bonded pair in a very large tank. A heavy digger and plant destroyer — decorate with robust rocks rather than live plants.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial toward heterospecifics; less aggressive toward its own partner once a bond is formed. Vigorous digger.
reproduction: Open substrate spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.
dimorphism: Males are larger; stripe pattern similar in both sexes.

description: A large and colourful cichlid endemic to coastal Madagascar, known in the hobby as the Madagascar rainbow cichlid or Nosy Be cichlid. Males display vivid multi-colour flanks during breeding. Highly territorial and aggressive; best kept as a single species pair. Rarely seen in the trade, making it a conservation-relevant species to maintain in captivity.
behavior: Highly territorial; males display and fight vigorously. Best kept as a bonded pair in a species tank.
reproduction: Open substrate spawner; both parents defend eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are more vividly coloured during spawning; females are slightly smaller.

description: One of the most visually striking snakeheads, with iridescent blue-green spots and orange markings. Endemic to the headwaters of Kyeinthali Chaung in Rakhine State, Myanmar. Requires cool water and benefits from a seasonal temperature drop to mimic its highland stream habitat. An obligate air breather; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics.
behavior: Aggressive toward conspecifics; tolerates fish it cannot eat. Regularly surfaces to breathe atmospheric air. Annual rest period (cooler, shorter photoperiod) supports long-term health.
reproduction: Pair spawns at the surface; male guards eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured; females fuller-bodied.

description: A cool-water snakehead from clear, fast-flowing highland streams in Rakhine State, Myanmar. Adorned with elaborate orange, black and white patterning on its dorsal fin. Requires cool temperatures (15–20°C) and becomes very aggressive if kept too warm. Extremely aggressive toward conspecifics; best kept as a solitary specimen or bonded pair with abundant cover.
behavior: Extremely aggressive toward conspecifics; tolerates fish too large to swallow. Obligate air breather — keep the tank tightly covered.
dimorphism: Males and females are similar; females may be slightly fuller-bodied.

description: A temperate snakehead from southern China, Taiwan, and northern Vietnam that tolerates a wide temperature range and can be kept without a heater in warm rooms. Despite the name 'small snakehead', adults reach 35 cm. An obligate air breather that readily jumps — a tight lid is essential. Best kept alone or as a confirmed pair.
behavior: Aggressive toward conspecifics; tolerates fish it cannot swallow. Surfaces regularly to breathe air.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar; females may be slightly stockier when gravid.
reproduction: Not a mouthbrooder: the eggs float at the surface and both parents remain to guard the eggs and fry.

description: One of the largest and most spectacular snakeheads, with vivid orange and black barring that fades in older fish. Native to the hill streams of northeast India and Bangladesh. Can reach 90 cm and requires very large aquaria suited only to specialist fishkeepers. Benefits from seasonal temperature reduction in winter. Extremely intolerant of conspecifics; best kept as a solitary specimen or confirmed pair.
behavior: Extremely aggressive toward conspecifics and other snakeheads. Generally ignores fish it cannot swallow. Obligate air breather — requires a tight-fitting lid.
dimorphism: Sexes appear similar; females may be fuller-bodied.

description: A large, strikingly patterned snakehead from the blackwater rivers of Sumatra and Borneo, bearing distinctive eye-spot markings along the flanks. Prefers soft, acidic water. Unlike most snakeheads, juveniles can be kept in groups, but adults become more territorial. An obligate air breather; requires a tight-fitting lid.
behavior: Sociable with conspecifics when young; increasingly territorial as adults. Predatory toward fish small enough to swallow.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar in appearance.

description: A hardy and adaptable snakehead from China, Vietnam, Taiwan, and the Philippines, capable of tolerating a wide range of water conditions. Calmer than many of its relatives when young, but adults form strong bonds in pairs and become highly territorial. An obligate air breather; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Best kept alone or as a bonded pair.
behavior: Relatively calm juveniles; adults form pairs and defend large territories. Obligate air breather.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar; females fuller-bodied when gravid.

description: A large, slender snakehead from the blackwater forests and slow-moving rivers of Southeast Asia, from Thailand to Indonesia. Prefers soft, acidic water and a dimly lit aquarium with plenty of floating plants and driftwood cover. Juveniles can initially coexist, but adults become territorial. An obligate air breather requiring a tight-fitting lid.
behavior: Tolerates conspecifics better than most Channa species when young; adults are increasingly territorial. Best kept as a confirmed pair or singly.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar in coloration and pattern.
reproduction: A biparental free spawner: the eggs float at the surface and both parents remain to guard the eggs and fry for an extended period.

description: A slender, peaceable spiny eel widespread across South and Southeast Asia, from India to Indonesia. Often mistaken for other Macrognathus species. Requires a soft sandy substrate to burrow in and ample cover such as rocks and driftwood. Very peaceful toward most tankmates, though it will eat very small fish and invertebrates. A skilled escape artist — always secure the lid.
behavior: Nocturnal and reclusive; spends most of the day buried in sand or hiding in cover. Peaceful with tankmates too large to be considered prey.
dimorphism: Females are slightly stockier when gravid; otherwise sexes appear similar.

description: A striking, boldly patterned spiny eel from the Irrawaddy, Sittang, and Salween river systems of Myanmar. Its strong zebra-stripe pattern makes it one of the most visually impressive spiny eels in the hobby. Requires a soft sandy substrate for burrowing and plenty of hiding spots. Peaceful with fish too large to eat. A tight-fitting lid is essential.
behavior: Nocturnal; buries in substrate or hides during the day. Territorial with conspecifics in smaller tanks; tolerant of other species.
dimorphism: Sexes appear similar; females may be slightly broader when gravid.
reproduction: Breeding has so far been unsuccessful in captivity.

description: A medium-sized freshwater puffer from the rocky rapids of the Mekong River, named for the tiny skin filaments that give it a bristly appearance. Highly aggressive and best kept as a solitary specimen in a mature, well-filtered aquarium. Requires hard-shelled foods such as snails and crustaceans to prevent teeth overgrowth. Highly sensitive to poor water quality.
behavior: Solitary and highly territorial; attacks most tankmates. Intelligent and interactive with its keeper.
dimorphism: Females may be slightly rounder than males.

description: A freshwater puffer from the Mekong and surrounding river systems of Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia, also traded as the target puffer. Identifiable by its distinctive circular eye-spot and bold markings. Like all puffers, it will nip fins and attack tankmates; best kept alone. Requires hard-shelled foods to prevent beak overgrowth.
behavior: Aggressive fin nipper; cannot be trusted in community tanks. Likes to root around and explore the aquascape.
dimorphism: Females may be slightly rounder-bodied than males.

description: A sleek, golden-bronze puffer from swift rivers of Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Unlike the typical rotund puffer body shape, it is more streamlined, reflecting its current-adapted lifestyle. An aggressive fin-nipper best kept in a species tank. Requires regular hard-shelled foods to manage its continuously growing teeth. Sensitive to poor water quality.
behavior: Highly aggressive fin nipper; unsuitable for community tanks. Active and curious by day.
dimorphism: Females tend to be rounder-bodied than males.

origin: Southeast Asia (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins)
description: A large, boldly marked freshwater puffer from slow-moving backwaters and floodplains of the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins. Primarily crepuscular — most active during dawn and dusk. A notorious fin-nipper and aggressive toward conspecifics; must be kept singly in a species-only tank. Requires hard-shelled food items such as snails and crustaceans to manage its fused beak. Sensitive to nitrogenous waste — frequent water changes essential.
behavior: Solitary and territorial. Extremely aggressive toward other fish; must be kept alone. Crepuscular activity pattern.
dimorphism: Females typically rounder-bellied than males.

origin: Southeast Asia (Chao Phraya and Mekong River basins)
description: A mid-sized freshwater puffer from the Chao Phraya and Mekong river systems of Thailand and Vietnam, found in lakes, ponds, and slow river reaches. Extremely aggressive — will not tolerate any tankmates and must be kept as a solitary specimen. Provide plenty of hiding spots among rocks and robust plants. Requires hard-shelled food regularly to prevent overgrowth of the fused beak. Sensitive to deteriorating water quality.
behavior: Highly aggressive; intolerant of all tankmates. Must be kept as a single specimen in a species-only aquarium.
dimorphism: Females are rounder-bodied; males may show darker colouration when breeding.

origin: Africa (middle Congo River basin, Republic of Congo and DRC)
description: A robustly built bichir from the middle Congo River basin, named after the legendary Mokele-mbembe. Like all Polypterus, it breathes atmospheric air using a primitive lung and must have surface access. A nocturnal predator that will consume any fish small enough to fit in its mouth; best kept with robust, similarly-sized tankmates or alone. Requires a tightly secured lid as bichirs are adept escape artists. Accepts sinking carnivore pellets and meaty frozen foods.
behavior: Nocturnal bottom-dweller. Predatory toward small fish; generally tolerant of robust similarly-sized tankmates. Must have surface air access.
dimorphism: Males have broader, thicker anal fins used in courtship; females are generally more robust-bodied.

origin: East Africa (coastal regions of Somalia, Kenya, and Zambezi River delta)
description: The smallest of the African lungfishes, yet still a substantial oddity reaching around 44 cm. Found in coastal wetlands and river deltas of East Africa, where it can aestivate in mud during dry seasons. Must have access to atmospheric air — leave a 15 cm air gap at the top of the tank. Highly carnivorous with powerful jaws; best kept singly. A living fossil whose lineage predates dinosaurs, making it a fascinating but demanding long-term commitment with a lifespan exceeding 20 years.
behavior: Solitary and can be aggressive toward tankmates. Must surface regularly to breathe air. Tolerates a wide range of water parameters in the wild.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism; sexing requires internal examination.

description: A tiny but feisty puffer from the slow, blackwater streams of Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo. Despite its diminutive size, it is highly aggressive and should be kept alone or monitored closely in carefully managed species groups. Males develop striking red eyes. Provide dense planting with driftwood and leaf litter to mimic its natural habitat and reduce aggression. Requires live and frozen meaty foods; hard-shelled food items essential to manage beak growth. Very sensitive to water quality.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial. Best kept alone or in a well-planted species tank. Males are particularly territorial toward each other.
dimorphism: Males develop vivid red eyes and more intense striping; females are plainer with duller colouration.

origin: Southeast Asia (Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Meklong basins)
description: A compact, spotted puffer from the river systems of Thailand, Laos, and Cambodia, inhabiting slow-moving backwaters and floodplain pools. Covered in a pattern of irregular dark spots and blotches on a pale body. Prone to fin-nipping and should be kept alone or only with fast-moving robust fish. A sandy substrate is preferred as this species enjoys digging. Hard-shelled invertebrates such as snails are essential to keep the fused beak trimmed. Sensitive to nitrogenous waste accumulation.
behavior: Aggressive fin-nipper; best kept as a solitary specimen. Enjoys digging in sandy substrate. Most active during daylight hours.
dimorphism: Females tend to be rounder-bellied than males.

origin: South America (Xingu and Fresco rivers, Brazil)
description: One of the most sought-after freshwater stingrays, with a striking black disc dotted with bold white spots. Comes from the fast, clear, oxygen-rich waters of the Xingu River in Brazil. Requires an enormous aquarium with emphasis on floor space over height and a soft sandy substrate to prevent damage to its underside. The venomous tail spine demands extreme caution during maintenance. Highly sensitive to poor water quality; powerful filtration and frequent water changes are non-negotiable. A long-term specialist commitment.
behavior: Generally docile toward other large fish but will consume anything that fits in its mouth. The venomous tail spine is a serious hazard; handle with extreme care during tank maintenance.
dimorphism: Males have claspers visible near the pelvic fins; females are generally larger-bodied.
reproduction: A live-bearer (matrotrophic viviparity) giving birth to litters of roughly 1-8 fully-formed pups after a gestation of about 9-12 weeks.

origin: South America (Tocantins and Araguaia rivers, Brazil)
description: A beautifully marked freshwater stingray from the Tocantins River basin of Brazil, with a dark disc covered in pale round spots. Though smaller than the black diamond stingray, it still demands a very large aquarium with maximum floor space and a soft sandy substrate. Hardy and prolific once established, making it one of the more rewarding rays for experienced keepers. Requires a mature, heavily filtered tank and feeding 2–3 times daily. The venomous tail spine is a hazard during maintenance.
behavior: Generally peaceful toward suitably sized tankmates but predatory toward small fish. Sensitive to stress and poor water quality. The venomous tail spine requires cautious handling.
dimorphism: Males have claspers near the pelvic fins; females tend to be broader-bodied.
reproduction: A live-bearer (matrotrophic viviparity) producing litters of around 1-11 pups after a gestation of about 9-12 weeks.

origin: South America (Amazon and Orinoco basins; Suriname, Guyana, French Guiana)
description: The most widely distributed freshwater stingray in South America and often considered the best entry point for new ray keepers due to its manageable size and relative hardiness. Has a smooth, mottled-brown disc with a reticulated pattern. Requires a large aquarium with a fine sandy substrate and soft, acidic water. Feed 2–3 times daily with meaty frozen foods. Powerful filtration is essential due to the high feeding frequency and metabolic waste. The tail spine is venomous — handle with care during maintenance.
behavior: Generally peaceful toward suitable tankmates but predatory toward small fish and invertebrates. Active on the substrate; needs ample unobstructed floor space.
dimorphism: Males have claspers near pelvic fins; females are typically broader-bodied.

description: A mid-sized freshwater stingray from the Paraná-Paraguay River system, patterned with small yellow spots across a dark disc. Compared to Amazonian rays, it prefers slightly harder, cooler water reflecting its subtropical South American origin. Requires a large aquarium with ample floor space and a fine sandy substrate. Venomous tail spine demands careful handling during maintenance. Feed meaty frozen foods 2–3 times daily and maintain near-zero nitrite levels.
behavior: Generally peaceful toward robust, similarly-sized tankmates but predatory toward small fish. Spends most time resting on the substrate. Venomous tail spine is a hazard during maintenance.
dimorphism: Males have claspers visible near the pelvic fins; females tend to be broader-bodied.

description: A beautiful freshwater stingray from the Tapajós River in Brazil, patterned with small pearl-like white spots across a dark-brown disc. Large-growing and a long-term specialist commitment. Requires an enormous aquarium with maximum floor space and a fine sandy substrate. Very sensitive to nitrogenous waste — feed 2–3 times daily with meaty foods but maintain a strict filtration and water-change regime. The venomous tail spine is a serious hazard during tank maintenance.
behavior: Generally docile toward appropriately-sized tankmates but predatory toward small fish. Active on the substrate, requiring ample unobstructed floor space. Venomous tail spine demands cautious handling.
dimorphism: Males have claspers near the pelvic fins; females are typically larger-bodied.

origin: South America (middle and lower Amazon, Tocantins, and Pará rivers, Brazil)
description: A beautifully patterned stingray from the lower Amazon and Tocantins basins, with a rough, raspy disc texture and intricate mosaic markings. Prefers warmer water than most of its relatives. Extremely sensitive to nitrite — even trace levels suppress feeding. Requires a large aquarium with fine sand, powerful biological filtration, and meticulous water changes. Feed 2–3 times daily. The venomous tail spine is a serious hazard; always handle with extreme care.
behavior: Generally calm toward suitably-sized companions but predatory toward small fish. Sensitive to nitrite above 0.025 ppm; deteriorating water quality causes rapid loss of appetite.
dimorphism: Males have claspers near the pelvic fins; females tend to be broader across the disc.

description: Australia's own arowana-like bony tongue, closely related to the Asian arowana. Silvery scales edged in pink give this fish a stunning appearance. A powerful, surface-orientated predator that will consume any fish or creature that fits in its mouth and will jump at any opportunity — a heavy, tightly-fitting lid is non-negotiable. Requires an enormous aquarium with excellent filtration and near-pristine water conditions. Best kept alone. Legal to keep unlike most of its Asian relatives.
behavior: Highly territorial and predatory. Must be kept alone; will attack and consume tankmates. A strong jumper — lid must be weighted and sealed.
dimorphism: Females tend to be slightly deeper-bodied; males may show more vivid colouration during spawning season.

origin: Africa (Senegal, Niger, Volta, Chad, and Congo basins)
description: The smallest commonly kept mormyrid, with a distinctive downward-pointing proboscis used to probe soft substrate for invertebrates. Like all mormyrids, it generates and detects weak electric fields for navigation and communication in murky water. One of the few mormyrid species bred consistently in captivity, making it more accessible than most relatives. Nocturnal and shy; requires soft substrate, hiding spots, and subdued lighting. Rarely accepts dried foods — live or frozen invertebrates are essential. Can be territorial toward other mormyrids.
behavior: Generally peaceful toward dissimilar fish but aggressive toward other mormyrids. Nocturnal. Uses weak electric organ discharges for navigation and communication.
dimorphism: Males develop a notch in the anal fin during breeding season; females are typically plumper when gravid.

origin: South America (coastal rivers of northeastern South America)
description: An enigmatic gymnotiform knifefish with a dramatically elongated, pencil-like body and a distinctive downward-pointing trumpet-shaped snout. Unlike the aggressive black ghost knifefish, this species is peaceful and can be kept in groups. Produces a weak electric field for navigation and communication. Nocturnal and shy; requires a fine sandy substrate for digging, hiding spots, and subdued lighting. Rarely accepts dried foods — live and frozen invertebrates are the staple. Excellent filtration and regular water changes are essential.
behavior: Peaceful toward similarly-sized fish; can be kept in small groups. Nocturnal; uses weak electric organ discharges for navigation. Digs in sandy substrate.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism reported in aquarium literature.

origin: Africa (Congo River basin; Cameroon, DRC, Angola)
description: A compact mormyrid with a dramatically curved, trunk-like snout used to probe sand and crevices for invertebrates. Like all mormyrids, it generates weak electric organ discharges that are unique to each individual — essentially an electric fingerprint. Highly sensitive to water quality; requires pristine soft, acidic conditions and a fine sandy substrate. Cannot be kept with other mormyrids. Nocturnal and initially shy, but becomes bolder with time. Rarely accepts dried foods.
behavior: Peaceful toward dissimilar fish but aggressive toward other mormyrids. Nocturnal. Uses unique electric organ discharges for individual recognition and communication.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism in aquarium literature.

origin: Africa (lower Zambezi, Shire, and Pungwe rivers; upper and central Congo; Lake Rukwa; east-flowing rivers of Tanzania)
description: A moderately-sized mormyrid from southern and east-central Africa, sometimes called the Bulldog due to its blunt, rounded snout. Generates weak electric organ discharges for navigation and communication in the murky waters it inhabits. Nocturnal and best kept alone without other mormyrids in the tank. Requires soft substrate, hiding spots, and low lighting. Accepts live and frozen invertebrates but rarely takes dried foods. Sensitive to poor water quality; excellent filtration is essential.
behavior: Peaceful toward non-mormyrid tankmates but aggressive toward other mormyrids and electric fish. Nocturnal; most active after lights out. Uses weak electric organ discharges for navigation.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism in aquarium literature.

origin: Central and South America (Costa Rica to Argentina; widespread across river basins)
description: One of South America's most widespread freshwater predators, the Wolf Fish is a powerful ambush hunter with impressive dentition and a large, gaping mouth. Despite its size and predatory nature, it is a relatively sedentary fish that spends daylight hours motionless at the bottom, launching sudden strikes at passing prey. Can learn to accept thawed whole fish and other meaty foods over time, eliminating the need for live feeders. A secured lid is essential — this species can leap. Best kept as a solitary specimen.
behavior: Highly aggressive and predatory; must be kept alone. Nocturnal ambush predator — relatively still during daylight. Strong jumper; secure all tank openings.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism; females may be slightly bulkier when gravid.

origin: South America (Trinidad, Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, northern Brazil)
description: A small but remarkably skilled ambush predator that floats motionless among plants, mimicking a dead leaf, until prey ventures within reach. Despite its diminutive size, it can consume fish almost its own length thanks to its highly protrusible jaws. Requires a heavily planted tank with dim lighting and soft, slightly acidic water. Keep only with fish too large to be swallowed. Refuses most dried foods — live or freshly killed invertebrates and small fish are essential. One of the most interesting micro-predators in the hobby.
behavior: Slow-moving ambush predator. Floats motionless imitating dead leaves; strikes explosively at prey. Generally peaceful toward fish too large to consume.
dimorphism: Males display darker colouration during breeding; females are more uniformly coloured and become gravid.

description: A superb ambush predator from the slow rivers and floodplains of southern Asia, with a flattened, leaf-like body that provides exceptional camouflage among marginal vegetation. Notably more active than most other leaffish species and will forage openly during the day. Can consume fish nearly its own size thanks to a highly protrusible mouth. Unlike closely related African leaffish, this species generally tolerates others of its kind. Requires live or freshly-frozen foods — rarely accepts prepared dry food. Needs a well-planted tank with ample hiding spots.
behavior: Predatory toward smaller fish; generally compatible with similarly-sized species. More active and bold than most leaffish. Will consume anything that fits in its mouth.
dimorphism: Males develop more intense colouration and may be slightly slimmer; females become noticeably rounder when gravid.

origin: Southeast Asia (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo)
description: A boldly striped leaffish from across Southeast Asia, inhabiting slow rivers, floodplains, and peat swamps. Larger and more robustly built than most leaffish relatives, with distinctive vertical banding. An opportunistic predator that will consume small fish; tank mates should be chosen accordingly. Unlike many leaffish, this species can often be conditioned to accept pellets and other prepared foods, making it more manageable. Provides excellent cover through dense planting.
behavior: Semi-aggressive; predatory toward small fish but generally peaceful toward similarly-sized species. Can be kept in groups if given ample space and hiding spots.
dimorphism: Males are typically more colourful and develop more intense banding; females are plumper when gravid.

origin: South America (Amazon basin; Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana; lower Orinoco)
description: One of the largest and most formidable freshwater predators in South America, capable of reaching nearly a meter in captivity and requiring an indoor pond or massive aquarium for long-term housing. Armed with impressive fang-like teeth and an enormous gape, the Giant Wolf Fish is an apex ambush predator. It is not aggressive in the traditional sense — it simply consumes anything it can fit in its mouth and sits motionless the rest of the time. A lifelong commitment for dedicated specialist keepers only.
behavior: Extreme apex predator; must be kept alone. Largely sedentary — spends most time motionless near the bottom. Strikes prey explosively. A secured lid is essential.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism; females may be slightly bulkier when gravid.

description: A striking glassfish from fast-flowing headwater streams of Myanmar, recognisable by the pronounced nuchal hump that develops on mature males. Unlike the common glassfish (*Parambassis ranga*), the humphead requires highly oxygenated, flowing water and is extremely sensitive to nitrate accumulation — conditions that make it a challenge for even experienced aquarists. Best kept in a species tank or with robust similarly-sized schooling fish. Must be kept in groups of eight or more. Rarely accepts dried foods.
behavior: Peaceful but active and fast-moving; requires strong current. Males can be argumentative with each other but less so in large groups. Sensitive to still, warm, nitrate-rich water.
dimorphism: Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump behind the head; females are plumper-bodied without the hump.
reproduction: Breeding has not yet been achieved in aquaria.

origin: Southeast Asia (Mekong basin in Laos and Thailand; Salween and Irrawaddy basins)
description: A diminutive, jewel-like predator from the slow-moving waters and flooded forest margins of mainland Southeast Asia. Males are brilliantly coloured in deep reds and iridescent blues; females are considerably plainer. Related to the scarlet badis (*Dario dario*) but slightly larger and from a broader geographic range. Extremely reclusive and shy; requires a densely planted tank with many small caves. Cannot compete for food with active or boisterous fish. Refuses most dried food — live or frozen micro-invertebrates are essential.
behavior: Reclusive and territorial toward conspecifics; males will fight. Peaceful toward dissimilar fish but cannot compete with active feeders. Hides frequently in caves and dense plants.
dimorphism: Males are brilliantly coloured in crimson and iridescent blue-green; females are drab and brownish.

origin: Asia (domesticated ornamental variety; wild ancestor from East Asia)
description: Domesticated ornamental variants of the Asian carp, selectively bred in Japan for centuries into hundreds of named colour varieties. True pond fish that require enormous volumes of well-oxygenated water — adult koi are simply too large for conventional aquariums and will only thrive in large outdoor ponds or purpose-built indoor systems. Despite this, young koi are frequently sold in aquarium shops. Extremely hardy and long-lived; some specimens exceed 30 years. Produce significant waste and require powerful pond filtration.
behavior: Peaceful omnivore; thrives in groups. Social and will interact with keepers at feeding time. Unsuitable for aquaria as adults — requires a large pond.
dimorphism: Mature females are noticeably rounder in the abdomen; males may develop white tubercles on the gill covers (pectoral fins area) during breeding season.

origin: Eurasia (widespread from western Europe to Siberia)
description: The wild ancestor of the domestic goldfish and a native of European freshwaters, the crucian carp is a hardy, cold-water fish with exceptional tolerance for oxygen-depleted and acidic waters — it can survive under ice for months by switching to anaerobic metabolism. A purely outdoor pond or cold-water aquarium species best kept in groups in large, cool, well-oxygenated water. Not a tropical tank fish. Peaceful community member that coexists easily with other cold-water species like goldfish and tench.
behavior: Peaceful and sociable; best kept in groups. Cold-water species — unsuitable for tropical tanks. Forages along the bottom and mid-water.
dimorphism: Females are typically deeper-bodied; males may develop breeding tubercles on the head during spawning season.

origin: North America (New Brunswick to Georgia; Great Lakes and Mississippi basin; widely introduced)
description: One of the most brilliantly coloured North American freshwater fish, with a kaleidoscope of blue, green, orange, and red markings. A native North American sunfish well suited to cold-water aquariums and outdoor ponds. Males in breeding colour are stunning but also highly territorial — provide ample space and structure to break lines of sight. A specialised snail-crusher with pharyngeal teeth adapted for hard prey. Requires cool water; unsuitable for tropical aquariums. Often collected locally rather than purchased.
behavior: Semi-aggressive; males are territorial, especially during breeding. Peaceful in well-spaced setups with compatible cold-water species. Predatory toward invertebrates and small fish.
dimorphism: Males display more vivid colouration with a distinctive red-orange spot on the ear flap; females are more muted in colour.
reproduction: A colonial nest-builder: the male excavates a shallow nest in the substrate, often among others, and guards the eggs and fry.

origin: North America (St. Lawrence and Great Lakes basins; Mississippi River system; widely introduced)
description: North America's most commonly caught sport fish and a familiar native sunfish, recognisable by its bright blue-purple gill cover flap and olive-to-yellow body. More tolerant of warm water than most sunfish relatives, making it viable for temperate aquariums across a wider temperature range. A bold, interactive fish that quickly becomes conditioned to hand feeding. Territorial during breeding but manageable in spacious, structured tanks. Often collected locally from lakes and rivers.
behavior: Semi-aggressive, especially during breeding. Territorial toward conspecifics but generally manageable with proper space. Forages actively in mid-water and near the bottom.
dimorphism: Males develop more vivid colouration during breeding and have a distinctive dark blue-black ear flap; females are more olive-toned.

origin: Captive hybrid (parentage debated; likely Taiwan origin in 1980s)
description: A popular artificially-bred cichlid hybrid developed in Taiwan, notable for its rotund, heart-shaped body and permanently 'smiling' mouth — a result of selective breeding that gives it a distinctive but deformed beak that limits its ability to bite. Hardy and personable despite its controversial origins. The small, permanently open mouth means it cannot feed on standard cichlid pellets efficiently; sinking soft pellets and frozen foods work best. Develops a genuine bond with keepers and recognises its owner. Avoids the intense aggression of its cichlid ancestors.
behavior: Semi-aggressive; less territorial than typical large cichlids. Social and interactive with keepers. Can be kept with similarly-sized peaceful to semi-aggressive fish.
dimorphism: Males and females are difficult to distinguish visually; females are typically slightly rounder in the abdomen when gravid.

origin: Europe and Asia (ornamental variety of the ide; widely introduced)
description: A golden-orange ornamental form of the European ide, prized as an elegant, surface-active pond fish. Fast-swimming and highly social, orfe should be kept in shoals of at least three and given a large, well-oxygenated pond — they are extremely demanding of dissolved oxygen and intolerant of stagnant water. Too large and active for aquaria as adults. Will take insects from the surface and can prey on very small fish. Long-lived and hardy in cool water, but among the first pond fish to suffer in low-oxygen conditions.
behavior: Active, fast-swimming surface shoaler; keep in groups of three or more. Can prey on very small fish. Highly sensitive to low oxygen — needs a large, well-aerated pond.
dimorphism: Males are slimmer and develop breeding tubercles on the head; females are deeper-bodied, especially when gravid.

origin: North America (widespread from Canada to Mexico; introduced widely)
description: A pinkish-orange ornamental form of the fathead minnow, one of the hardiest and most adaptable freshwater fish in the hobby. Frequently sold inexpensively as feeder fish, but makes a charming, peaceful resident of unheated and pond aquariums in its own right. Extraordinarily tolerant of temperature swings, poor water, and low oxygen. Best kept in a shoal of six or more cool-water companions. Males develop a fleshy 'fathead' and breeding tubercles and guard eggs laid on the underside of surfaces.
behavior: Peaceful, active shoaler; keep in groups of six or more. Extremely hardy and adaptable. Males become territorial around nest sites during breeding and guard the eggs.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop a swollen, dark head with white breeding tubercles and a fleshy nape pad; females are smaller and plainer.

origin: East Asia (China, Taiwan, Russia; introduced elsewhere)
description: A small, peaceful temperate cyprinid that develops dazzling iridescent pink-and-blue spawning colours in males. A subtropical species that benefits from a cooler winter rest, making it ideal for unheated aquaria and ponds. Famous for its remarkable breeding strategy: females use a long ovipositor to lay eggs inside the gill cavity of living freshwater mussels, where the young develop in safety. A peaceful shoaling fish best kept in groups of six or more with sand substrate and good water flow.
behavior: Peaceful shoaling species; keep in groups of six or more. Prefers cooler water with a winter rest. Requires living mussels to breed.
dimorphism: Spawning males are vivid iridescent pink and blue; females are silvery and develop a long trailing ovipositor when ready to breed.
reproduction: A brood parasite of freshwater mussels: the female uses a long ovipositor to lay her eggs inside a living mussel's gill chamber, where the male's sperm fertilises them and the young develop before leaving the host.

origin: Central and eastern Europe, northern Asia Minor
description: A peaceful native European cyprinid and a protected species across much of its range. Like its Asian relative the rosy bitterling, it relies on living freshwater mussels to reproduce — females deposit eggs inside the mussel's gill chamber via a long ovipositor. A coldwater shoaling fish well suited to unheated aquaria and garden ponds, showing its best colours after a cool winter rest. Hardy and undemanding within a temperate range. Keep in groups of six or more with dense planting and a mature, well-filtered setup.
behavior: Peaceful shoaling fish; keep in groups of six or more. Coldwater species that benefits from a winter rest. Requires living mussels to spawn.
dimorphism: Spawning males show a violet-blue sheen and reddish fins; females are plainer and develop a long ovipositor when ready to breed.
reproduction: A brood parasite of freshwater mussels: the female uses a long ovipositor to lay her eggs inside a living mussel, where the male's sperm fertilises them and the young develop for about a month before leaving the host.

origin: North America (Mobile Bay drainage; Georgia, Alabama, Tennessee, USA)
description: A dazzling North American minnow that, in breeding condition, rivals tropical fish for colour — males flush with iridescent pinks, blues, and purples and develop bright red-tipped fins. A coolwater shoaling species from clear, well-oxygenated streams of the Mobile Bay drainage. Needs cooler temperatures than most tropical community fish and shows its best colour and behaviour in large groups. Keep in a shoal of six or more in a planted, well-filtered tank with gentle current. An excellent choice for an unheated temperate aquarium.
behavior: Peaceful, active shoaler; keep in groups of six or more for best colour and behaviour. Prefers cool, well-oxygenated water with gentle current.
dimorphism: Breeding males are intensely coloured with iridescent pink-blue bodies and red-tipped fins; females are more silvery and subdued.

origin: North America (Mississippi basin and Gulf drainages; widely introduced)
description: A hardy North American minnow whose breeding males turn steel-blue with rosy-red fins, making them a striking choice for unheated aquaria. An active, fast-swimming shoaler from creeks and rivers across the central USA, where it has become invasive well outside its native range. Best kept in a group of six or more in a spacious, mature tank with good current and well-oxygenated water. Long-finned or slow tankmates are best avoided, as red shiners can be nippy. Undemanding and adaptable within a cool temperate range.
behavior: Active, fast-swimming shoaler; keep in groups of six or more. Can nip long-finned or slow tankmates. Prefers cool, well-oxygenated water with current.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop a steel-blue body, rosy-red fins, and prominent white breeding tubercles on the head; females are plainer and silvery.
reproduction: An egg scatterer that broadcasts its eggs into crevices and onto rocks or plants over several batches through the season, with no parental care.

origin: Eurasia (widespread from western Europe to East Asia)
description: A small, streamlined shoaling fish native to the cool, fast-flowing streams and lakes of Europe and northern Asia. A true coldwater species that does not tolerate warm water — it requires highly oxygenated, cool conditions and may need a chiller in summer. Breeding males develop reddish bellies and contrasting markings. An excellent fish for a stream-style biotope aquarium with strong current and a sand-and-gravel bed. Keep in a shoal of six or more; minnows are highly social and stressed when kept alone.
behavior: Highly social shoaler; keep in groups of six or more. Demands cool, very well-oxygenated water with strong current. Stressed if kept in small numbers or warm water.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop a reddish belly, darker flanks, and white breeding tubercles; females are plainer and rounder when gravid.

origin: Europe (widespread; also Korea; introduced to parts of Britain and Italy)
description: A small, bottom-dwelling European cyprinid with a pair of barbels and a mottled, camouflaged body suited to sandy and gravelly riverbeds. A peaceful, sociable coldwater fish that lives in loose troops and constantly forages over the substrate for small invertebrates. Ideal for a temperate stream-biotope aquarium or pond with cool, well-oxygenated water and a soft sand-and-gravel bed for sifting. Keep in groups of six or more — gudgeon are gregarious and far more confident in company. Completely peaceful toward other fish.
behavior: Peaceful, sociable bottom-dweller; keep in groups of six or more. Forages constantly over sand and gravel. Prefers cool, well-oxygenated water.
dimorphism: Sexes are very similar; breeding males develop white tubercles on the head and pectoral fins, and females are rounder when gravid.

origin: North America (Atlantic and Gulf Slope drainages, New Hampshire to Florida)
