description: A small, jewel-like North American native sunfish marked with dark vertical bands and flecked with iridescent green and gold spots. A peaceful, coolwater fish that thrives in heavily planted, well-maintained tanks and benefits from a cool winter rest. Somewhat sensitive to poor water quality, so requires regular water changes. Prefers live and frozen foods and may be reluctant to accept dried food. Best kept in a small group in a temperate, dimly lit, planted aquarium with plenty of cover. An excellent native-biotope species.
behavior: Peaceful and somewhat shy; does best in small groups with dense planting and cover. Prefers live and frozen foods. Sensitive to poor water quality.
dimorphism: Breeding males are more intensely coloured with brighter iridescent spotting; females are plainer and become rounder when gravid.
reproduction: The male builds a nest in sand or gravel among vegetation and guards the area, though the buoyant eggs tend to drift from the nest.

origin: North America (Atlantic and Gulf Slope drainages, New York to Florida)
description: One of the prettier and most peaceful North American native sunfish, its dark body scattered with glittering iridescent blue spots that catch the light. A timid, coolwater species that finds bright open spaces stressful and prefers heavily planted tanks with shaded retreats. Benefits from a cool winter rest to trigger breeding. Sensitive to poor water quality and should be kept in well-maintained, stable conditions with regular water changes. Best paired with other calm, low-energy coolwater natives. Prefers live and frozen foods.
behavior: Peaceful and timid; prefers heavily planted tanks with shade and cover. Best kept in small groups with calm tankmates. Sensitive to poor water quality.
dimorphism: Breeding males show more intense iridescent blue spotting; females are plainer and become rounder when gravid.

origin: North America (Atlantic and Gulf Slope drainages, New Jersey to Florida)
description: Arguably the most striking of the North American native sunfish, marked with bold black vertical bands and red-tinged front fin rays, resembling a tiny coldwater angelfish. A true coldwater species that must not be kept warm long-term, as heat dramatically shortens its lifespan. A challenging fish for experienced keepers: it is a fussy feeder that usually refuses dried food and demands live or frozen invertebrates. Peaceful and gregarious, best kept in a heavily planted group of five or more, but becomes territorial when spawning. Needs summer cooling in warm climates.
behavior: Peaceful and sociable; keep in groups of five or more. Becomes territorial during spawning. Coldwater species — needs cooling in summer. Often refuses dried foods.
dimorphism: Breeding males are more intensely coloured with brighter fin markings; females are plainer and become rounder when gravid.

origin: North America (North Carolina to Alabama; northern Everglades, USA)
description: A tiny, secretive nano fish from the detritus-rich swamps and ditches of the southeastern USA. Despite its drab appearance at rest, breeding males perform striking courtship displays, flashing iridescent blue. A temperate species that benefits from cooler conditions rather than constant tropical warmth. Best kept in a small, heavily planted species tank with soft, slightly acidic water and minimal current. Like most pygmy sunfish, it strongly prefers live and frozen micro-foods and may ignore flakes entirely. Ideal for a quiet, planted nano aquarium.
behavior: Peaceful but territorial in a micro sense; males defend small display areas. Shy and best kept in a heavily planted species tank. Strongly prefers live and frozen foods.
dimorphism: Courting males turn near-black with iridescent blue fin spangling; females remain mottled brown and are plumper.

origin: North America (Georgia to central Florida; Okefenokee Swamp region, USA)
description: A diminutive but surprisingly fierce micro-predator from the blackwater swamps of the southeastern USA. Breeding males develop intense iridescent blue colouration and display dramatically. Though tiny, it is aggressive and territorial in miniature, so it is best kept as a species-only display. A temperate fish that tolerates a wide range but prefers cooler water and dense planting. Requires live foods — California blackworms, daphnia, grindal worms — and typically refuses flakes and frozen food, making it a fish for dedicated keepers. A fascinating subject for a planted nano biotope.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial despite its tiny size; best kept species-only. Males display vividly during breeding. Requires live foods and dense planting.
dimorphism: Courting males turn dark with brilliant iridescent blue markings; females remain mottled brown and plainer.

origin: North America (Great Lakes and Mississippi basins; Ontario to Alabama, USA)
description: One of the most colourful freshwater fish in North America, the breeding male a riot of electric blue and orange bands. A true coldwater stream specialist that inhabits fast, clear, well-oxygenated riffles and spends its time darting between rocks on the bottom — it lacks a functional swim bladder and rests on the substrate. Requires cold, highly oxygenated water with strong current and will languish in warm or still conditions. A challenging but rewarding native species best suited to a coolwater riffle-biotope tank. Needs live and frozen foods to maintain colour and condition.
behavior: Peaceful and shy; rests on the bottom among rocks, darting between cover. Requires cold, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated water. Best fed live and frozen foods.
dimorphism: Breeding males are vividly coloured with electric-blue and orange banding; females are mottled brown and far plainer.
reproduction: A spring spawner that congregates over pebbly stream margins to spawn, the female laying her eggs among the gravel with no extended parental care.

origin: North America (Lake Erie and Mississippi basins; Michigan to Texas, USA)
description: A brilliantly coloured little darter whose breeding males display vivid blue-green bars and the orange throat that gives the species its name. A coldwater stream fish from the central USA, it rests on the bottom among rocks and gravel in clear, cool, well-oxygenated riffles. Like other darters it has a reduced swim bladder and lives on the substrate. Males become aggressive toward one another during the breeding season, so provide ample space and cover. Requires cold, flowing water and a diet of live and frozen invertebrates. A jewel of the temperate native aquarium.
behavior: Generally peaceful but males are aggressive toward each other during breeding. Bottom-dwelling; rests among rocks. Requires cold, oxygenated, flowing water and live foods.
dimorphism: Breeding males show vivid blue-green bars and an orange throat and belly; females are mottled brown and much plainer.

origin: North America (Atlantic, Great Lakes, and Mississippi basins; Quebec to Alabama, USA)
description: A small, scrappy North American riffle darter, more subtly coloured than its rainbow relatives but full of character. Adults inhabit shallow, swift riffles over gravel and cobble, resting on the bottom and darting between stones. Males are devoted egg-guarders, defending their nests under flat rocks against intruders many times their size. A true coldwater stream specialist that requires cool, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated water and a diet of live and frozen invertebrates to thrive. Best suited to a temperate riffle-biotope aquarium.
behavior: Bottom-dwelling and territorial, especially males guarding eggs. Requires cool, fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water. Best fed live and frozen foods.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop knob-tipped dorsal spines used to mimic eggs at the nest and are darker; females are plainer with a paler belly.

origin: North America (South Carolina to Texas; lower Coastal Plain, USA)
description: A hardy, attractive North American killifish from the still and slow waters of the southeastern USA, its golden-flecked body lit with iridescent green-gold spots and red speckling in mature males. A peaceful, surface-orientated fish well suited to subtropical and unheated aquaria, even tolerating an outdoor summer in many regions. Adaptable to a wide pH range and easy to feed, accepting flakes and pellets readily. Like most topminnows it is a capable jumper, so a secure lid is essential. An excellent, undemanding native species for a planted tank.
behavior: Peaceful surface-dweller; keep in small groups. Hardy and adaptable. A capable jumper — keep the tank well covered.
dimorphism: Males are more vividly coloured with red speckling and brighter gold flecking; females are plainer and rounder-bodied.

origin: North America (throughout Florida; sporadic along Atlantic coast, USA)
description: A small, peaceful North American killifish prized for the electric-blue, red, and black finnage of breeding males, which rivals many tropical fish for colour. Native to the heavily vegetated springs and streams of Florida, it thrives in well-planted aquaria and adapts to a wide temperature range, needing no heater in most homes. Somewhat skittish, it shows more confidence with dither fish and dense cover. Sensitive to abrupt water changes, so acclimate carefully. An excellent choice for a planted nano or temperate community tank.
behavior: Peaceful and somewhat skittish; more confident in groups and with dither fish. Prefers heavily planted tanks. Sensitive to sudden changes in water chemistry.
dimorphism: Males display vivid blue, red, and black dorsal and anal fins; females have clear or pale fins and are plainer overall.

origin: North America (St. Lawrence-Great Lakes, Hudson Bay, and Mississippi basins; Canada and USA)
description: A tough, unassuming little fish that is actually a member of the pike family, not a true minnow. It lurks motionless among dense vegetation, ambushing any prey small enough to swallow. Remarkably hardy, it survives in oxygen-poor, acidic, heavily vegetated swamp waters that few other fish tolerate and can even gulp air. A coolwater species ideal for a temperate, dimly lit, densely planted aquarium with soft, acidic water and minimal flow. Generally peaceful toward fish too large to eat, but a capable predator of smaller tankmates.
behavior: An ambush predator that lurks in plant thickets; will eat fish small enough to swallow. Tough and not easily bullied, but generally peaceful outside spawning. Tolerates low oxygen and acidic water.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar; females tend to be larger and rounder, while breeding males may show a greenish sheen on the fins.

origin: Eurasia (Black, Azov, and Caspian Sea drainages; Siberia)
description: The smallest of the true sturgeons, yet still a large, ancient pond fish that grows far too big for any normal aquarium — it belongs in a large, deep, heavily filtered pond of several thousand litres. A primitive, bottom-cruising species with rows of bony scutes and barbel-fringed snout for detecting food on the substrate. Demands cool, very well-oxygenated, fast-moving water and will perish quickly in warm or stagnant conditions. Peaceful and long-lived, but a serious specialist commitment requiring powerful filtration and constant aeration, especially in summer.
behavior: Peaceful, constantly cruising the bottom in search of food. Requires cool, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated water — sensitive to warmth and low oxygen. A pond fish, not an aquarium species, as an adult.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism; females grow larger and become noticeably rounder when carrying eggs.
reproduction: A migratory broadcast spawner that releases tens of thousands of eggs over gravel in spring with no parental care; it is not bred in home aquaria.

origin: Asia (Siberian rivers; Ob, Yenisei, Lena, Lake Baikal)
description: A large, fast-growing sturgeon commonly sold for garden ponds but which quickly outgrows all but the very largest systems — a mature fish needs a deep pond of well over 11,000 litres. An ancient, armoured bottom-feeder that cruises constantly along the substrate using its barbels to detect food. Demands cool, exceptionally well-oxygenated water and dedicated sturgeon food with high protein content; it cannot compete with greedy pond fish at feeding time. A serious, decades-long commitment that is too often bought small without appreciation for its eventual size and oxygen needs.
behavior: Peaceful, constantly cruising the bottom for food. Cannot compete with fast surface-feeders. Requires cool, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated water and dies quickly in warm or stagnant conditions.
dimorphism: No reliable external dimorphism; females grow larger and rounder when carrying eggs.

origin: Eurasia (throughout Europe and Siberia; widely introduced)
description: A handsome, boldly striped native European predator with spiny dorsal fins and red-orange pelvic fins. A coolwater fish of lakes and slow rivers, it is an active daytime hunter that will take any fish up to about a third of its own length. Best kept in a large, cool, well-oxygenated aquarium or pond with strong filtration and fine sand. Can be kept with other robust fish too large to be eaten, but is somewhat territorial toward its own kind. Long-lived and rewarding for the temperate native or pond specialist, but demands space and pristine, oxygen-rich water.
behavior: Active daytime predator; takes fish up to a third its length. Somewhat territorial toward conspecifics. Requires cool, well-oxygenated water with good current.
dimorphism: No strong external dimorphism; females grow larger and become noticeably rounder when carrying eggs.

origin: Eurasia (most of Europe and western Asia; widely introduced)
description: A stout, dark-green European cyprinid with tiny scales, a slimy coat, and a pair of small barbels, traditionally nicknamed the 'doctor fish'. A peaceful, sociable bottom-dweller that spends its time grubbing through the substrate for invertebrates and is famously tolerant of low oxygen, warm summers, and a wide range of water conditions. A classic pond fish and one of the hardiest available — also offered as an ornamental golden form. Keep in groups of four or more in a large pond or spacious coolwater tank with soft substrate and dense planting it can root through.
behavior: Peaceful, sociable bottom-dweller; keep in groups of four or more. Roots through substrate for food. Extremely hardy and tolerant of low oxygen and warm water. A capable jumper.
dimorphism: Males have larger, thicker pelvic fins with a noticeably enlarged second ray; females are plainer-finned and rounder when gravid.

origin: Captive hybrid (Texas cichlid crossed with red parrot/Amphilophus lineages)
description: A prized man-made hybrid cichlid bred for intense red, orange, and pearl colouration over a large, robust body. Created by crossing the Texas cichlid with red-coloured Amphilophus and parrot lineages, then selectively bred through a 'fading' process where juveniles turn from black to brilliant red. Notably more aggressive than a true Texas cichlid — closer in temperament to a flowerhorn — and should be treated as a solitary or carefully managed fish among large, robust tankmates. Hardy and personable, but demands a big tank, powerful filtration, and an owner prepared for serious aggression.
behavior: Highly aggressive and territorial — among the most pugnacious aquarium cichlids. Best kept alone or only with large, robust fish. Digger that will rearrange substrate and decor.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and may develop a nuchal hump; females are smaller and often show black spots in the dorsal fin.

description: An elegant, fairy-like Tanganyikan rock-dweller with a pale yellow body, lyre-shaped fins edged in white and blue, and a dark gill spot. Famous as a model species for studying cooperative breeding — offspring stay with their parents and help raise subsequent broods, forming complex social colonies. Peaceful for a cichlid but territorial around its chosen cave, especially when spawning. Requires hard, alkaline water and plenty of rockwork forming caves and broken sightlines. An excellent, characterful introduction to Tanganyikan cichlids that does best kept as a pair or colony.
behavior: Peaceful toward dissimilar fish but territorial around its cave, especially when breeding. Forms cooperative colonies where older offspring help raise younger broods. Needs rockwork with caves.
dimorphism: Males grow slightly larger with longer fin extensions; females are smaller and rounder when in breeding condition.

description: One of the tiniest and most charismatic cichlids in the hobby, a shell-dwelling Tanganyikan that lives its whole life in and around an empty snail shell. The popular gold morph glows with metallic yellow. Despite its minute size it has an outsized personality, fiercely defending its shell — it will dig sand out from beneath the shell and rearrange its territory constantly. Provide an open sand bed with several empty snail shells (more shells than fish). A superb nano cichlid for hard, alkaline water; can share a tank with rock- and open-water Tanganyikans that occupy different zones.
behavior: Territorial around its shell, which it defends vigorously and constantly rearranges by digging. Otherwise peaceful toward fish occupying other zones. Males may keep harems of females in neighbouring shells.
dimorphism: Males are markedly larger (to ~6 cm) than females (to ~3.5 cm); males often show bolder finnage.
reproduction: A shell-dweller: the female lays her eggs inside a buried snail shell and guards and fans them until they hatch in about three days, the fry going free-swimming around day ten before being moved on.

origin: Africa (endemic to the Zambian shores of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A small, hardy Tanganyikan cichlid with a pale body, sky-blue eyes, and red-edged dorsal fin. Intermediate in lifestyle between a true shell-dweller and a rock-dweller, it readily spawns in either shells or rocky crevices and excavates pits in the sand. Relatively peaceful and undemanding for a Tanganyikan, making it a good beginner's Lake cichlid, though it becomes territorial when breeding. Best kept as a bonded pair or small group in hard, alkaline water with a sandy substrate, scattered shells, and rockwork. Readily accepts prepared and frozen foods.
behavior: Relatively peaceful but territorial when breeding. Excavates sand pits and defends a small territory around its chosen shell or crevice. Good for mixed Tanganyikan setups with broken sightlines.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar; males average slightly larger, and females become noticeably rounder when in spawning condition.

origin: Africa (endemic to the south-eastern part of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A handsome, torpedo-shaped Tanganyikan rock-dweller marked with bold dark vertical bars over a pale, lavender-tinged body. Spends its life close to rocky structure, darting into crevices when threatened, and rarely strays far from cover. Territorial toward its own kind, so best kept as a single bonded pair in a tank with abundant stacked rockwork creating caves and broken lines of sight. A predatory species that relishes meaty frozen and live foods. Requires the hard, alkaline, well-filtered water typical of Lake Tanganyika.
behavior: Territorial, especially toward conspecifics; best kept as a single pair. Closely tied to rocky cover and darts into crevices when alarmed. Predatory feeder.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with more pointed, extended fins; females are smaller and rounder, with a shorter genital papilla.

origin: Africa (endemic to northern Lake Tanganyika, DR Congo)
description: A relatively recently described Tanganyikan rock-dweller, the sunflower cichlid has a fawn-coloured body lit with iridescent blue speckling and yellow-flushed fins. A pugnacious species that defends its territory fiercely and brooks no intrusion, particularly from other territorial fish. Best kept as a single pair in a species tank or, in a large aquarium, with other Tanganyikans that occupy different niches such as open-water Cyprichromis. Requires the hard, alkaline water and abundant rockwork of its native lake. Predominantly carnivorous, taking meaty frozen and live foods alongside quality prepared diets.
behavior: Aggressively territorial, especially toward other territorial species; best in a species tank or large mixed setup with niche separation. Stays close to rocky cover.
dimorphism: Dominant males grow notably larger (to ~9 cm) than females; males develop more intense colour and longer finnage.

description: A stocky, big-headed Tanganyikan cichlid with a dark, almost black body and a steep forehead. Despite its modest size it is remarkably pugnacious, holding and defending a rocky or shell territory with disproportionate ferocity — it particularly resents conspecifics of the same sex. Best kept as a single bonded pair in a species tank or with robust Tanganyikan tankmates that can hold their own. Provide abundant rockwork, caves, and optionally empty shells for spawning. Requires the hard, alkaline water of its native lake; readily accepts prepared, frozen, and live foods.
behavior: Extremely territorial for its size, especially toward same-sex conspecifics. Best kept as a single pair or with robust tankmates. Defends a rock or shell territory vigorously.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with a more pronounced, steeper head profile; females are smaller and rounder when in spawning condition.

description: An elegant, eel-like Tanganyikan rock-dweller with a creamy-white body crossed by dark facial bars and, in some forms, rows of spots along the flanks. Slender and built to slip through rocky crevices, it hunts invertebrates among the lake's rubble zones. Can be quite aggressive, particularly toward its own kind, so is best kept as a single specimen or a bonded pair with only one male, alongside robust tankmates. Requires abundant rockwork forming caves and tunnels, plus the hard, alkaline water typical of Lake Tanganyika. A hearty eater that takes most prepared, frozen, and live foods.
behavior: Aggressive toward conspecifics and intolerant of crowding; keep one male with females or a single pair. Slips through rocky crevices hunting invertebrates. Needs robust tankmates.
dimorphism: Sexes are very similar; males average slightly larger, and sexing usually requires venting or observing breeding behaviour.

origin: Africa (endemic to the west-central coast of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A stocky, algae-grazing Tanganyikan cichlid from the genus famed for its challenging temperament and specialised diet. Like all Tropheus it is intensely aggressive toward its own kind, so it must be kept in a large colony (a dozen or more, heavily skewed toward females) so that aggression is diffused across many targets. Critically, it is a strict herbivore prone to a fatal digestive condition known as 'bloat' if fed protein-rich foods — its diet must be spirulina- and vegetable-based. A demanding fish for experienced Tanganyikan keepers who can provide a large, rocky, well-filtered tank.
behavior: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics; must be kept in a large colony to spread aggression. A maternal mouthbrooder. Prone to fatal bloat if fed high-protein foods.
dimorphism: Sexes are very similar; dominant males may show slightly more intense colour, and sexing typically requires venting.

origin: Africa (endemic to the south-western part of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A large, spectacular aufwuchs-grazing Tanganyikan cichlid with trailing, thread-like fin extensions that give it its name. Among the most aggressive of all Lake Tanganyika cichlids, it relentlessly defends its grazing territory and is brutal toward conspecifics, so it demands a very large tank, a sizable group to spread aggression, and only the most robust tankmates. Like Tropheus it is a specialised algae-rasper that must be fed a high-fibre, low-protein vegetable diet to avoid fatal bloat. A challenging species strictly for experienced Tanganyikan aquarists with the space and dedication it requires.
behavior: Extremely aggressive and territorial; needs a very large tank and a group to diffuse aggression. A specialised algae-grazer prone to bloat on protein-rich diets. Only for experienced keepers.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with longer thread-like fin extensions and more intense colour; females are smaller with shorter fins.

origin: Africa (endemic to the north end of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A powerful, strong-charactered Tanganyikan cichlid with a specialised rasping mouth adapted for scraping unicellular algae from rocks. Like its relatives in the genus, it is intensely territorial and combative — males will not tolerate one another, so a single male must be kept with several females in an expansive, rocky tank. A strict algae-grazer that requires a high-fibre, low-protein vegetable diet to prevent the fatal digestive bloat that plagues aufwuchs-feeding cichlids. A serious, space-hungry species reserved for dedicated, well-informed Tanganyikan specialists.
behavior: Strongly territorial and aggressive; males are intolerant of each other, so keep one male with several females. Needs a large rocky tank. A specialised algae-rasper prone to bloat on rich diets.
dimorphism: Sexes are similar; dominant males grow larger and more intensely coloured, but reliable sexing usually requires venting.

description: A quirky, bottom-hugging Tanganyikan cichlid adapted to life in the lake's surge zone, where it hops over rocks like a freshwater blenny — it has reduced swim-bladder buoyancy and rests on its pectoral fins. Marked with rows of iridescent blue spots over a barred body. A biparental mouthbrooder famous for forming lifelong pair bonds. Conspecific aggression is extreme, so keep only a mated pair unless the tank is very large. Strictly herbivorous and bloat-prone, it needs a vegetable-based diet, plus highly oxygenated, turbulent water and abundant rockwork. A characterful but demanding specialist fish.
behavior: Extreme conspecific aggression; keep only a bonded pair unless the tank is very large. A biparental mouthbrooder that pairs for life. Hops over rocks; needs turbulent, oxygen-rich water and a herbivore diet.
dimorphism: Sexes are very similar in size and colour; reliable sexing requires venting or observing breeding roles.
reproduction: A biparental mouthbrooder: the female first incubates the fertilised eggs, then transfers the larvae to the male's mouth for continued brooding before release.

description: The smallest of the Tanganyikan goby cichlids, a delicate surge-zone specialist with a slender body, a pointed snout, and a jewel-like spot on the dorsal fin. Like its goby-cichlid relatives it has reduced buoyancy and hops along the rocky bottom. A biparental mouthbrooder that forms lasting pairs. Territorial toward its own kind, so best kept as a single pair or, with care, in a larger group in a spacious tank. Strictly herbivorous and bloat-prone — it must be fed a low-protein, vegetable-based diet. Requires turbulent, well-oxygenated water and plenty of rockwork.
behavior: Territorial toward conspecifics; best kept as a bonded pair. A biparental mouthbrooder. Hops over rocks in the surge zone; needs oxygen-rich water and a herbivore diet to avoid bloat.
dimorphism: Sexes are very similar; males may be marginally larger, and reliable sexing requires venting.

origin: Africa (endemic to the southern part of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A graceful, silvery Tanganyikan sand-sifting cichlid that cruises just above the lake's open sand beds, taking mouthfuls of substrate and sifting out tiny invertebrates through its gills. An unusually peaceful Lake cichlid that lives in loose colonies, it requires a deep, fine sand bed it can sift and nest-build in, plus open swimming space rather than heavy rockwork. Best kept as a group with several females per male. Sensitive to water quality and somewhat delicate, so a mature, pristine tank is essential. A serene contrast to the boisterous rock-dwellers of the lake.
behavior: Peaceful, open-sand colony fish; males become territorial only when breeding. Constantly sifts fine sand for food and builds sand nests. Needs open space and a deep sand bed rather than rockwork.
dimorphism: Males develop slightly more intense colour and longer finnage in breeding condition; females are plainer.

origin: Africa (endemic to and widespread in Lake Tanganyika)
description: A dazzling open-sand cichlid of Lake Tanganyika, the breeding male glittering with iridescent blue and yellow as he builds and defends an elaborate sand-crater nest to court passing females. One of the most peaceful sand-sifters, it should be kept in a colony of eight or more with several females per male over a broad, open bed of fine sand. It sifts the substrate for micro-invertebrates and needs open swimming room rather than rockwork. Pristine, well-oxygenated water is essential, as the species is sensitive to deteriorating conditions. A spectacular but demanding display fish for the Tanganyikan specialist.
behavior: Peaceful colony fish; males build and defend sand-crater nests when breeding. Sifts fine sand for food. Needs a large, open sand bed and a group of eight or more with a female-skewed sex ratio.
dimorphism: Breeding males are vividly iridescent blue and yellow and build sand nests; females are silvery and far plainer.

origin: Africa (endemic to the southern half of Lake Tanganyika)
description: A majestic deep-water Tanganyikan cichlid, the most prized 'Zaire blue' form glowing an intense electric blue with bold black bars and a pronounced nuchal hump on mature males. A slow-moving, stately predator that in the wild lives in deep rocky habitat and hunts small fish at night. Despite its size it is relatively peaceful and does best in a group of six or more (one male to several females) in a large, deep tank with rockwork and excellent filtration. Long-lived and slow-growing, it is a centrepiece fish that rewards patience. Feed meaty foods, but avoid feeder fish to prevent disease.
behavior: Relatively peaceful but predatory toward small fish; a slow-moving, stately species. Best kept in groups of six or more with one male to several females. Long-lived and slow-growing.
dimorphism: Mature males grow larger and develop a pronounced nuchal hump on the forehead; females are smaller with a much less prominent hump.
reproduction: A maternal mouthbrooder commonly bred in aquaria, the female incubating the eggs for around 35-50 days and nourishing the developing fry in her mouth.

origin: Africa (endemic to and widespread in Lake Tanganyika)
description: A sleek, torpedo-shaped Tanganyikan predator built for speed and ambush, with a silvery, dark-mottled body. One of the lake's most effective rock-zone hunters, it lurks among the stones and strikes at any fish or invertebrate small enough to swallow. Aggressive and territorial, it should be kept only with robust tankmates too large to be eaten, in a spacious tank with abundant rockwork forming ambush points and territories. Requires the hard, alkaline water of its native lake. A characterful predator for the experienced Tanganyikan keeper who can house it appropriately.
behavior: Aggressive ambush predator; will eat any fish small enough to swallow. Territorial, needs rockwork for ambush points. Keep only with robust tankmates too large to be prey.
dimorphism: Males grow larger; sexes are otherwise similar and reliable sexing usually requires venting.

origin: Africa (endemic to Lake Tanganyika, Zambia to Tanzania)
description: A boldly patterned Tanganyikan predator, sometimes called the blue-spotted compressed cichlid for the rows of iridescent spots over its compressed, predatory body. A rock-zone ambush hunter that establishes territories among crevices and strikes at small fish and invertebrates. Highly aggressive and best kept in a species tank or with other robust Tanganyikans that can hold their own. Provide plenty of rockwork forming caves and ambush points, plus the hard, alkaline water typical of the lake. A handsome but pugnacious predator for experienced aquarists.
behavior: Aggressive, territorial ambush predator; preys on smaller fish and invertebrates. Best in a species tank or with robust Tanganyikan tankmates. Needs rocky cover for ambush territories.
dimorphism: Males grow larger; sexes are otherwise similar, with reliable sexing usually requiring venting.

origin: Africa (endemic to and widespread in Lake Tanganyika)
description: The largest cichlid in the world, a colossal Tanganyikan predator that can reach nearly a metre in length and far outgrows all but the very largest aquaria — adults truly require pond-sized systems. A powerful open-water piscivore, it is renowned among cichlids for its devoted biparental care, with pairs defending vast territories and guarding enormous broods, often dying after a single monumental spawning. Juveniles are sometimes sold small without warning of their eventual size. A spectacular but utterly space-hungry species strictly for keepers able to provide enormous volumes and pristine, hard alkaline water.
behavior: Highly territorial open-water predator; becomes piscivorous as it grows. Forms strong biparental pairs that fiercely guard huge broods. Far outgrows normal aquaria — needs a pond-sized system as an adult.
dimorphism: Males grow larger (to ~90 cm) than females (to ~70 cm); otherwise similar, with sexing easiest in mature paired fish.

origin: Africa (endemic to the shore regions of Lake Tanganyika)
description: The only killifish endemic to Lake Tanganyika and unusually large and long-lived for the family — a sparkling, elongated fish whose males glitter with rows of iridescent blue-green spangles. A non-annual, open-water shoaling species that swims in loose groups along rocky shores and lays its eggs in rock crevices. Peaceful but skittish and a notorious jumper, so a tightly fitting lid is essential. Requires the hard, alkaline water of its native lake and must be acclimatised very carefully, as it can be delicate. Keep in a group with several females per male; do not house multiple males together.
behavior: Peaceful but skittish open-water shoaler; keep in groups with a female-skewed ratio and only one male. A notorious jumper — needs a tight lid. Delicate; acclimatise very carefully.
dimorphism: Males are larger and brilliantly spangled with iridescent blue-green; females are plainer and silvery.

origin: Africa (Lake Malawi, around Hongi Island and Mphanga Rocks)
description: A popular, brightly coloured Malawi mbuna, the male a vivid electric blue topped with a flash of orange-red along the dorsal fin and forehead. Still formally undescribed, it is universally traded under the name Labidochromis sp. 'Hongi'. More pugnacious than its close relative the yellow lab, males are aggressive toward one another, so keep a single male with a group of females in a tank with abundant rockwork forming caves and broken sightlines. Requires hard, alkaline water and a balanced diet that is not overly protein-rich. A hardy and colourful introduction to the Malawi rock-dwellers.
behavior: Aggressive mbuna; males are territorial toward each other, so keep one male with several females. Needs rockwork with caves and broken sightlines. A maternal mouthbrooder.
dimorphism: Males are vivid blue with a red-orange dorsal and forehead; females are paler blue-grey with less intense colour.

description: A large, blotched Malawi hap and a cunning ambush predator — in the wild it lies motionless on its side feigning death, then strikes at the small fish that come to investigate. Marked with irregular dark patches over a pale body that provide camouflage. The most aggressive member of its genus, it is highly territorial and will eat any tankmate small enough to swallow, so it needs a large tank and robust companions. Requires hard, alkaline water, a sandy substrate it can dig, and ample open swimming space alongside rocky cover. A characterful predator for the experienced Malawi keeper.
behavior: Aggressive ambush predator; eats fish small enough to swallow and is highly territorial. Digs in sand and rearranges decor. Needs a large tank with robust tankmates.
dimorphism: Males develop bright blue-and-yellow breeding colour; females and juveniles retain the blotched brown camouflage pattern.

origin: Africa (endemic to the southern part of Lake Malawi)
description: A handsome and highly prized Malawi hap, the dominant male glowing electric blue with broken dark bars and a sprinkling of golden spots. Like its relatives it is a piscivorous ambush predator that hunts small fish among the rocks and sand of the lake's intermediate zone. Aggressive and strongly territorial, particularly toward other males, so a single male should be kept with females or robust similarly-sized tankmates in a large, well-structured tank. Requires hard, alkaline water, open swimming space, and rocky cover. A spectacular centrepiece predator for the dedicated Malawi aquarist.
behavior: Aggressive, territorial piscivore; males are intolerant of each other. Hunts small fish among rocks and sand. Needs a large tank with robust tankmates and ample structure.
dimorphism: Dominant males are vivid electric blue with dark bars and gold spotting; females and subdominant males are mottled brown.

origin: Africa (endemic to the northern part of Lake Malawi)
description: One of the most stunning Malawi haps, the mature male a breathtaking solid electric blue glittering with raised, star-like white scales, often with white lips. A relatively peaceful, stately species for its size that spends much of its time gliding over and sifting sandy substrate for invertebrates. Best kept in a group of five or more (one male to several females) in a large tank with a deep sand bed and some rocky structure. Avoid housing with tiny fish, which may be eaten. Requires the hard, alkaline water of Lake Malawi. A spectacular yet comparatively gentle centrepiece cichlid.
behavior: Relatively peaceful for a large hap; sifts sand for food and is generally calm with similarly-sized tankmates. Keep in groups of five or more with a female-skewed ratio. May eat very small fish.
dimorphism: Mature males are solid electric blue with star-like white scale spangling and white lips; females are silvery-grey and much plainer.

description: A larger, striking dwarf cichlid from the blackwater Rio Negro, where it lives among leaf litter in soft, acidic, tannin-stained water. Among the more demanding apistos, it is sensitive to abrupt changes in water chemistry and to chemicals, so careful acclimatisation and stable parameters are essential. Best kept as a pair or harem in a dimly lit, well-planted blackwater tank with leaf litter, driftwood, and caves. Males are colourful and may be territorial, especially when breeding. A rewarding species reserved for experienced keepers willing to provide true blackwater conditions.
behavior: Peaceful toward dissimilar fish but territorial, especially when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Sensitive to water-chemistry swings; needs stable, soft, acidic blackwater.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with extended finnage; females are smaller and turn bright yellow with black markings when guarding eggs.

origin: South America (Branco and middle Negro River basins, Brazil)
description: A delicate, finely marked dwarf cichlid from the soft, acidic blackwaters of the Branco and Negro basins, named for the dark lateral stripe running along its body. A peaceful, retiring apisto that does best in a quiet, well-planted tank with subdued lighting, leaf litter, and plenty of caves. Like most blackwater dwarf cichlids it strongly prefers soft, acidic water and live or frozen micro-foods. Forms cave-spawning harems where the female tends the brood. A graceful species for the dwarf-cichlid enthusiast who can provide gentle, acidic conditions.
behavior: Peaceful and somewhat shy; territorial only when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Prefers soft, acidic, dimly lit blackwater with plenty of cover.
dimorphism: Males are larger with longer dorsal and caudal extensions; females are smaller and turn yellow when brooding.

origin: South America (Inírida River basin, Orinoco drainage, Colombia)
description: An elegant dwarf cichlid from the Inírida blackwaters of Colombia, the male sporting elongated, thread-like dorsal-fin rays that give the species its name. A peaceful apisto well suited to a quiet community of small, gentle fish, provided the water is soft and acidic. Prefers a heavily planted tank with floating plants for shade, driftwood, leaf litter, and caves for spawning. Forms harems in which the female defends eggs and fry in a cave while the male guards the wider territory. A beautiful, manageable choice for the dwarf-cichlid keeper.
behavior: Peaceful and suited to gentle community tanks; territorial only when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Needs soft, acidic, well-planted blackwater conditions.
dimorphism: Males are larger with extended thread-like dorsal rays and brighter colour; females are smaller and turn yellow when brooding.

description: A small, relatively shy dwarf cichlid from the Tahuayo basin of Peru, with subtle but attractive markings that intensify in displaying males. One of the more retiring apistos, it does best in a calm, well-planted tank with plenty of cover and soft, acidic water — peat filtration, leaf litter, and alder cones all help recreate its natural conditions. A cave spawner in which the female deposits eggs on a cave ceiling and guards them closely. Best paired with small, peaceful tankmates such as tetras and corydoras. A delicate species for the patient dwarf-cichlid keeper.
behavior: Peaceful and shy; can chase when breeding or guarding fry. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Prefers soft, acidic, heavily planted water with plenty of hiding places.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with extended fins; females are smaller and turn bright yellow when guarding eggs.

origin: South America (lower Negro and Amazon-Solimões rivers, Brazil)
description: A slender, elegant dwarf cichlid widely distributed through the lower Negro and Amazon, tolerant of a broad temperature range but, like its relatives, happiest in soft, acidic water. A peaceful apisto that becomes territorial only at breeding time, making it a good community fish for gentle tankmates. Provide a sandy substrate it can dig, plus driftwood, plants, and caves for spawning. Best kept as a pair or harem with one male and several females. An attractive and comparatively undemanding introduction to the larger blackwater Apistogramma.
behavior: Peaceful; territorial only during breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder best kept as one male with several females. Prefers soft, acidic water and a sandy, well-planted tank.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with extended finnage; females are smaller and turn yellow when brooding.

description: A handsome dwarf cichlid from the Uaupés (Vaupés) blackwaters, named for the dark mask-like markings on the head of displaying males. A peaceful apisto that holds a bottom territory and becomes territorial chiefly when breeding. Prefers soft, acidic, well-planted water with leaf litter, driftwood, and caves for the female to spawn in. Best kept as a pair or harem with one male and several females, alongside small, gentle tankmates. A micropredator that relishes small live and frozen foods. A characterful blackwater species for the dwarf-cichlid enthusiast.
behavior: Peaceful; territorial mainly when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Prefers soft, acidic, well-planted water with caves and leaf litter.
dimorphism: Males are larger with a bold dark facial mask and extended finnage; females are smaller and turn yellow when guarding eggs.

origin: South America (Essequibo, Demerara, and Corantijn drainages, Guianas)
description: One of the larger and hardier dwarf cichlids — a 'giant among dwarfs' — from the coastal river systems of the Guianas. Unusually for the genus it does not require soft, acidic blackwater and is perfectly comfortable in neutral, moderately hard water, making it a forgiving introduction to apistos. Males hold bottom territories and become assertive when spawning, with one male keeping a harem of females that each tend their own caves. Provide a well-planted tank with caves, tunnels, and roots. A robust, rewarding dwarf cichlid for the keeper not ready to chase blackwater parameters.
behavior: Peaceful but holds a bottom territory and turns assertive when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Hardy and tolerant of neutral, moderately hard water.
dimorphism: Males are notably larger with extended fins and brighter colour; females are smaller and turn yellow with black markings when brooding.
reproduction: A harem-polygynous cave spawner: the female lays her eggs in a crevice or cavity and tends the brood, while the male holds a larger territory and courts several females.

origin: South America (Uaupés and nearby Negro River basin, Brazil)
description: A spectacular but demanding dwarf cichlid from the extreme blackwaters of the Uaupés and Negro, the male glowing with red-orange and blue and trailing finnage. It requires genuinely soft, highly acidic, warm, very clean water — peat filtration and blackwater extracts are essential — and proves sensitive and intolerant of harder or alkaline conditions. Semi-aggressive, especially toward similar species, so choose tankmates carefully. Rarely imported and best reserved for experienced apisto keepers who can replicate true Rio Negro conditions. A stunning showpiece for the dedicated blackwater specialist.
behavior: Semi-aggressive, especially toward similar species; territorial when breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder. Requires very soft, highly acidic, warm blackwater and careful acclimatisation.
dimorphism: Males are larger and vividly coloured in red-orange and blue with extended fins; females are smaller and turn yellow when brooding.

origin: South America (Napo, Putumayo, and Caquetá drainages; Peru, Ecuador, Colombia)
description: An attractive dwarf cichlid from the upper Amazon headwaters of Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia, belonging to the regani species group. A peaceful apisto that does well in soft to neutral, slightly acidic water and becomes territorial mainly when breeding. Provide a sandy substrate, plants, driftwood, and caves for spawning, and keep as a pair or harem with one male to several females. A micropredator best fed small live and frozen foods. A manageable, good-looking dwarf cichlid for community tanks of small, peaceful species.
behavior: Peaceful; territorial mainly during breeding. A cave-spawning harem breeder best kept as one male with several females. Prefers soft to neutral, slightly acidic, well-planted water.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with extended fins; females are smaller and turn yellow when guarding eggs.

origin: South America (Ucayali and Amazon rivers; Peru and Colombia)
description: A tiny, apistogramma-like dwarf cichlid in its own monotypic genus, distinguished by having more anal-fin spines than true Apistogramma. One of the smallest cichlids kept in the hobby, with males barely reaching 3 cm. More tolerant of water conditions than most blackwater dwarf cichlids — it does well in soft to neutral water and does not require strongly acidic blackwater. A placid micro-predator that becomes territorial only when spawning, though it can be touchy toward its own kind. Provide many small caves for the female to choose from, plus plants and driftwood. A charming nano cichlid for peaceful community tanks.
behavior: Placid but territorial when spawning and touchy toward conspecifics. A cave-spawning harem breeder. More tolerant of neutral water than most dwarf cichlids; needs plenty of small caves.
dimorphism: Males are larger with more extended finnage; females are tiny and turn yellow when guarding eggs and fry.
